topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Digital Filtering - Review of Z-Transform

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Z-Transform** is a mathematical tool that converts a discrete-time signal defined in the time domain into a complex frequency domain representation, expressed as a function of a complex variable $z$. It serves as the discrete-time counterpart of the **Laplace Transform** for continuous-time signals and is the foundational analytical engine behind all digital filter design and analysis.

For a discrete-time sequence $x[n]$, the **Bilateral (Two-Sided) Z-Transform** is formally defined as:

$$X(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{x[n]\} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\,z^{-n}$$

where $z$ is a complex variable of the form $z = re^{j\Omega}$ and $\Omega$ is the normalized digital frequency in radians.

The **Unilateral (One-Sided) Z-Transform** is restricted to causal sequences and is defined as:

$$X(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{x[n]\} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x[n]\,z^{-n}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The unilateral Z-transform is the default tool used in causal LTI digital filter analysis, where $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$. The bilateral form is essential for stability analysis through the **Region of Convergence (ROC)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the **Z-Transform as a "DNA Sequencer" for signals**:
- A signal $x[n]$ is like a long string of letters (samples).
- The Z-transform acts like a **special high-powered microscope** that doesn't just look at the letters, but classifies them based on how fast they grow or decay, and how they oscillate.
- The complex plane (the $z$-plane) is like a **map**. Every signal has a specific "safe zone" on this map called the **Region of Convergence (ROC)** — outside this zone, the Z-transform simply does not exist (the sum diverges).
- For digital filter design, this map is critical: it tells us whether a filter is **stable** or will blow up like a balloon with too much air.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Physical Meaning | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Z-Plane** | Complex plane where $z = re^{j\Omega}$ | Pole-zero plot visualization |
| **ROC** | Set of $z$ values for which $X(z)$ converges | Determines causality & stability |
| **Unit Circle** | $z = e^{j\Omega}$ (where $r = 1$) | ROC boundary; DTFT evaluation path |
| **Pole** | Value of $z$ where $X(z) = \infty$ | Filter resonance / instability point |
| **Zero** | Value of $z$ where $X(z) = 0$ | Frequency rejection / null point |

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability Criterion (NEP 2020 Outcome Mapped):** A causal LTI digital filter is **BIBO stable** if and only if **all poles lie strictly inside the unit circle** (i.e., $|z| < 1$ for every pole), which is equivalent to the ROC including the unit circle $|z| = 1$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Region of Convergence (ROC) — Properties and Analytical Logic

The ROC is **not** a property of the algebraic expression of $X(z)$ alone — it is a property of the **sequence** $x[n]$ itself. Two entirely different sequences can have the same $X(z)$ expression but different ROCs. The ROC is therefore an inseparable companion of the Z-transform pair.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logical Properties of ROC

- **Step 1 — Boundedness:** The ROC of $X(z)$ is bounded by the poles of $X(z)$ (or extends to infinity if there are no poles).
- **Step 2 — Concentric Rings:** The ROC is a **ring** in the $z$-plane: $r_2 < \vert z \vert < r_1$, where $r_1$ and $r_2$ are convergence radii.
- **Step 3 — Causal Sequence Rule:** For a **right-sided (causal)** sequence $x[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$, the ROC is the **exterior of a circle** $\vert z \vert > r_2$.
- **Step 4 — Anti-Causal Sequence Rule:** For a **left-sided (anti-causal)** sequence $x[n] = 0$ for $n > 0$, the ROC is the **interior of a circle** $\vert z \vert < r_1$.
- **Step 5 — Two-Sided Sequence Rule:** For a **finite-duration / two-sided** sequence, the ROC is the **entire $z$-plane** except possibly $z = 0$ and/or $z = \infty$.
- **Step 6 — Stability Link:** If the ROC **includes the unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$, then $x[n]$ is a **stable** sequence. This is the central diagnostic used in digital filter design.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property / Transform | Mathematical Statement | ROC / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Definition (Bilateral)** | $X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]z^{-n}$ | ROC: $r_2 < \vert z \vert < r_1$ |
| 2 | **Linearity** | $\mathcal{Z}\{a\,x[n] + b\,y[n]\} = aX(z) + bY(z)$ | ROC contains $R_x \cap R_y$ |
| 3 | **Time Shift** | $\mathcal{Z}\{x[n - n_0]\} = z^{-n_0} X(z)$ | ROC unchanged (except $0$ / $\infty$) |
| 4 | **Time Reversal** | $\mathcal{Z}\{x[-n]\} = X(1/z)$ | Inverted ROC: $1/r_1 < \vert z \vert < 1/r_2$ |
| 5 | **Multiplication by $a^n$** | $\mathcal{Z}\{a^n x[n]\} = X(z/a)$ | Scaled ROC: $r_2 \cdot \vert a \vert < \vert z \vert < r_1 \cdot \vert a \vert$ |
| 6 | **Differentiation in $z$** | $\mathcal{Z}\{n\,x[n]\} = -z \dfrac{dX(z)}{dz}$ | ROC unchanged |
| 7 | **Convolution Theorem** | $\mathcal{Z}\{x[n] * h[n]\} = X(z)H(z)$ | ROC contains $R_x \cap R_h$ |
| 8 | **Initial Value Theorem** | $x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$ | Valid for causal $x[n]$ |
| 9 | **Final Value Theorem** | $\lim_{n \to \infty} x[n] = \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1)X(z)$ | Valid when poles of $(z-1)X(z)$ are inside unit circle |
| 10 | **$\delta[n]$** | $1$ | All $z$ (entire plane) |
| 11 | **$u[n]$** | $\dfrac{1}{1 - z^{-1}} = \dfrac{z}{z-1}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| 12 | **$a^n u[n]$** | $\dfrac{1}{1 - a\,z^{-1}} = \dfrac{z}{z-a}$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ |
| 13 | **$n\,a^n u[n]$** | $\dfrac{a\,z^{-1}}{(1 - a\,z^{-1})^2}$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ |
| 14 | **$u[-n-1]$** | $-\dfrac{1}{1 - z^{-1}}$ | $\vert z \vert < 1$ |
| 15 | **$\cos(\Omega_0 n)\,u[n]$** | $\dfrac{1 - z^{-1}\cos\Omega_0}{1 - 2z^{-1}\cos\Omega_0 + z^{-2}}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |
| 16 | **$\sin(\Omega_0 n)\,u[n]$** | $\dfrac{z^{-1}\sin\Omega_0}{1 - 2z^{-1}\cos\Omega_0 + z^{-2}}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Memory Hook:** The Z-transform of $a^n u[n]$ has a single pole at $z = a$ with ROC $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$. **Stability of a causal sequence requires $\vert a \vert < 1$** — a single inequality that decides filter stability.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Z-transform is the **lifeblood of digital signal processing (DSP)** because:

- **Audio Equalizers (e.g., smartphone music apps):** The infinite impulse response (IIR) and finite impulse response (FIR) filter coefficients are designed using pole-zero placement in the $z$-plane.
- **Biomedical Signal Processing (ECG/EEG monitors):** Notch filters at 50/60 Hz to remove powerline interference are designed by placing zeros on the unit circle.
- **Communication Receivers:** Matched filters, pulse-shaping filters (raised cosine), and adaptive filters all use Z-transform transfer functions $H(z)$.
- **Control Systems (Digital):** Digital PID controllers and state-space filters are analyzed through Z-domain transfer functions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Z-Transform of the Causal Exponential Sequence $a^n u[n]$

This is the **most fundamental derivation** in the entire course. Every other Z-transform pair can be derived from it. We will derive it from first principles with no skipped steps.

**Given:** $x[n] = a^n u[n]$ where $u[n]$ is the unit step.

**Step 1 — Write the definition** by substituting $x[n]$ into the bilateral Z-transform:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} a^n u[n]\, z^{-n}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the unit-step constraint** $u[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ and $u[n] = 1$ for $n \geq 0$:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} a^n z^{-n}$$

**Step 3 — Rewrite the sum in standard geometric series form** by introducing the base $\alpha = a z^{-1}$:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (a z^{-1})^n$$

**Step 4 — Use the geometric series identity** $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \alpha^n = \dfrac{1}{1 - \alpha}$, valid for $\vert \alpha \vert < 1$:

$$X(z) = \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$$

**Step 5 — State the convergence condition explicitly** $\vert a z^{-1} \vert < 1 \implies \vert a \vert < \vert z \vert$:

$$\boxed{X(z) = \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}} = \frac{z}{z - a}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert}$$

**Step 6 — Identify the pole location:** the only pole is at $z = a$.

**Step 7 — Conclude the stability condition:** for a causal filter to be stable, we need $\vert a \vert < 1$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Time-Shift Property $\mathcal{Z}\{x[n - n_0]\} = z^{-n_0} X(z)$

**Step 1 — Substitute** the shifted sequence $y[n] = x[n - n_0]$ into the Z-transform definition:

$$Y(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n - n_0]\, z^{-n}$$

**Step 2 — Change the index variable** by letting $m = n - n_0$, so $n = m + n_0$ and $dn = dm$:

$$Y(z) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m]\, z^{-(m + n_0)}$$

**Step 3 — Split the exponential** $z^{-(m + n_0)} = z^{-m} \cdot z^{-n_0}$:

$$Y(z) = z^{-n_0} \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m]\, z^{-m}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize** the sum as $X(z)$:

$$Y(z) = z^{-n_0} X(z)$$

**Step 5 — Conclude the ROC** is unchanged (modulo possible inclusion of $z = 0$ if $n_0 > 0$, or $z = \infty$ if $n_0 < 0$):

$$\boxed{\mathcal{Z}\{x[n - n_0]\} = z^{-n_0} X(z)}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Z-Transform of $n\,a^n u[n]$ Using Differentiation in $z$

This is essential for deriving the impulse response of digital resonators.

**Step 1 — Start from the known result** $X(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$.

**Step 2 — Differentiate both sides with respect to $z$:**

$$\frac{dX(z)}{dz} = \frac{d}{dz}\left( \frac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}} \right) = \frac{-a \cdot (-1) z^{-2}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2} = \frac{a z^{-2}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply both sides by $-z$** to invoke the differentiation property $-z \dfrac{dX(z)}{dz} = \mathcal{Z}\{n\,x[n]\}$:

$$-z \frac{dX(z)}{dz} = -z \cdot \frac{a z^{-2}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2} = \frac{-a z^{-1}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2}$$

**Step 4 — Re-derive carefully** (correcting the sign):

$$-z \cdot \frac{a z^{-2}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2} = \frac{-a z^{-1}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2}$$

This corresponds to $-n\,a^n u[n]$, so the positive form is:

$$\mathcal{Z}\{n\,a^n u[n]\} = \frac{a\,z^{-1}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^2}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Inverse Z-Transform by Partial Fraction Expansion

Given:

$$X(z) = \frac{N(z)}{D(z)} = \frac{z^2 + 1}{(z - 0.5)(z - 0.8)}$$

**Step 1 — Express in $z^{-1}$ form** for standard partial fraction decomposition (since standard tables use $z^{-1}$):

$$X(z) = \frac{1 + z^{-2}}{(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})(1 - 0.8 z^{-1})}$$

**Step 2 — Perform partial fraction expansion:**

$$X(z) = \frac{A}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} + \frac{B}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $A$** by multiplying both sides by $(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})$ and setting $z^{-1} = 2$:

$$A = \left. \frac{1 + z^{-2}}{1 - 0.8 z^{-1}} \right|_{z^{-1} = 2} = \frac{1 + 4}{1 - 1.6} = \frac{5}{-0.6} = -\frac{25}{3}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $B$** by setting $z^{-1} = 1.25$:

$$B = \left. \frac{1 + z^{-2}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} \right|_{z^{-1} = 1.25} = \frac{1 + 1.5625}{1 - 0.625} = \frac{2.5625}{0.375} = \frac{41}{6}$$

**Step 5 — Take the inverse Z-transform term by term** using the table:

$$x[n] = -\frac{25}{3}(0.5)^n u[n] + \frac{41}{6}(0.8)^n u[n], \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 0.8$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Standard Pairs and Their Interpretation

| Sequence $x[n]$ | $X(z)$ | Pole Location | ROC for Causal Version | Filter Type Inferred |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\delta[n]$ | $1$ | No poles | All $z$ | All-pass flat response |
| $u[n]$ | $\dfrac{z}{z-1}$ | $z = 1$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ | DC / integrator |
| $a^n u[n]$ | $\dfrac{z}{z-a}$ | $z = a$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ | First-order low-pass (if $0 < a < 1$) |
| $\cos(\Omega_0 n) u[n]$ | $\dfrac{z^2 - z\cos\Omega_0}{z^2 - 2z\cos\Omega_0 + 1}$ | $e^{\pm j\Omega_0}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ | Digital resonator |
| $\sin(\Omega_0 n) u[n]$ | $\dfrac{z\sin\Omega_0}{z^2 - 2z\cos\Omega_0 + 1}$ | $e^{\pm j\Omega_0}$ | $\vert z \vert > 1$ | Digital oscillator |
| $(n+1) a^n u[n]$ | $\dfrac{z^2}{(z-a)^2}$ | $z = a$ (double) | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ | Second-order section |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Z-Plane Topology — Pole-Zero Diagram for $a^n u[n]$

The following Mermaid diagram maps out the analytical structure of a first-order digital filter's $z$-plane behavior. Since graphical Mermaid rendering of the $z$-plane is non-trivial, we represent the system as a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Sequence x of n] --> B[Bilateral Z Transform Definition]
    B --> C{X converges?}
    C -- No --> D[Diverges - No Z Transform]
    C -- Yes --> E[Identify Poles and Zeros]
    E --> F[Plot on z Plane r e to power j Omega]
    F --> G{Is Causal?}
    G -- Yes --> H[ROC is Exterior of Pole Circle]
    G -- No --> I[ROC is Interior of Pole Circle]
    H --> J{All Poles inside unit circle?}
    I --> J
    J -- Yes --> K[STABLE Filter Design]
    J -- No --> L[UNSTABLE - Reject Design]
    K --> M[Apply Time Shift z power minus n zero]
    K --> N[Apply Convolution X of z times H of z]
    M --> O[Output Y of z]
    N --> O
    O --> P[Inverse Z Transform via PFE]
    P --> Q[Output Sequence y of n]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Input $x[n]$ enters the bilateral Z-transform definition.
- The **convergence test** determines whether the ROC is non-empty.
- **Pole-zero identification** is the gate to stability analysis.
- For digital filter design, the two key operations are **time-shift** (for delay elements) and **convolution** (for cascaded LTI sections).
- The **inverse Z-transform via partial fraction expansion (PFE)** recovers the time-domain filter response $y[n]$.

## 4.2 System Flow — Z-Transform in Digital Filter Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TimeDomain [Time Domain LTI System]
        X1[x of n] --> CONV[Convolution Operator with h of n]
        CONV --> Y1[y of n]
    end
    subgraph ZDomain [Z Domain LTI System]
        X2[X of z] --> PROD[Multiplication H of z]
        PROD --> Y2[Y of z]
    end
    X1 -->|Z Transform| X2
    Y1 -->|Z Transform| Y2
    PROD -->|Inverse Z Transform| Y1
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **left side** shows the time-domain LTI filter: $y[n] = x[n] * h[n]$ requires direct convolution (computationally expensive).
- The **right side** shows the equivalent Z-domain operation: $Y(z) = X(z) \cdot H(z)$, which is just a **multiplication** (vastly cheaper).
- The Z-transform converts a **convolution** (costly, $O(N^2)$) into a **multiplication** (cheap, $O(N)$), which is the central computational reason why digital filters are designed in the Z-domain.

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Stability, Causality, and ROC

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ST[Start: Given X of z and ROC] --> Q1{Is ROC Exterior of Outermost Pole?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> CAU[Causal Sequence]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is ROC Interior of Innermost Pole?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> ANT[Anti Causal Sequence]
    Q2 -- No --> TWO[Two Sided Sequence]
    CAU --> Q3{Does ROC include unit circle?}
    ANT --> Q3
    TWO --> Q3
    Q3 -- Yes --> STABLE[BIBO Stable]
    Q3 -- No --> UNSTABLE[Not BIBO Stable]
```

**Interpretation:**
- **Causal** sequences have ROCs that extend **outward** from the outermost pole.
- **Anti-causal** sequences have ROCs that extend **inward** toward the origin.
- **BIBO stability** is confirmed **only** when the unit circle lies within the ROC.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define the Z-transform of a discrete-time sequence $x[n]$. What is the significance of the Region of Convergence (ROC)?**

**Model Answer:**
The Z-transform of a discrete-time sequence $x[n]$ is defined as:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$$

where $z$ is a complex variable.

**Significance of ROC:**
1. The ROC specifies the set of values of $z$ for which $X(z)$ converges (i.e., the sum yields a finite value).
2. Two different sequences can have the same $X(z)$ expression but different ROCs — so the ROC uniquely identifies the sequence.
3. The ROC determines **causality** (right-sided $\Rightarrow$ exterior ROC) and **stability** (unit circle must be in ROC).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Always state the ROC explicitly when writing any Z-transform pair. Omitting the ROC is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction in KTU valuation.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Series 2]`
**State and explain the time-shifting property of the Z-transform with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**
**Statement:** If $\mathcal{Z}\{x[n]\} = X(z)$ with ROC $R$, then:

$$\mathcal{Z}\{x[n - n_0]\} = z^{-n_0} X(z), \quad \text{ROC: } R \text{ (except possibly } z = 0 \text{ or } z = \infty\text{)}$$

**Explanation:** A shift of $n_0$ samples in the time domain corresponds to multiplication by $z^{-n_0}$ in the Z-domain. A **delay** of one sample multiplies $X(z)$ by $z^{-1}$.

**Example:** $\mathcal{Z}\{\delta[n-1]\} = z^{-1}$, $\mathcal{Z}\{\delta[n-3]\} = z^{-3}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 1 Choice 1]`

**(a)** Determine the Z-transform of the sequence $x[n] = 2^n u[n] + 3 \cdot (-1)^n u[n]$. Specify the ROC and verify the result using the initial value theorem. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Find the inverse Z-transform of:

$$X(z) = \frac{1 + 2z^{-1}}{(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})(1 - 0.75 z^{-1})}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 0.75$$

using partial fraction expansion. State whether the resulting sequence is causal and stable. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Apply linearity of Z-transform:**

$$X(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{2^n u[n]\} + 3 \cdot \mathcal{Z}\{(-1)^n u[n]\}$$

**Step 2 — Use the standard pair** $\mathcal{Z}\{a^n u[n]\} = \dfrac{z}{z-a}$:

$$X(z) = \frac{z}{z-2} + 3 \cdot \frac{z}{z-(-1)} = \frac{z}{z-2} + \frac{3z}{z+1}$$

**Step 3 — Combine into a single rational expression** with common denominator $(z-2)(z+1)$:

$$X(z) = \frac{z(z+1) + 3z(z-2)}{(z-2)(z+1)} = \frac{z^2 + z + 3z^2 - 6z}{(z-2)(z+1)} = \frac{4z^2 - 5z}{(z-2)(z+1)}$$

**Step 4 — Express in $z^{-1}$ form** by dividing numerator and denominator by $z^2$:

$$X(z) = \frac{4 - 5z^{-1}}{1 - z^{-1} - 2z^{-2}}$$

**Step 5 — Determine the ROC** — for a sum of two causal exponentials, the ROC is the exterior of the outermost pole $\max(\vert 2 \vert, \vert -1 \vert) = 2$:

$$\boxed{\text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 2}$$

**Step 6 — Verify using the initial value theorem** $x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$:

$$x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} \frac{4 - 5z^{-1}}{1 - z^{-1} - 2z^{-2}} = \frac{4 - 0}{1 - 0 - 0} = 4$$

**Step 7 — Cross-check by direct evaluation:** $x[0] = 2^0 + 3(-1)^0 = 1 + 3 = 4$ ✓

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Set up partial fraction expansion:**

$$X(z) = \frac{A}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} + \frac{B}{1 - 0.75 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $A$** (cover-up method, set $z^{-1} = 2$):

$$A = \left. \frac{1 + 2z^{-1}}{1 - 0.75 z^{-1}} \right|_{z^{-1} = 2} = \frac{1 + 4}{1 - 1.5} = \frac{5}{-0.5} = -10$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $B$** (set $z^{-1} = 4/3$):

$$B = \left. \frac{1 + 2z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} \right|_{z^{-1} = 4/3} = \frac{1 + 8/3}{1 - 2/3} = \frac{11/3}{1/3} = 11$$

**Step 4 — Take inverse Z-transform** using the standard pair:

$$x[n] = -10 \cdot (0.5)^n u[n] + 11 \cdot (0.75)^n u[n]$$

**Step 5 — Causality check:** Since both terms are multiplied by $u[n]$, $x[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$. **Sequence is causal.**

**Step 6 — Stability check:** Poles are at $z = 0.5$ and $z = 0.75$, both inside the unit circle ($\vert z \vert < 1$). The ROC $\vert z \vert > 0.75$ **includes the unit circle**. **Sequence is BIBO stable.**

$$\boxed{x[n] = \left[ -10 (0.5)^n + 11 (0.75)^n \right] u[n], \quad \text{causal and stable}}$$

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 1 Choice 2]`

**(a)** For the system described by the difference equation:

$$y[n] - 0.9\,y[n-1] + 0.2\,y[n-2] = x[n] + x[n-1]$$

determine: (i) the transfer function $H(z)$, (ii) the impulse response $h[n]$, and (iii) the pole-zero plot description. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss any **four** properties of the Region of Convergence (ROC) with one example for each. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Take the Z-transform of both sides** of the difference equation, applying the time-shift property:

$$Y(z) - 0.9\,z^{-1} Y(z) + 0.2\,z^{-2} Y(z) = X(z) + z^{-1} X(z)$$

**Step 2 — Factor out $Y(z)$ and $X(z)$:**

$$Y(z) \left( 1 - 0.9 z^{-1} + 0.2 z^{-2} \right) = X(z) \left( 1 + z^{-1} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the transfer function** $H(z) = Y(z) / X(z)$:

$$H(z) = \frac{1 + z^{-1}}{1 - 0.9 z^{-1} + 0.2 z^{-2}}$$

**Step 4 — Factor the denominator** by finding the roots of $1 - 0.9 z^{-1} + 0.2 z^{-2} = 0$:

$$z^{-1} = \frac{0.9 \pm \sqrt{0.81 - 0.8}}{0.4} = \frac{0.9 \pm 0.1}{0.4}$$

This gives $z^{-1} = 2.5$ or $z^{-1} = 0.5$, hence poles at $z = 0.4$ and $z = 2$.

Wait, correcting the factorization: rewrite in $z$ form — denominator $z^2 - 0.9 z + 0.2 = (z - 0.4)(z - 0.5)$. Therefore:

$$H(z) = \frac{z^2 + z}{(z - 0.4)(z - 0.5)}$$

**Step 5 — Decompose into partial fractions** (in $z^{-1}$ form):

$$H(z) = \frac{A}{1 - 0.4 z^{-1}} + \frac{B}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $A$** (set $z^{-1} = 2.5$):

$$A = \left. \frac{1 + z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} \right|_{z^{-1} = 2.5} = \frac{1 + 2.5}{1 - 1.25} = \frac{3.5}{-0.25} = -14$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $B$** (set $z^{-1} = 2$):

$$B = \left. \frac{1 + z^{-1}}{1 - 0.4 z^{-1}} \right|_{z^{-1} = 2} = \frac{1 + 2}{1 - 0.8} = \frac{3}{0.2} = 15$$

**Step 8 — Inverse Z-transform term by term** (assuming causal, ROC $\vert z \vert > 0.5$):

$$\boxed{h[n] = \left[ -14 (0.4)^n + 15 (0.5)^n \right] u[n]}$$

**Step 9 — Pole-zero description:**
- **Zeros:** Roots of numerator $z^2 + z = 0 \Rightarrow z = 0$ (double zero) and $z = -1$.
- **Poles:** $z = 0.4$ and $z = 0.5$ (both inside unit circle — **stable system**).
- **ROC:** $\vert z \vert > 0.5$ (includes unit circle — confirms BIBO stability).

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Property 1 — ROC is a ring in the $z$-plane:** The ROC is bounded by poles. Example: For $x[n] = a^n u[n]$, ROC is $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ (exterior of pole at $z = a$).

**Property 2 — ROC does not contain any pole:** $X(z)$ is finite everywhere inside its ROC. Example: For $H(z) = \dfrac{z}{z-0.8}$, the ROC $\vert z \vert > 0.8$ excludes the pole at $z = 0.8$.

**Property 3 — Causal right-sided sequences have ROC as exterior of the outermost pole:** Example: $x[n] = u[n]$ has ROC $\vert z \vert > 1$, which is the exterior of its pole at $z = 1$.

**Property 4 — A finite-duration sequence has ROC equal to the entire $z$-plane (except possibly $z = 0$ and $z = \infty$):** Example: $x[n] = \delta[n] + 2\delta[n-3]$ has $X(z) = 1 + 2z^{-3}$ with ROC being all $z \neq 0$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ROC is a -2 mark killer.** Two sequences can have the same $X(z)$ but different ROCs. Always state the ROC alongside the Z-transform.
> 2. **Confusing causal and stable.** Causal means ROC is *exterior of a circle*. Stable means ROC *includes unit circle*. A causal system is stable **iff** all poles are inside the unit circle.
> 3. **Long division error.** When doing inverse Z-transform via power series, ensure you write coefficients as $x[0], x[1], x[2], \ldots$ in the correct order.
> 4. **Poles on the unit circle** (e.g., $z = 1$ for $u[n]$) indicate **marginal stability** — not BIBO stable, since the impulse response does not decay. This is a common KTU trick question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Review of Z-Transform**

- ✅ **Definition:** $X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]z^{-n}$ (bilateral); the same sum from $n = 0$ (unilateral/causal).
- ✅ **ROC is mandatory** with every Z-transform pair. ROC = $r_2 < \vert z \vert < r_1$.
- ✅ **Causality rule:** Right-sided sequence $\Rightarrow$ ROC is exterior of a circle.
- ✅ **Anti-causality rule:** Left-sided sequence $\Rightarrow$ ROC is interior of a circle.
- ✅ **Stability rule:** BIBO stable $\iff$ unit circle ($\vert z \vert = 1$) is inside ROC.
- ✅ **Key standard pair:** $\mathcal{Z}\{a^n u[n]\} = \dfrac{z}{z-a}$, ROC $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$.
- ✅ **Time shift:** $\mathcal{Z}\{x[n - n_0]\} = z^{-n_0} X(z)$.
- ✅ **Convolution:** $\mathcal{Z}\{x[n] * h[n]\} = X(z) H(z)$ — **the central reason** for using Z-transform in filter design.
- ✅ **Differentiation in $z$:** $\mathcal{Z}\{n x[n]\} = -z \dfrac{dX(z)}{dz}$.
- ✅ **Initial value theorem:** $x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$ (causal sequences only).
- ✅ **Final value theorem:** $\lim_{n \to \infty} x[n] = \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1) X(z)$ (poles of $(z-1)X(z)$ must be inside unit circle).
- ✅ **Inverse Z-transform methods:** (1) Partial Fraction Expansion (PFE), (2) Long Division, (3) Contour Integration (residue method).
- ✅ **Pole location determines filter type:** Pole near unit circle $\Rightarrow$ resonant filter; pole at origin $\Rightarrow$ pure delay.
- ✅ **Zero location determines null frequencies:** Zero on unit circle at angle $\Omega_0 \Rightarrow$ complete rejection at frequency $\Omega_0$.
- ✅ **DTFT connection:** $X(e^{j\Omega}) = X(z) \big\vert_{z = e^{j\Omega}}$ if ROC includes unit circle — i.e., DTFT is the Z-transform evaluated on the unit circle.
- ✅ **Syllabus mapping (CO1):** This topic establishes the analytical foundation for IIR and FIR filter design in subsequent modules.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Z-Domain Transfer Function, Concept of Poles and Zeroes, Location of Poles and Zeroes and Their Effect on Filter Responses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT758 - DIGITAL FILTER DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Z-Domain Transfer Function, Concept of Poles and Zeroes, Location of Poles and Zeroes and Their Effect on Filter Responses # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Z-Domain Transfer Function In the world of digital filters, we cannot use the classical Laplace-domain transfer function **H(s)** because we are dealing with **discrete-time signals** sampled at uniform intervals. The mathematical tool that replaces the Laplace transform for discrete sequences is the **z-transform**, and the corresponding system descriptor is the **Z-Domain Transfer Function**, denoted **H(z)**. ### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus) > **Definition:** The Z-Domain Transfer Function **H(z)** of a Linear Shift-Invariant (LSI) discrete-time system is defined as the ratio of the z-transform of the output sequence **y(n)** to the z-transform of the input sequence **x(n)**, under the assumption of **zero initial conditions**. Mathematically, this is expressed as: $$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} h(n) \, z^{-n}$$ where **h(n)** is the **impulse response** of the discrete system. ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Audio Equalizer" Intuition Think of a **H(z)** like the circuit diagram of a **graphic equalizer** on a music player: - The **numerator coefficients (b_k)** are the **volume sliders for the different input frequencies** — they shape *which frequencies are boosted*. - The **denominator coefficients (a_k)** are the **feedback knobs** — they control *how the system remembers and reacts to its own past output*. - **Poles and Zeros** are the **resonant frequencies and the dead-zones** — the exact spots on the spectrum where the equalizer's response either peaks (resonance) or dies down (null). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A causal, stable, LSI digital filter is *fully and uniquely* described by the locations of its poles and zeros inside the z-plane. Once you know the pole-zero pattern, you know the magnitude response, phase response, stability, and filter type. ## 1.2 The Concept of Poles and Zeroes Given a general rational transfer function: $$H(z) = \frac{b_0 \, z^{N} + b_1 \, z^{N-1} + b_2 \, z^{N-2} + \dots + b_N}{a_0 \, z^{M} + a_1 \, z^{M-1} + a_2 \, z^{M-2} + \dots + a_M}$$ We can factorize this into a **pole-zero form**: $$H(z) = K \cdot \frac{(z - z_1)(z - z_2)\dots(z - z_N)}{(z - p_1)(z - p_2)\dots(z - p_M)}$$ where **K = b_0 / a_0** is the **gain constant**. > [!NOTE] > **Zero (z_i):** A value of **z** for which **H(z) = 0**. These are the roots of the numerator polynomial. At a zero, the filter completely *blocks* the corresponding frequency. > > **Pole (p_i):** A value of **z** for which **H(z) → ∞**. These are the roots of the denominator polynomial. At a pole, the filter produces a *resonant peak* at the corresponding frequency. ### Analogy — Poles and Zeros as "Magnets and Voids" Imagine the z-plane as a flat **rubber sheet**: - A **zero** is a small **dimple (void)** pressed into the sheet. If you roll a marble near it, the marble is *deflected away* — the filter response is small there. - A **pole** is a sharp **upward spike (magnet)** sticking out of the sheet. The marble is *attracted to it* — the filter response becomes very large near a pole. - The closer a pole gets to the **unit circle |z| = 1**, the sharper and taller the resonance becomes. - The closer a zero gets to the unit circle, the deeper the cancellation notch becomes. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Rational Form of the Transfer Function For an **N-th order Linear Constant-Coefficient Difference Equation (LCCDE)**: $$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N} b_k \, x(n-k) - \sum_{k=1}^{M} a_k \, y(n-k)$$ Taking the z-transform on both sides (assuming zero initial conditions): $$Y(z) \left[ 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{M} a_k \, z^{-k} \right] = X(z) \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{N} b_k \, z^{-k} \right]$$ Therefore the transfer function in **negative-power form** is: $$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{N} b_k \, z^{-k}}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{M} a_k \, z^{-k}}$$ Multiplying numerator and denominator by **z^M** gives the **positive-power form** (the "polynomial" form that we factorize): $$H(z) = \frac{b_0 \, z^{M-N} (z - z_1)(z - z_2)\dots(z - z_N)}{(z - p_1)(z - p_2)\dots(z - p_M)}$$ > [!NOTE] > If **M ≥ N**, the system is **causal** (strictly proper). If **N > M**, the system is **non-causal** or has a **pure delay** factor (improper). KTU examiners love testing this distinction. ## 2.2 Stability Criterion — The Unit Circle Rule For a **causal LSI system**, the necessary and sufficient condition for **Bounded-Input Bounded-Output (BIBO) stability** is: > **All poles must lie strictly inside the unit circle: |p_i| < 1, for every pole p_i.** | Condition on Poles | System Behaviour | |---|---| | All poles **inside** unit circle (|p_i| < 1) | **Stable** — impulse response decays to zero | | All poles **on** unit circle (|p_i| = 1) | **Marginally stable** — pure oscillation | | **Any** pole **outside** unit circle (|p_i| > 1) | **Unstable** — impulse response grows without bound | | Poles exactly at origin (p_i = 0) | **Stable**, but trivial (no resonance) | > [!IMPORTANT] > Zeros can be *anywhere* in the z-plane — inside, on, or outside the unit circle — without affecting stability. Zeros only affect the **shape** of the frequency response (magnitude and phase). ## 2.3 Frequency Response from H(z) To convert a z-domain transfer function into a **frequency response**, we evaluate **H(z)** on the unit circle by substituting **z = e^{jω}**: $$H(e^{j\omega}) = H(z) \Big\vert_{z = e^{j\omega}}$$ This gives us: - **Magnitude Response:** $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ — the gain at frequency **ω** - **Phase Response:** $\angle H(e^{j\omega})$ — the phase shift at frequency **ω** ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Z-Domain Transfer Function | $H(z) = Y(z) / X(z)$ | Output/Input ratio | | 2 | Rational (positive-power) form | $H(z) = \frac{\sum b_k z^{N-k}}{\sum a_k z^{M-k}}$ | Standard form for factorization | | 3 | Pole-Zero (Factored) form | $H(z) = K \cdot \frac{\prod (z - z_i)}{\prod (z - p_i)}$ | K is gain | | 4 | Stability (causal system) | $\vert p_i \vert < 1$ for all poles | Inside unit circle | | 5 | Frequency response | $H(e^{j\omega}) = H(z) \big\vert_{z=e^{j\omega}}$ | ω ∈ [-π, π] | | 6 | Magnitude response | $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ | In dB: $20 \log_{10}\vert H \vert$ | | 7 | Phase response | $\angle H(e^{j\omega}) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\text{Im}}{\text{Re}}\right)$ | In radians | | 8 | Real pole near z = +1 | $\Rightarrow$ **Low-pass** filter | Long memory at low freq | | 9 | Real pole near z = -1 | $\Rightarrow$ **High-pass** filter | Alternating response | | 10 | Complex conj. poles near $e^{\pm j\omega_0}$ | $\Rightarrow$ **Band-pass** filter | Resonance at ω_0 | | 11 | Zero at z = +1 (DC) | $\Rightarrow$ Blocks DC | High-pass effect | | 12 | Zero at z = -1 (Nyquist) | $\Rightarrow$ Blocks ω = π | Low-pass effect | | 13 | Order of filter | $\max(M, N)$ | Number of poles/zeroes | | 14 | Order of difference equation | $\max(\text{order of } y, \text{order of } x)$ | Same as #13 | | 15 | Power spectrum of output | $P_y(\omega) = \vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert^{2} P_x(\omega)$ | PSD relation | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The pole-zero analysis is the **backbone of filter design** in: - **Audio Engineering:** Graphic equalizers in music apps place poles/zeros at exact frequencies to boost bass, mid, or treble. - **Telecommunications:** Band-pass filters in radios select a specific carrier frequency by placing complex conjugate poles near the desired ω_0. - **Biomedical Signal Processing:** Notch filters (zeros on unit circle at 50/60 Hz) remove power-line interference from ECG/EEG signals. - **Image Processing:** 2-D extensions of z-transforms (using two variables z_1, z_2) use the same pole-zero philosophy for edge detection and smoothing. - **Control Systems:** Digital controllers (PID) are tuned by relocating poles for desired transient response. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Difference Equation to H(z) **Starting Point — The General LCCDE:** $$y(n) + a_1 y(n-1) + a_2 y(n-2) = b_0 x(n) + b_1 x(n-1) + b_2 x(n-2)$$ **Step 1 — Apply the Z-Transform Term-by-Term.** Using the standard z-transform property — the **time-shift property**: $$\mathcal{Z}\{x(n-k)\} = z^{-k} X(z)$$ We apply this to every term of the difference equation. **Step 2 — Transform the Left-Hand Side (LHS):** $$\mathcal{Z}\{y(n)\} = Y(z)$$ $$\mathcal{Z}\{a_1 y(n-1)\} = a_1 \, z^{-1} Y(z)$$ $$\mathcal{Z}\{a_2 y(n-2)\} = a_2 \, z^{-2} Y(z)$$ So the LHS in z-domain becomes: $$Y(z) \left[ 1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2} \right]$$ **Step 3 — Transform the Right-Hand Side (RHS):** $$\mathcal{Z}\{b_0 x(n)\} = b_0 X(z)$$ $$\mathcal{Z}\{b_1 x(n-1)\} = b_1 z^{-1} X(z)$$ $$\mathcal{Z}\{b_2 x(n-2)\} = b_2 z^{-2} X(z)$$ So the RHS in z-domain becomes: $$X(z) \left[ b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2} \right]$$ **Step 4 — Equate LHS and RHS.** $$Y(z) \left[ 1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2} \right] = X(z) \left[ b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2} \right]$$ **Step 5 — Solve for the Ratio Y(z)/X(z) = H(z).** $$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2}}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2}}$$ **Step 6 — Convert to Positive-Power Form (multiply top and bottom by z²).** $$\begin{aligned} H(z) &= \frac{z^{2} (b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2})}{z^{2} (1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2})} \\ &= \frac{b_0 z^{2} + b_1 z + b_2}{z^{2} + a_1 z + a_2} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 7 — Factorize into Pole-Zero Form.** Assume the denominator factors as **(z - p_1)(z - p_2)** and the numerator as **(z - z_1)(z - z_2)** (scaled by K): $$H(z) = K \cdot \frac{(z - z_1)(z - z_2)}{(z - p_1)(z - p_2)}$$ This is the **canonical pole-zero form** used universally in KTU board examinations. ## 3.2 Derivation: Magnitude Response from a Pole Consider a single real pole at **p** (where **0 < p < 1**). The transfer function is: $$H(z) = \frac{1}{1 - p \, z^{-1}} = \frac{z}{z - p}$$ **Step 1 — Substitute z = e^{jω} to get the frequency response.** $$H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{e^{j\omega}}{e^{j\omega} - p}$$ **Step 2 — Multiply numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate of the denominator.** The conjugate of $(e^{j\omega} - p)$ is $(e^{-j\omega} - p)$ (since p is real): $$H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{e^{j\omega} (e^{-j\omega} - p)}{(e^{j\omega} - p)(e^{-j\omega} - p)}$$ **Step 3 — Expand the denominator.** $$\begin{aligned} (e^{j\omega} - p)(e^{-j\omega} - p) &= e^{j\omega}e^{-j\omega} - p e^{j\omega} - p e^{-j\omega} + p^2 \\ &= 1 - p(e^{j\omega} + e^{-j\omega}) + p^2 \\ &= 1 - 2p \cos(\omega) + p^2 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Expand the numerator.** $$e^{j\omega}(e^{-j\omega} - p) = 1 - p e^{j\omega}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the magnitude.** Since the denominator $(1 - 2p \cos\omega + p^2)$ is purely real: $$\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \frac{\vert 1 - p e^{j\omega} \vert}{\vert 1 - 2p \cos\omega + p^2 \vert}$$ For **|p| < 1**, the denominator is always positive, so: $$\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \frac{\sqrt{1 - 2p\cos\omega + p^2}}{1 - 2p\cos\omega + p^2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - 2p\cos\omega + p^2}}$$ **Step 6 — Analyze the maximum.** The function $\frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - 2p\cos\omega + p^2}}$ is **maximized** when $\cos\omega = 1$, i.e., when **ω = 0**. $$\vert H(e^{j0}) \vert_{\max} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - 2p + p^2}} = \frac{1}{1 - p}$$ **Step 7 — Interpretation.** Since the magnitude is maximum at **ω = 0 (DC)**, this filter behaves as a **Low-Pass Filter**. As **p → 1** (pole moves toward the unit circle), the gain at DC becomes $\frac{1}{1-p} \to \infty$ — a **sharper and sharper** low-pass response. > [!NOTE] > **Generalization:** A real pole at **+p** (positive, near 1) gives a **low-pass** response. A real pole at **-p** (negative, near -1) gives a **high-pass** response. A complex conjugate pair at $p = re^{\pm j\omega_0}$ gives a **band-pass** response centered at $\omega_0$. ## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example **Problem:** Given a transfer function $H(z) = \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}$, find the poles, zeros, plot them, and identify the filter type. **Step 1 — Convert to positive-power form.** $$H(z) = \frac{z - 1}{z - 0.5}$$ **Step 2 — Identify the zero.** Setting numerator = 0: $z - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow \boxed{z_1 = 1}$ **Step 3 — Identify the pole.** Setting denominator = 0: $z - 0.5 = 0 \Rightarrow \boxed{p_1 = 0.5}$ **Step 4 — Check stability.** $|p_1| = 0.5 < 1$ → system is **stable**. **Step 5 — Compute magnitude response.** $$\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \frac{\vert e^{j\omega} - 1 \vert}{\vert e^{j\omega} - 0.5 \vert} = \frac{\sqrt{2 - 2\cos\omega}}{\sqrt{1.25 - \cos\omega}}$$ - At **ω = 0**: $\vert H \vert = 0 / 0.5 = 0$ → blocks DC - At **ω = π**: $\vert H \vert = 2 / 1.5 = 1.33$ → passes high frequency - The zero is at **z = 1** (which corresponds to **ω = 0**, i.e., DC) **Step 6 — Conclusion.** The filter **blocks DC** and **passes high frequencies** → it is a **High-Pass Filter**. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Z-Plane Showing Poles, Zeros, and the Unit Circle ```mermaid graph TD Plane["Z PLANE"] Origin["ORIGIN (0,0)"] UC["UNIT CIRCLE |z| = 1"] Real["REAL AXIS"] Imag["IMAGINARY AXIS"] Pole1["POLE p1 (stable)"] Pole2["POLE p2 (unstable)"] Zero1["ZERO z1 (notch)"] Zero2["ZERO z2 (deep null)"] Conjugate1["COMPLEX CONJ POLE r.e^(+j.w0)"] Conjugate2["COMPLEX CONJ POLE r.e^(-j.w0)"] Plane --- Origin Plane --- Real Plane --- Imag Plane --- UC Plane --- Pole1 Plane --- Pole2 Plane --- Zero1 Plane --- Zero2 Plane --- Conjugate1 Plane --- Conjugate2 ``` > The above schematic maps the conceptual z-plane. In actual KTU board exams, students are expected to **hand-draw the z-plane** with: (a) the unit circle, (b) cross-hairs for real/imaginary axes, (c) "×" marks for poles, and (d) "○" marks for zeros. ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Pole-Zero Filter Analysis ```mermaid flowchart TD A["START: Difference Equation or H(z)"] --> B["Apply Z-Transform / Factorize"] B --> C["Extract POLES from Denominator"] B --> D["Extract ZEROS from Numerator"] C --> E["CHECK STABILITY
All |p_i| less than 1?"] E -->|"YES"| F1["STABLE SYSTEM"] E -->|"NO"| F2["UNSTABLE SYSTEM
Re-design or scale coefficients"] D --> G["PLOT POLES AND ZEROS
in Z-Plane"] G --> H["IDENTIFY FILTER TYPE
from Pole-Zero Location Pattern"] H --> I1["LOW-PASS
Pole near +1, Zero at -1"] H --> I2["HIGH-PASS
Pole near -1, Zero at +1"] H --> I3["BAND-PASS
Complex conj. poles on/near unit circle"] H --> I4["BAND-STOP / NOTCH
Zeros on unit circle, complex conj."] I1 --> J["COMPUTE H(e^jw)
Substitute z = e^jw"] I2 --> J I3 --> J I4 --> J J --> K["PLOT MAGNITUDE and PHASE RESPONSE"] K --> L["END: Filter Specification Verified"] ``` ## 4.3 Hand-Drawn Style — Pole-Zero Patterns for the Four Classical Filter Types | Filter Type | Pole Location | Zero Location | Effect | |---|---|---|---| | **Low-Pass (LPF)** | Real pole near **z = +1** (positive real axis) | Real zero at **z = -1** | Passes ω ≈ 0, blocks ω ≈ π | | **High-Pass (HPF)** | Real pole near **z = -1** (negative real axis) | Real zero at **z = +1** | Blocks DC, passes high freq | | **Band-Pass (BPF)** | Complex conjugate poles at $re^{\pm j\omega_0}$ on/near unit circle | Real zeros at z = +1 and z = -1 | Resonates at ω_0, blocks others | | **Band-Stop / Notch** | Real pole at z = 0 (or simple real pole) | Complex conjugate zeros on unit circle at $e^{\pm j\omega_0}$ | Blocks ω_0, passes others | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Drawing Convention:** Use a "×" for poles and a "○" for zeros. Draw the unit circle as a solid line. If poles/zeros are complex conjugates, always plot them as a **symmetric pair** about the real axis. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **Q:** Define the Z-domain transfer function of a discrete-time system. Why is it important in digital filter design? **Model Answer:** The Z-domain transfer function **H(z)** of a linear shift-invariant discrete-time system is defined as the ratio of the z-transform of the output sequence **y(n)** to the z-transform of the input sequence **x(n)**, assuming zero initial conditions: $$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} h(n) \, z^{-n}$$ where **h(n)** is the impulse response of the system. **Importance in Digital Filter Design:** - It compactly represents the filter's behaviour in a single algebraic expression. - The **poles and zeros** of H(z) directly determine the filter's **frequency response**, **stability**, and **type** (low-pass, high-pass, band-pass, or band-stop). - It allows easy conversion of the difference equation into a realizable digital filter structure. - It provides a clear geometric interpretation through the z-plane (unit circle visualization). - Stability can be checked **visually** — all poles inside the unit circle means the filter is stable. --- ### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Q:** What are poles and zeros of a digital filter? State the stability condition for a causal system in terms of pole locations. **Model Answer:** **Poles:** Poles are the values of **z** for which the magnitude of the transfer function becomes **infinite** (i.e., the denominator polynomial equals zero). Mathematically, poles are the roots of the denominator polynomial of H(z). **Zeros:** Zeros are the values of **z** for which the magnitude of the transfer function becomes **zero** (i.e., the numerator polynomial equals zero). Mathematically, zeros are the roots of the numerator polynomial of H(z). **Stability Condition for a Causal System:** > For a causal Linear Shift-Invariant (LSI) system to be **Bounded-Input Bounded-Output (BIBO) stable**, **all poles must lie strictly inside the unit circle** in the z-plane, i.e., > > $$\vert p_i \vert < 1 \quad \text{for every pole } p_i$$ If any pole lies **on** the unit circle ($|p_i| = 1$), the system is **marginally stable** (produces sustained oscillations). If any pole lies **outside** the unit circle ($|p_i| > 1$), the system is **unstable** (output grows without bound). > [!NOTE] > Zeros have **no restriction** on their location — they can lie inside, on, or outside the unit circle without affecting stability. They only affect the magnitude and phase response shape. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, with sub-parts (a) and (b) carrying 7 marks each. Two full alternative questions are provided below for student practice. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1]** **Q(a) [7 Marks]:** A digital filter is described by the difference equation: $$y(n) = 0.5 \, y(n-1) + x(n) - x(n-1)$$ Find the transfer function **H(z)**, identify its poles and zeros, and determine whether the system is stable. Also, plot the pole-zero pattern. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Apply Z-Transform to both sides.** $$Y(z) = 0.5 \, z^{-1} Y(z) + X(z) - z^{-1} X(z)$$ **Step 2 — Collect Y(z) terms on LHS and X(z) terms on RHS.** $$Y(z) - 0.5 \, z^{-1} Y(z) = X(z) - z^{-1} X(z)$$ $$Y(z) \, [1 - 0.5 \, z^{-1}] = X(z) \, [1 - z^{-1}]$$ **Step 3 — Solve for H(z) = Y(z)/X(z).** $$H(z) = \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5 \, z^{-1}}$$ **Step 4 — Convert to positive-power form (multiply top and bottom by z).** $$H(z) = \frac{z - 1}{z - 0.5}$$ **Step 5 — Identify poles and zeros.** - **Zero:** Setting numerator = 0: $z - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow \boxed{z_1 = 1}$ - **Pole:** Setting denominator = 0: $z - 0.5 = 0 \Rightarrow \boxed{p_1 = 0.5}$ **Step 6 — Check stability.** $|p_1| = |0.5| = 0.5 < 1$ → **Pole is INSIDE the unit circle** → System is **STABLE**. **Step 7 — Pole-Zero Plot.** ``` Imaginary Axis | × | × (none) | (none) | ────────────●──────────── Real Axis O | Zero | z=1| | × | × p=0.5 | (inside) | | Unit Circle (|z|=1) ``` The pole is at **0.5** on the real axis (inside the unit circle), and the zero is at **z = 1** (on the unit circle at the rightmost point). **Q(b) [7 Marks]:** For the same system, derive the magnitude response |H(e^{jω})| and identify the type of filter (LPF, HPF, BPF, or BSF). Show your calculations at ω = 0, ω = π/2, and ω = π. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Substitute z = e^{jω} into H(z).** $$H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{e^{j\omega} - 1}{e^{j\omega} - 0.5}$$ **Step 2 — Derive the magnitude expression.** $$\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \frac{\vert e^{j\omega} - 1 \vert}{\vert e^{j\omega} - 0.5 \vert} = \frac{\sqrt{(\cos\omega - 1)^2 + \sin^2\omega}}{\sqrt{(\cos\omega - 0.5)^2 + \sin^2\omega}}$$ **Step 3 — Simplify the numerator.** $$\vert e^{j\omega} - 1 \vert = \sqrt{\cos^2\omega - 2\cos\omega + 1 + \sin^2\omega} = \sqrt{2 - 2\cos\omega}$$ **Step 4 — Simplify the denominator.** $$\vert e^{j\omega} - 0.5 \vert = \sqrt{\cos^2\omega - \cos\omega + 0.25 + \sin^2\omega} = \sqrt{1.25 - \cos\omega}$$ **Step 5 — Final magnitude expression.** $$\boxed{\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \sqrt{\frac{2 - 2\cos\omega}{1.25 - \cos\omega}}}$$ **Step 6 — Evaluate at specific frequencies.** | Frequency | Calculation | Result | |---|---|---| | **ω = 0** (DC) | $\sqrt{(2-2)/(1.25-1)} = \sqrt{0/0.25} = 0$ | **0** (DC blocked) | | **ω = π/2** | $\sqrt{(2-0)/(1.25-0)} = \sqrt{2/1.25} = \sqrt{1.6}$ | **1.265** | | **ω = π** (Nyquist) | $\sqrt{(2-(-2))/(1.25-(-1))} = \sqrt{4/2.25} = \sqrt{1.778}$ | **1.333** | **Step 7 — Conclusion — Filter Type.** The filter **completely blocks DC (ω = 0)** and **passes high frequencies (ω = π)** with gain ≈ 1.33. This is the characteristic behaviour of a **HIGH-PASS FILTER (HPF)**. > **Geometric Interpretation:** The zero at **z = 1** lies on the unit circle **exactly at ω = 0**, which forces the magnitude to zero at DC. The pole at **z = 0.5** lies inside the unit circle on the real axis, providing gain at higher frequencies. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]** **Q(a) [7 Marks]:** With the help of pole-zero plots, explain how the location of poles and zeros affects the frequency response of a digital filter. Discuss at least four distinct pole-zero configurations. **Model Solution:** The pole-zero plot in the z-plane is a powerful graphical tool that reveals the frequency response of a digital filter at a glance. The key principle is: > **Poles near the unit circle → create resonant peaks (high gain) at the corresponding frequency.** > **Zeros near the unit circle → create notches / nulls (low gain) at the corresponding frequency.** **Configuration 1 — Real Pole near z = +1 (Low-Pass Filter)** When a real pole is located close to **z = +1** (the point corresponding to **ω = 0**, i.e., DC), the magnitude response peaks at DC and rolls off as ω increases. This is the **low-pass filter** characteristic. - Example: pole at p = 0.9 → magnitude at DC = 1/(1-0.9) = 10 - A zero at z = -1 further enhances the roll-off by nulling the response at ω = π. **Configuration 2 — Real Pole near z = -1 (High-Pass Filter)** When a real pole is near **z = -1** (the point corresponding to **ω = π**, i.e., the Nyquist frequency), the magnitude peaks at high frequencies and is small near DC. - The zero at z = +1 forces the DC gain to zero, creating a clean **high-pass** shape. **Configuration 3 — Complex Conjugate Poles near e^{±jω_0} (Band-Pass Filter)** A pair of complex conjugate poles at $p = re^{\pm j\omega_0}$ (with **r close to 1**) creates a **resonant peak** at $\omega_0$. The closer **r → 1**, the **sharper** the peak (higher Q-factor). - This is the basis of the **band-pass filter** — it selects a narrow band of frequencies centered at $\omega_0$. **Configuration 4 — Complex Conjugate Zeros on the Unit Circle (Band-Stop / Notch Filter)** Placing a pair of complex conjugate zeros **on the unit circle** at $z = e^{\pm j\omega_0}$ completely blocks the frequency $\omega_0$ while passing all other frequencies. This is a **notch filter** (band-stop filter). - Real-world example: 50 Hz notch filter to remove power-line interference from biomedical signals. **Configuration 5 — Multiple Poles at the Origin (Broadband Response)** Multiple poles at z = 0 simply contribute a factor of $z^{-k}$ in H(z), which is a **pure delay** with unit magnitude. They don't create any resonance — they just delay the signal. **Summary Table:** | Configuration | Filter Type | |---|---| | Real pole near +1, zero at -1 | Low-Pass | | Real pole near -1, zero at +1 | High-Pass | | Complex conj. poles near $e^{\pm j\omega_0}$ | Band-Pass | | Complex conj. zeros on unit circle | Band-Stop (Notch) | | Poles at origin | All-pass / Pure delay | **Q(b) [7 Marks]:** Consider a digital filter with transfer function: $$H(z) = \frac{(z - 1)(z + 1)}{(z - 0.8 e^{j\pi/3})(z - 0.8 e^{-j\pi/3})}$$ Find the magnitude response at ω = 0, ω = π/3, and ω = π. Identify the filter type. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Identify poles and zeros.** - **Zeros:** $z_1 = 1$ and $z_2 = -1$ (both on the unit circle) - **Poles:** $p_{1,2} = 0.8 e^{\pm j\pi/3}$ (complex conjugates, magnitude 0.8 < 1 → **stable**) **Step 2 — Substitute z = e^{jω} into H(z).** $$H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{(e^{j\omega} - 1)(e^{j\omega} + 1)}{(e^{j\omega} - 0.8 e^{j\pi/3})(e^{j\omega} - 0.8 e^{-j\pi/3})}$$ **Step 3 — Simplify the numerator.** $(e^{j\omega} - 1)(e^{j\omega} + 1) = e^{2j\omega} - 1$ **Step 4 — Magnitude at ω = 0 (DC).** $$H(e^{j0}) = \frac{(1-1)(1+1)}{(1 - 0.8 e^{j\pi/3})(1 - 0.8 e^{-j\pi/3})} = \frac{0 \cdot 2}{|\cdot|} = 0$$ $|H(e^{j0})| = \boxed{0}$ → DC is **blocked** by the zero at z = 1. **Step 5 — Magnitude at ω = π/3 (Resonance).** $$H(e^{j\pi/3}) = \frac{(e^{j\pi/3} - 1)(e^{j\pi/3} + 1)}{(e^{j\pi/3} - 0.8 e^{j\pi/3})(e^{j\pi/3} - 0.8 e^{-j\pi/3})}$$ The denominator's first factor: $e^{j\pi/3} - 0.8 e^{j\pi/3} = 0.2 e^{j\pi/3}$ (very small → large magnitude). $$|H(e^{j\pi/3})| = \frac{|e^{j\pi/3} - 1| \cdot |e^{j\pi/3} + 1|}{|0.2| \cdot |e^{j\pi/3} - 0.8 e^{-j\pi/3}|}$$ - $|e^{j\pi/3} - 1| = \sqrt{(\cos 60° - 1)^2 + \sin^2 60°} = \sqrt{0.25 + 0.75} = 1$ - $|e^{j\pi/3} + 1| = \sqrt{(\cos 60° + 1)^2 + \sin^2 60°} = \sqrt{2.25 + 0.75} = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$ - $|e^{j\pi/3} - 0.8 e^{-j\pi/3}| = |e^{j\pi/3} - 0.8 e^{-j\pi/3}| = \sqrt{(0.5 - 0.4)^2 + (0.866 + 0.693)^2}$ $= \sqrt{0.01 + 2.43} = \sqrt{2.44} \approx 1.562$ $$|H(e^{j\pi/3})| = \frac{1 \times 1.732}{0.2 \times 1.562} = \frac{1.732}{0.3124} \approx \boxed{5.545}$$ → Strong **resonance peak** at ω = π/3 due to pole proximity. **Step 6 — Magnitude at ω = π.** $$H(e^{j\pi}) = \frac{(e^{j\pi} - 1)(e^{j\pi} + 1)}{(e^{j\pi} - 0.8 e^{j\pi/3})(e^{j\pi} - 0.8 e^{-j\pi/3})}$$ - $|e^{j\pi} - 1| = |-1 - 1| = 2$ - $|e^{j\pi} + 1| = |-1 + 1| = 0$ $$|H(e^{j\pi})| = \boxed{0}$$ → Nyquist frequency is **blocked** by the zero at z = -1. **Step 7 — Conclusion — Filter Type.** The filter: - **Blocks DC** (ω = 0) due to zero at z = 1. - **Resonates at ω = π/3** due to complex conjugate poles. - **Blocks Nyquist** (ω = π) due to zero at z = -1. This is a **BAND-PASS FILTER** centered at **ω = π/3**, with the passband bracketed by the two zeros at z = ±1. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes to Avoid:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert H(z) to positive-power form** before factoring — you cannot extract poles/zeros from the negative-power form directly. Always multiply by z^M first. > 2. **Confusing poles and zeros** — poles come from the **denominator**, zeros from the **numerator**. Students often swap them under exam pressure. > 3. **Stating the stability condition incorrectly** — for **causal** systems only, $|p_i| < 1$ guarantees stability. For non-causal systems, the condition is different (mirror-image pole locations). > 4. **Missing the unit circle in the pole-zero plot** — KTU examiners *always* expect the unit circle drawn in the z-plane. Omitting it costs 1–2 marks. > 5. **Not computing magnitudes at key frequencies** — for "identify the filter type" questions, **always** evaluate |H| at ω = 0 and ω = π to confirm the filter behaviour. > 6. **Forgetting complex conjugate pairs** — if coefficients are real, complex poles/zeros **always** appear in conjugate pairs. Forgetting one member of the pair is a common error. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)** - **H(z) Definition:** $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$ — ratio of output z-transform to input z-transform, under zero initial conditions. - **Rational Form:** $H(z) = \frac{\sum b_k z^{-k}}{1 + \sum a_k z^{-k}}$ — starting from any LCCDE. - **Pole-Zero Form:** $H(z) = K \cdot \frac{\prod (z - z_i)}{\prod (z - p_i)}$ — after factorization. - **Poles** = roots of denominator; **Zeros** = roots of numerator. - **Stability Rule (Causal):** All poles must satisfy $|p_i| < 1$ — strictly inside the unit circle. - **Frequency Response:** Substitute $z = e^{j\omega}$ into H(z) to obtain $H(e^{j\omega})$. - **Filter Type Identification:** - **LPF** → pole near z = +1, zero at z = -1 → passes DC, blocks ω = π - **HPF** → pole near z = -1, zero at z = +1 → blocks DC, passes high freq - **BPF** → complex conjugate poles at $re^{\pm j\omega_0}$ near unit circle → resonates at ω_0 - **BSF / Notch** → complex conjugate zeros on unit circle at $e^{\pm j\omega_0}$ → blocks ω_0 - **Pole on Unit Circle** → produces a pure sinusoid (oscillator, marginally stable). - **Pole at Origin** → pure delay, no resonance. - **Pole-Zero Plot Symbols:** "×" for pole, "○" for zero, **always** draw the unit circle. - **Coefficient Symmetry:** Real coefficients ⇒ complex poles/zeros appear as conjugate pairs. - **Order of Filter:** max(M, N) where M is the order of denominator and N is the order of numerator. - **Pure Delay Factor:** $z^{-1}$ in H(z) corresponds to a unit delay — magnitude 1, phase $-\omega$. - **Geometric Distance Interpretation:** $|H(e^{j\omega})| = \frac{\text{Product of distances from zeros to point } e^{j\omega}}{\text{Product of distances from poles to point } e^{j\omega}}$.

topics.title: FIR and IIR Type of Digital Filters, Ideal and Practical Responses of Low Pass, High Pass, Band Pass and Band Reject Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT758 - DIGITAL FILTER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** FIR and IIR Type of Digital Filters, Ideal and Practical Responses of Low Pass, High Pass, Band Pass and Band Reject Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Digital Filter?

A **Digital Filter** is a discrete-time linear time-invariant (LTI) system that performs mathematical operations on a sampled, discrete-time signal to enhance or suppress specific frequency components. It is implemented using digital hardware (FPGA, ASIC) or software (DSP processors) by computing the output as a weighted sum of present and past input samples and past output samples.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A digital filter operates on a discrete-time signal $x(n)$ to produce an output $y(n)$ according to a difference equation of the form:
> $$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x(n-k) - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, y(n-k)$$
> The filter's frequency response $H(e^{j\omega})$ completely characterizes its behavior in the frequency domain.

## 1.2 Two Fundamental Families of Digital Filters

### A. Finite Impulse Response (FIR) Filter
An FIR filter has an impulse response $h(n)$ that is of **finite duration** (i.e., it becomes exactly zero after a finite number of samples $N-1$). It contains no feedback path, meaning the output depends *only* on present and past inputs.

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k) \, x(n-k)$$

### B. Infinite Impulse Response (IIR) Filter
An IIR filter has an impulse response $h(n)$ that theoretically extends to **infinite duration** because it contains feedback (poles). The output depends on both inputs and past outputs.

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x(n-k) - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, y(n-k)$$

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy — The "Audio Equalizer" Perspective

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a sophisticated water filter attached to your kitchen tap. The water (signal) flows in, and the filter removes unwanted particles (noise/high frequencies) while letting clean water pass. A **Low Pass Filter** is like a strainer that lets only slow water droplets through (low frequency). A **High Pass Filter** blocks the slow droplets and lets only fast jets through (high frequency). A **Band Pass Filter** is like a coffee filter that only allows particles in a specific size range. A **Band Reject (Notch) Filter** is the opposite — it blocks a specific band of frequencies (like removing the 50 Hz electrical hum from audio recordings).
>
> - **FIR Filter** = a "feed-forward" sieve — clean water that passes is purely based on what entered; no memory of previously filtered water is retained.
> - **IIR Filter** = a "feedback" sieve — the filter "remembers" some of the water that just passed and uses it to influence future filtering, creating a recursive, longer-lasting effect.

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics Used in KTU Board Exams

| Metric | Symbol | Unit / Typical Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Passband Edge Frequency** | $\omega_p$ | **radians/sample** ($0 < \omega_p < \pi$) |
| **Stopband Edge Frequency** | $\omega_s$ | **radians/sample** ($\omega_s > \omega_p$) |
| **Transition Width** | $\Delta\omega$ | $= \omega_s - \omega_p$ |
| **Passband Ripple** | $\delta_p$ or $A_p$ | **dB** (typically $0.1$ to $3$ dB) |
| **Stopband Attenuation** | $\delta_s$ or $A_s$ | **dB** (typically $40$ to $60$ dB) |
| **Filter Order** | $N$ | Integer (FIR) or $max(M,N)$ (IIR) |
| **Sampling Frequency** | $F_s$ | **Hz** (e.g., $8$ kHz, $44.1$ kHz) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Frequency for Normalization:** All digital frequencies are normalized to the Nyquist range $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$, where $\pi$ corresponds to half the sampling frequency ($F_s/2$).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 FIR Filter — Architecture and Properties

An FIR filter of order $N$ is described by a difference equation with **only zeros** (no poles except at the origin, all located at $z=0$):

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} b_k \, x(n-k) \quad \text{where} \quad b_k = h(k)$$

**Transfer Function:**

$$H(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h(n) \, z^{-n}$$

**Key Properties (Universally True for FIR):**
1. **Always BIBO Stable** — because all poles are at the origin (inside the unit circle).
2. **Inherently Linear Phase** is achievable if $h(n)$ is symmetric: $h(n) = h(N-1-n)$.
3. **No Feedback Path** → no limit cycle oscillations, no quantization feedback noise.
4. **Higher Order Required** than IIR for equivalent magnitude specifications.
5. **Frequency Response** is purely governed by the DFT of impulse response:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h(n) \, e^{-j\omega n} = \vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert \, e^{j\phi(\omega)}$$

## 2.2 IIR Filter — Architecture and Properties

An IIR filter has both poles and zeros. The general recursive difference equation is:

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x(n-k) - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, y(n-k)$$

**Transfer Function:**

$$H(z) = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{-k}}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, z^{-k}} = \frac{B(z)}{A(z)}$$

**Key Properties:**
1. **Stability Conditional** — all poles must lie strictly inside the unit circle ($\vert z_p \vert < 1$).
2. **Non-Linear Phase** in general (except for all-pass structures).
3. **Memory-Efficient** — achieves sharp transitions with low order (e.g., order 4–10).
4. **Susceptible to Limit Cycles** and coefficient quantization sensitivity.

## 2.3 Ideal Frequency Responses (Magnitude Specifications)

The four classical filter types are defined by their **magnitude response** $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert$. An "ideal" filter has a unity passband, zero stopband, and zero transition width.

### 2.3.1 Ideal Low Pass Filter (LPF)
Passes all frequencies below a cutoff $\omega_c$ and rejects all frequencies above.

$$\vert H_{LP}(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \begin{cases} 1, & \vert \omega \vert \le \omega_c \\ 0, & \omega_c < \vert \omega \vert \le \pi \end{cases}$$

### 2.3.2 Ideal High Pass Filter (HPF)
Rejects all frequencies below a cutoff $\omega_c$ and passes all frequencies above.

$$\vert H_{HP}(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \begin{cases} 0, & \vert \omega \vert < \omega_c \\ 1, & \omega_c \le \vert \omega \vert \le \pi \end{cases}$$

### 2.3.3 Ideal Band Pass Filter (BPF)
Passes frequencies in the band $[\omega_1, \omega_2]$ and rejects everything else.

$$\vert H_{BP}(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \begin{cases} 1, & \omega_1 \le \vert \omega \vert \le \omega_2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### 2.3.4 Ideal Band Reject (Band Stop / Notch) Filter (BSF / BSF)
Rejects frequencies in the band $[\omega_1, \omega_2]$ and passes everything else.

$$\vert H_{BS}(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \begin{cases} 0, & \omega_1 \le \vert \omega \vert \le \omega_2 \\ 1, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

## 2.4 Relationship Among the Four Filter Types (Complementarity)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight (Frequently Asked in KTU Board Exams):**
> $$\boxed{\,H_{HP}(e^{j\omega}) = 1 - H_{LP}(e^{j\omega})\,}$$
> $$\boxed{\,H_{BP}(e^{j\omega}) = H_{LP,\omega_2}(e^{j\omega}) - H_{LP,\omega_1}(e^{j\omega})\,}$$
> $$\boxed{\,H_{BS}(e^{j\omega}) = 1 - H_{BP}(e^{j\omega})\,}$$
> This means the HPF is the **complement** of the LPF, the BPF is the **difference** of two LPFs, and the BSF is the **complement** of the BPF.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Notes / Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **FIR Difference Equation** | $y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k) \, x(n-k)$ | Non-recursive |
| **IIR Difference Equation** | $y(n) = \sum b_k x(n-k) - \sum a_k y(n-k)$ | Recursive |
| **FIR Transfer Function** | $H(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h(n) z^{-n}$ | All-zero system |
| **IIR Transfer Function** | $H(z) = B(z)/A(z)$ | Pole-zero system |
| **FIR Stability Condition** | Always stable (poles at origin) | Universally true |
| **IIR Stability Condition** | All poles satisfy $\vert z_p \vert < 1$ | Inside unit circle |
| **Linear Phase Condition (FIR)** | $h(n) = \pm h(N-1-n)$ | Symmetric / Anti-symmetric |
| **Phase Delay (FIR, Symmetric)** | $\alpha = (N-1)/2$ | Constant group delay |
| **Ideal LPF Magnitude** | $1$ for $\vert\omega\vert\le\omega_c$, else $0$ | Brick-wall response |
| **Ideal HPF Magnitude** | $0$ for $\vert\omega\vert<\omega_c$, else $1$ | Complement of LPF |
| **Ideal BPF Magnitude** | $1$ for $\omega_1 \le \vert\omega\vert \le \omega_2$ | Difference of LPFs |
| **Ideal BSF Magnitude** | $0$ for $\omega_1 \le \vert\omega\vert \le \omega_2$ | Complement of BPF |
| **Inverse DTFT (Sinc)** | $h_{LP}(n) = \frac{\omega_c}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\omega_c n}$ | Centered sinc at $n=0$ |
| **Normalised Sinc Form** | $h_{LP}(n) = \frac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\pi n}$ | For $n \neq 0$; equals $\omega_c/\pi$ at $n=0$ |
| **HPF Impulse Response** | $h_{HP}(n) = \delta(n) - h_{LP}(n)$ | Complement principle |
| **BPF Impulse Response** | $h_{BP}(n) = \frac{\sin(\omega_2 n) - \sin(\omega_1 n)}{\pi n}$ | Difference of sinc terms |
| **BSF Impulse Response** | $h_{BS}(n) = \delta(n) - h_{BP}(n)$ | Complement of BPF |
| **Number of Taps (FIR)** | $N = \frac{A - 8}{2.285 \, \Delta\omega} + 1$ | Kaiser window formula |
| **Ripple (dB) Conversion** | $A = -20 \log_{10}(\delta)$ | dB to linear |
| **Gibbs Overshoot** | $\approx 8.95\%$ | Always present for truncated sinc |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Impulse Response of the Ideal Low Pass Filter (LPF)

**Step 1 — Start with the ideal magnitude specification.**
The ideal LPF has frequency response:

$$H_{LP}(e^{j\omega}) = \begin{cases} e^{-j\omega \alpha}, & \vert \omega \vert \le \omega_c \\ 0, & \omega_c < \vert \omega \vert \le \pi \end{cases}$$

The linear phase term $e^{-j\omega \alpha}$ ensures causality after windowing. Here, $\alpha = (N-1)/2$ is the desired constant group delay.

**Step 2 — Compute the impulse response using the Inverse Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (IDTFT).**

$$h_{LP}(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} H_{LP}(e^{j\omega}) \, e^{j\omega n} \, d\omega$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the piecewise definition. The integrand is zero outside the passband, so the integration limits collapse to $[-\omega_c, \omega_c]$:**

$$h_{LP}(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\omega_c}^{\omega_c} e^{-j\omega \alpha} \, e^{j\omega n} \, d\omega = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\omega_c}^{\omega_c} e^{j\omega (n - \alpha)} \, d\omega$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral. Use $\int e^{j\omega (n-\alpha)} d\omega = \frac{e^{j\omega (n-\alpha)}}{j(n-\alpha)}$ for $n \neq \alpha$:**

$$h_{LP}(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ \frac{e^{j\omega (n-\alpha)}}{j(n-\alpha)} \right]_{-\omega_c}^{\omega_c} = \frac{1}{2\pi \, j(n-\alpha)} \left[ e^{j\omega_c (n-\alpha)} - e^{-j\omega_c (n-\alpha)} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Recognize the numerator as $2j \sin(\omega_c (n-\alpha))$ by Euler's identity:**

$$h_{LP}(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi \, j(n-\alpha)} \cdot 2j \sin(\omega_c (n-\alpha)) = \frac{\sin(\omega_c (n-\alpha))}{\pi (n-\alpha)}$$

**Step 6 — Write the final centred-sinc form. Substituting $m = n - \alpha$ for visual clarity:**

$$\boxed{\,h_{LP}(n) = \frac{\sin(\omega_c (n - \alpha))}{\pi (n - \alpha)} = \frac{\omega_c}{\pi} \cdot \text{sinc}\left(\frac{\omega_c (n-\alpha)}{\pi}\right)\,}$$

with the special value $h_{LP}(\alpha) = \dfrac{\omega_c}{\pi}$ obtained by L'Hôpital's rule.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The ideal LPF impulse response is an **infinite-length, symmetric, centred sinc function** that extends from $n = -\infty$ to $n = +\infty$. This immediately tells us two things: (1) the filter is **non-causal** (defined for negative $n$), and (2) it is an **IIR-type ideal response** which must be truncated to a finite length to be implemented as an FIR filter.

## 3.2 Derivation: Impulse Response of the Ideal High Pass Filter (HPF)

**Step 1 — Apply the complementarity principle. The HPF is the all-pass filter minus the LPF:**

$$H_{HP}(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega \alpha} - H_{LP}(e^{j\omega})$$

**Step 2 — Take the inverse DTFT of both sides. The all-pass term corresponds to a shifted unit impulse:**

$$h_{HP}(n) = \delta(n - \alpha) - h_{LP}(n)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the LPF sinc form derived in Section 3.1:**

$$\boxed{\,h_{HP}(n) = \delta(n - \alpha) - \frac{\sin(\omega_c (n - \alpha))}{\pi (n - \alpha)}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special case** $h_{HP}(\alpha) = 1 - \dfrac{\omega_c}{\pi}$. This is the centre tap value that **flips sign** depending on whether $\omega_c < \pi/2$ (positive) or $\omega_c > \pi/2$ (negative).

## 3.3 Derivation: Impulse Response of the Ideal Band Pass Filter (BPF)

**Step 1 — Express BPF as the difference of two LPFs.** A LPF with cutoff $\omega_2$ minus a LPF with cutoff $\omega_1$ yields a BPF centred between $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$:

$$H_{BP}(e^{j\omega}) = H_{LP,\omega_2}(e^{j\omega}) - H_{LP,\omega_1}(e^{j\omega})$$

**Step 2 — Take the inverse DTFT:**

$$h_{BP}(n) = h_{LP,\omega_2}(n) - h_{LP,\omega_1}(n)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the sinc forms:**

$$\boxed{\,h_{BP}(n) = \frac{\sin(\omega_2 (n - \alpha))}{\pi (n - \alpha)} - \frac{\sin(\omega_1 (n - \alpha))}{\pi (n - \alpha)}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This can be viewed as a **sinc-modulated cosine** (using sum-to-product identities):
> $$h_{BP}(n) = \frac{2 \cos(\omega_0 (n-\alpha)) \cdot \sin(\Delta\omega (n-\alpha))}{\pi (n-\alpha)}$$
> where $\omega_0 = (\omega_1 + \omega_2)/2$ is the **centre frequency** and $\Delta\omega = (\omega_2 - \omega_1)/2$ is the **half-bandwidth**.

## 3.4 Derivation: Impulse Response of the Ideal Band Stop (Notch) Filter (BSF)

**Step 1 — Apply the complementarity principle. The BSF is the all-pass minus the BPF:**

$$H_{BS}(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega \alpha} - H_{BP}(e^{j\omega})$$

**Step 2 — Take the inverse DTFT and substitute the BPF impulse response:**

$$\boxed{\,h_{BS}(n) = \delta(n - \alpha) - h_{BP}(n) = \delta(n-\alpha) - \frac{\sin(\omega_2 (n-\alpha)) - \sin(\omega_1 (n-\alpha))}{\pi (n-\alpha)}\,}$$

## 3.5 Practical Realizations of "Ideal" Responses

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Board Exam Insight:** The "ideal" responses derived above are **physically unrealizable** because:
> 1. They are **non-causal** (defined for $n < 0$).
> 2. They have **infinite duration** (IIR-like).
> 3. They have a **discontinuous** frequency response (brick-wall), leading to the **Gibbs phenomenon** (8.95% overshoot/undershoot) when truncated.

### 3.5.1 FIR Practical Realization — Windowing Method

**Step 1:** Truncate the ideal impulse response to length $N$ (centred at $\alpha = (N-1)/2$).
**Step 2:** Multiply by a window function $w(n)$ to taper the ends and reduce Gibbs ripples.

$$h_{\text{FIR}}(n) = h_{\text{ideal}}(n) \cdot w(n), \quad 0 \le n \le N-1$$

| Window Type | Mainlobe Width | Side-lobe Level (dB) | Stopband Attenuation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Rectangular** | $4\pi/N$ | $-13$ dB | $\approx 21$ dB |
| **Hann (Hanning)** | $8\pi/N$ | $-31$ dB | $\approx 44$ dB |
| **Hamming** | $8\pi/N$ | $-41$ dB | $\approx 53$ dB |
| **Blackman** | $12\pi/N$ | $-57$ dB | $\approx 74$ dB |
| **Kaiser ($\beta=6$)** | Variable | $-46$ dB | $\approx 60$ dB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off Principle (Examiners love this!):** Narrower mainlobe ↔ wider transition band. Lower side-lobe level ↔ better stopband attenuation but wider mainlobe.

### 3.5.2 IIR Practical Realization — Bilinear Transformation of Analog Prototypes

The analog Butterworth, Chebyshev-I, Chebyshev-II, and Elliptic filters are transformed into the digital domain using the **Bilinear Transform**:

$$s = \frac{2}{T_s} \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$$

The pre-warping relation to preserve the critical frequency $\Omega_c$ is:

$$\Omega_c = \frac{2}{T_s} \tan\left(\frac{\omega_c}{2}\right)$$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: FIR vs IIR — Comparison Summary

| Parameter | FIR Filter | IIR Filter |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Impulse Response** | Finite ($N$ samples) | Infinite |
| **Poles / Zeros** | All poles at $z=0$ | Poles & zeros both |
| **Stability** | Guaranteed | Conditional ($\vert z_p \vert < 1$) |
| **Phase Response** | Can be exactly linear | Generally non-linear |
| **Order for Sharp Cutoff** | High ($N \approx 20\text{--}100$) | Low ($N \approx 4\text{--}10$) |
| **Computational Cost** | High (multiplications/additions) | Low |
| **Memory** | Stores $N$ past inputs | Stores past inputs and outputs |
| **Limit Cycles** | None | Possible (overflow oscillations) |
| **Quantization Sensitivity** | Low (multiplier-less possible) | High |
| **Design Method** | Windowing, Frequency Sampling, Parks-McClellan | Bilinear transform of analog prototype |
| **Real-time Application** | Less suited for very low-power | Better for power-constrained systems |
| **Audio / Medical Use** | Preferred (linear phase) | Used in equalizers, control systems |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — FIR Filter Architecture (Direct Form)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **Direct Form realization** of an FIR filter of order $N=4$ with coefficients $h(0), h(1), h(2), h(3)$. The structure shows the **tapped delay line** characteristic of FIR filters.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inpA[Input x n] --> dlyA[Delay z inv]
    dlyA --> dlyB[Delay z inv]
    dlyB --> dlyC[Delay z inv]
    dlyC --> dlyD[Delay z inv]
    inpA --> mulA[Multiplier h0]
    dlyA --> mulB[Multiplier h1]
    dlyB --> mulC[Multiplier h2]
    dlyC --> mulD[Multiplier h3]
    mulA --> sumA[Adder]
    mulB --> sumA
    mulC --> sumA
    mulD --> sumA
    sumA --> outA[Output y n]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each $z^{-1}$ block is a unit-sample delay. The signal $x(n)$ enters at the left, is progressively delayed, and at every tap, the delayed sample is multiplied by the corresponding coefficient $h(k)$. All weighted samples are summed at the right to produce $y(n)$.

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — IIR Filter Architecture (Direct Form I)

The IIR filter of order 2 (Biquad section) is shown. Note the **feedback loop** (the distinctive feature of IIR).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inpB[Input x n] --> fbAddA[Adder FB]
    fbAddA --> fbDlyA[Delay z inv]
    fbDlyA --> fbDlyB[Delay z inv]
    fbDlyB --> fbAddA
    fbAddA --> ffMulA[Mul b0]
    fbAddA --> ffMulB[Mul b1]
    fbAddA --> ffMulC[Mul b2]
    fbDlyA --> ffMulB
    fbDlyB --> ffMulC
    ffMulA --> ffAddA[Adder FF]
    ffMulB --> ffAddA
    ffMulC --> ffAddA
    ffAddA --> outB[Output y n]
    ffAddA --> fbDlyC[Delay z inv]
    fbDlyC --> fbDlyD[Delay z inv]
    fbDlyD --> ffAddA
```

## 4.3 Frequency Response Comparison — Ideal vs Practical Filters

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Filter Type Selection] --> q1{Choose by Frequency Band?}
    q1 --> lowcase[Allow LOW Frequencies]
    q1 --> highcase[Allow HIGH Frequencies]
    q1 --> midcase[Allow MIDDLE Frequencies]
    q1 --> stopcase[Block MIDDLE Frequencies]
    lowcase --> lpfBlock[LOW PASS FILTER LPF]
    highcase --> hpfBlock[HIGH PASS FILTER HPF]
    midcase --> bpfBlock[BAND PASS FILTER BPF]
    stopcase --> bsfBlock[BAND STOP FILTER BSF]
    lpfBlock --> lpfResp[Magnitude equals 1 for omega less than omega_c, else 0]
    hpfBlock --> hpfResp[Magnitude equals 0 for omega less than omega_c, else 1]
    bpfBlock --> bpfResp[Magnitude equals 1 between omega_1 and omega_2, else 0]
    bsfBlock --> bsfResp[Magnitude equals 0 between omega_1 and omega_2, else 1]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: General IIR Filter Design Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    specA[Filter Specifications ap, as, wp, ws] --> analogA[Choose Analog Prototype]
    analogA --> butterA[Butterworth Max Flat]
    analogA --> cheb1A[Chebyshev I Equiripple Pass]
    analogA --> cheb2A[Chebyshev II Equiripple Stop]
    analogA --> ellipA[Elliptic Equiripple Both]
    butterA --> bilinA[Bilinear Transform with Pre warping]
    cheb1A --> bilinA
    cheb2A --> bilinA
    ellipA --> bilinA
    bilinA --> digitalA[Digital Filter H z]
    digitalA --> verifyA[Verify Stability, Phase, Group Delay]
```

## 4.5 Typical Impulse Response Shapes (Qualitative Sketch via Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ir1A[Ideal LPF Impulse Response] --> ir1B[Centred sinc, infinite duration, non causal]
    ir2A[Ideal HPF Impulse Response] --> ir2B[Delta at centre minus sinc, alternating]
    ir3A[Ideal BPF Impulse Response] --> ir3B[Cosine modulated sinc, oscillatory decay]
    ir4A[Ideal BSF Impulse Response] --> ir4B[Delta at centre minus BPF shape, oscillatory]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility of this Architecture Knowledge:** When you face a KTU board question asking to *design* a filter, the first decision is **FIR vs IIR**:
> - Pick **FIR** for audio, biomedical, data transmission (linear phase is mandatory).
> - Pick **IIR** for real-time control, equalizers, or when order constraints are tight.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between FIR and IIR filters. List any two advantages of FIR filters over IIR filters.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | FIR Filter | IIR Filter |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Impulse response | Finite duration | Infinite duration |
| Feedback | Absent (non-recursive) | Present (recursive) |
| Stability | Always stable | Conditionally stable |
| Phase | Can be exactly linear | Generally non-linear |
| Computational cost | Higher order → more computations | Lower order → fewer computations |

**Two advantages of FIR filters:**
1. **Guaranteed stability** — all poles are at the origin, so they are always inside the unit circle.
2. **Linear phase response** — symmetric impulse response $h(n) = h(N-1-n)$ yields constant group delay, preventing phase distortion of wideband signals (critical for audio and data transmission).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Sketch the magnitude response of an ideal Low Pass Filter, High Pass Filter, Band Pass Filter, and Band Reject Filter. State two reasons why ideal filters are not realizable in practice.**

**Model Answer:**

**Sketches (Conceptual — drawn on answer sheet):**

- **LPF:** Rectangular block of height 1 from $-\omega_c$ to $+\omega_c$, zero outside.
- **HPF:** Rectangular block of height 1 from $\omega_c$ to $\pi$ and $-\omega_c$ to $-\pi$, zero in middle.
- **BPF:** Rectangular block of height 1 between $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$ (and symmetrically on negative side).
- **BSF:** Height 1 everywhere except a "hole" between $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$.

**Two reasons ideal filters are unrealizable:**
1. **Non-causality** — The ideal impulse response $h(n) = \dfrac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\pi n}$ is non-zero for negative $n$, so the filter responds *before* the input is applied.
