2. **Infinite duration** — The ideal impulse response extends from $n = -\infty$ to $n = +\infty$, requiring infinite memory and infinite computational delay.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the special value at $n = \alpha$:** Many students write $h(n) = \dfrac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\pi n}$ blindly and get a $0/0$ form at $n=0$. Always state $h(0) = \omega_c/\pi$ separately (L'Hôpital's rule).
> 2. **Confusing $h(n)$ for $H(e^{j\omega})$:** The impulse response is a function of $n$ (time), not $\omega$ (frequency). Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for symbol confusion.
> 3. **Skipping linearity-of-phase justification:** When designing FIR filters, simply multiplying by $e^{-j\omega \alpha}$ in the formula is not enough — state explicitly that it ensures *constant group delay*.
> 4. **Ignoring normalization:** All frequencies must be expressed in the range $[0, \pi]$ (normalized) or $[0, F_s/2]$ (Hz). Wrong units = full mark deduction.

---

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024] — *(14 Marks)*

**(a)** Derive the impulse response of an **ideal Low Pass Filter** with cutoff frequency $\omega_c$ and linear phase delay $\alpha$. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the practical realization of an FIR low pass filter using the **window method**. Compare the rectangular, Hamming, and Blackman windows in terms of mainlobe width and stopband attenuation. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1: Definition of ideal LPF in frequency domain.**

The magnitude is unity in the passband and zero in the stopband, with linear phase $e^{-j\omega \alpha}$:

$$H_{LP}(e^{j\omega}) = \begin{cases} e^{-j\omega \alpha}, & -\omega_c \le \omega \le \omega_c \\ 0, & \omega_c < \vert \omega \vert \le \pi \end{cases}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Inverse Discrete-Time Fourier Transform.**

$$h_{LP}(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} H_{LP}(e^{j\omega}) \, e^{j\omega n} \, d\omega$$

**Step 3: Substitute piecewise. Outside the passband, integrand is zero, so integrate only over $[-\omega_c, \omega_c]$:**

$$h_{LP}(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\omega_c}^{\omega_c} e^{-j\omega \alpha} \, e^{j\omega n} \, d\omega = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\omega_c}^{\omega_c} e^{j\omega (n-\alpha)} \, d\omega$$

**Step 4: Integrate:**

$$h_{LP}(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ \frac{e^{j\omega (n-\alpha)}}{j(n-\alpha)} \right]_{-\omega_c}^{\omega_c} = \frac{1}{2\pi j(n-\alpha)} \left[ e^{j\omega_c (n-\alpha)} - e^{-j\omega_c (n-\alpha)} \right]$$

**Step 5: Apply Euler's identity** $e^{j\theta} - e^{-j\theta} = 2j\sin(\theta)$:

$$h_{LP}(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi j(n-\alpha)} \cdot 2j \sin(\omega_c (n-\alpha)) = \frac{\sin(\omega_c (n-\alpha))}{\pi (n-\alpha)}$$

**Step 6: For $n = \alpha$** (the indeterminate form $0/0$), use L'Hôpital's rule:

$$h_{LP}(\alpha) = \lim_{n \to \alpha} \frac{\sin(\omega_c (n-\alpha))}{\pi (n-\alpha)} = \frac{\omega_c}{\pi}$$

**Final boxed answer:**

$$\boxed{\,h_{LP}(n) = \frac{\sin(\omega_c (n - \alpha))}{\pi (n - \alpha)} \quad \text{for } n \neq \alpha; \quad h_{LP}(\alpha) = \frac{\omega_c}{\pi}\,}$$

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Practical FIR Realization via Window Method:**

The ideal impulse response $h_{LP}(n)$ is **infinite** and **non-causal**. To make it realizable:

**Step 1: Truncate.** Restrict $h_{LP}(n)$ to the range $0 \le n \le N-1$ (causal). This is equivalent to multiplying by a **rectangular window** of length $N$.

**Step 2: Shift.** The truncated response is shifted right by $\alpha = (N-1)/2$ samples to make it causal:

$$h_{FIR}(n) = h_{LP}(n - \alpha) \cdot w(n), \quad n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$

**Step 3: Apply window** $w(n)$ to taper the discontinuities at the edges and reduce **Gibbs phenomenon** (the 8.95% ripple overshoot in the magnitude response).

**Window Comparison Table:**

| Window | Mainlobe Width | Stopband Attenuation | Transition Width |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Rectangular $w(n) = 1$ | $4\pi/N$ | $\approx 21$ dB | Smallest |
| Hamming $0.54 - 0.46\cos(2\pi n/N-1)$ | $8\pi/N$ | $\approx 53$ dB | Moderate |
| Blackman $0.42 - 0.5\cos(\cdot) + 0.08\cos(2\cdot)$ | $12\pi/N$ | $\approx 74$ dB | Largest |

**Trade-off observation:** As stopband attenuation improves (rectangular → Blackman), mainlobe widens and transition band becomes less sharp. Designers choose the window based on the minimum stopband attenuation specification $A_s$.

---

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam - May 2023] — *(14 Marks)* *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Starting from the impulse response of an ideal Low Pass Filter, derive the impulse responses of an ideal **High Pass Filter** and an ideal **Band Pass Filter**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the help of neat magnitude response plots, explain the four basic types of digital filters (LPF, HPF, BPF, BSF). State the complementarity relationship among them. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1: Recall the ideal LPF impulse response (derived in Question A):**

$$h_{LP}(n) = \frac{\sin(\omega_c (n - \alpha))}{\pi (n - \alpha)}$$

**Step 2: Derive the HPF impulse response using the complementarity principle.**

An all-pass filter (magnitude 1 for all $\omega$) shifted by $\alpha$ samples is $H_{AP}(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega \alpha}$. Its impulse response is $\delta(n - \alpha)$.

The HPF is the **difference** of all-pass and LPF:

$$H_{HP}(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega \alpha} - H_{LP}(e^{j\omega})$$

Taking the inverse DTFT:

$$h_{HP}(n) = \delta(n - \alpha) - h_{LP}(n)$$

$$\boxed{\,h_{HP}(n) = \delta(n - \alpha) - \frac{\sin(\omega_c (n - \alpha))}{\pi (n - \alpha)}\,}$$

**Special value** at $n = \alpha$: $\quad h_{HP}(\alpha) = 1 - \dfrac{\omega_c}{\pi}$.

**Step 3: Derive the BPF impulse response using two LPFs.**

A BPF with passband $[\omega_1, \omega_2]$ is the difference of an LPF with cutoff $\omega_2$ and an LPF with cutoff $\omega_1$:

$$H_{BP}(e^{j\omega}) = H_{LP,\omega_2}(e^{j\omega}) - H_{LP,\omega_1}(e^{j\omega})$$

Taking the inverse DTFT:

$$h_{BP}(n) = h_{LP,\omega_2}(n) - h_{LP,\omega_1}(n)$$

$$\boxed{\,h_{BP}(n) = \frac{\sin(\omega_2 (n - \alpha))}{\pi (n - \alpha)} - \frac{\sin(\omega_1 (n - \alpha))}{\pi (n - \alpha)}\,}$$

**Alternative cosine-modulated form (using sum-to-product identity):**

$$h_{BP}(n) = \frac{2 \cos\left(\frac{\omega_1 + \omega_2}{2} (n-\alpha)\right) \cdot \sin\left(\frac{\omega_2 - \omega_1}{2} (n-\alpha)\right)}{\pi (n-\alpha)}$$

This reveals the **cosine carrier** at the centre frequency $\omega_0 = (\omega_1 + \omega_2)/2$, modulated by a **sinc envelope** of half-bandwidth $\Delta\omega = (\omega_2 - \omega_1)/2$.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Magnitude Response Plots — Conceptual Description (Draw on answer sheet):**

- **LPF:** Magnitude = 1 for $0 \le \vert \omega \vert \le \omega_c$, and 0 for $\omega_c < \vert\omega\vert \le \pi$. Rectangular block centred at origin.
- **HPF:** Magnitude = 0 for $0 \le \vert \omega \vert < \omega_c$, and 1 for $\omega_c \le \vert\omega\vert \le \pi$. Inverse image of LPF — everything passed except the low band.
- **BPF:** Magnitude = 1 for $\omega_1 \le \vert \omega \vert \le \omega_2$ (and symmetric on negative axis), zero elsewhere. A "donut" or "ring" in the 2D frequency plane.
- **BSF (Notch):** Magnitude = 0 for $\omega_1 \le \vert \omega \vert \le \omega_2$, equals 1 elsewhere. A "hole" in the spectrum.

**Complementarity Relationships:**

$$H_{HP}(e^{j\omega}) + H_{LP}(e^{j\omega}) = 1 \quad \text{(All-Pass)}$$

$$H_{BP}(e^{j\omega}) = H_{LP,\omega_2}(e^{j\omega}) - H_{LP,\omega_1}(e^{j\omega})$$

$$H_{BS}(e^{j\omega}) + H_{BP}(e^{j\omega}) = 1 \quad \text{(All-Pass)}$$

These relationships are useful because once an LPF is designed, the HPF, BPF, and BSF can be obtained via simple spectral transformations (e.g., $z \to -z$ inversion for LPF-to-HPF conversion).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Digital Filter** = discrete-time LTI system that shapes signal spectrum by difference equation.
- [x] **FIR** = finite impulse response, all-zero (poles at origin), always stable, can have linear phase.
- [x] **IIR** = infinite impulse response, recursive (poles and zeros), conditionally stable, non-linear phase.
- [x] **FIR Difference Eqn:** $y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k) x(n-k)$ (no feedback).
- [x] **IIR Difference Eqn:** $y(n) = \sum b_k x(n-k) - \sum a_k y(n-k)$ (with feedback).
- [x] **Ideal LPF:** $\vert H(e^{j\omega})\vert = 1$ for $\vert\omega\vert \le \omega_c$, else 0.
- [x] **Ideal HPF:** $\vert H(e^{j\omega})\vert = 0$ for $\vert\omega\vert < \omega_c$, else 1.
- [x] **Ideal BPF:** $\vert H(e^{j\omega})\vert = 1$ for $\omega_1 \le \vert\omega\vert \le \omega_2$, else 0.
- [x] **Ideal BSF:** $\vert H(e^{j\omega})\vert = 0$ for $\omega_1 \le \vert\omega\vert \le \omega_2$, else 1.
- [x] **LPF Impulse Response:** $h_{LP}(n) = \dfrac{\sin(\omega_c(n-\alpha))}{\pi(n-\alpha)}$, with $h_{LP}(\alpha) = \omega_c/\pi$.
- [x] **HPF Impulse Response:** $h_{HP}(n) = \delta(n-\alpha) - h_{LP}(n)$.
- [x] **BPF Impulse Response:** $h_{BP}(n) = \dfrac{\sin(\omega_2(n-\alpha)) - \sin(\omega_1(n-\alpha))}{\pi(n-\alpha)}$.
- [x] **BSF Impulse Response:** $h_{BS}(n) = \delta(n-\alpha) - h_{BP}(n)$.
- [x] **All ideal responses are non-causal and infinite-duration** — must be windowed and shifted to be realizable.
- [x] **Gibbs phenomenon:** Truncation causes 8.95% overshoot/undershoot in magnitude response.
- [x] **FIR design method (Module 1 focus):** Windowing — multiply ideal $h(n)$ by $w(n)$.
- [x] **Rectangular window** = sharpest transition, worst stopband (21 dB).
- [x] **Blackman window** = widest transition, best stopband (74 dB).
- [x] **Linear phase FIR** requires $h(n) = \pm h(N-1-n)$ (symmetry/anti-symmetry).
- [x] **Group delay** for symmetric FIR = $\alpha = (N-1)/2$ samples (constant).
- [x] **Phase = 0 or $\pi$ for LPF/HPF**; phase has constant slope for BPF/BSF if linear-phase symmetric.
- [x] **All frequencies must be normalized** to $\omega \in [0, \pi]$ (or equivalently to $[0, F_s/2]$ in Hz).
- [x] **Choose FIR** when linear phase is mandatory (audio, biomedical, data links).
- [x] **Choose IIR** when order is strictly limited and phase is not critical (control systems, equalizers).
- [x] **Bilinear transform** with **pre-warping** is the standard IIR design technique from analog prototypes.
- [x] **Stability of IIR** verified by checking all $\vert z_p \vert < 1$ (inside unit circle).
- [x] **Limitation of IIR:** Susceptible to limit cycles and coefficient quantization errors.
- [x] **Biquad section:** Standard building block for IIR implementation (order-2 sub-filter).
- [x] **Direct Form I** = canonical; **Direct Form II** = fewer delay elements (canonical form).
- [x] **Magnitude in dB:** $A(\omega) = 20 \log_{10} \vert H(e^{j\omega})\vert$.
- [x] **Phase in radians:** $\phi(\omega) = \arg\{H(e^{j\omega})\}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analog Filter Responses - Butterworth, Chebyshev and Elliptic Responses, Applications and Advantages of Digital Filtering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT758 - DIGITAL FILTER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Analog Filter Responses - Butterworth, Chebyshev and Elliptic Responses, Applications and Advantages of Digital Filtering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 1: Analog Filter Responses & Digital Filtering

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Analog Filter Approximation – The Core Idea

An **analog filter approximation** is a *closed-form mathematical prototype* (a transfer function $H_a(s)$) that satisfies an ideal magnitude specification with the least possible order $N$. Because an ideal brick-wall filter is *non-causal* and *physically unrealizable*, we use well-known polynomial or rational approximations (Butterworth, Chebyshev, Elliptic, Bessel) to come as close as possible to the ideal while remaining realizable with passive/active components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 – Syllabus Definition**
> An analog filter is a signal processing network that operates on continuous-time signals. Its magnitude response $\vert H_a(j\Omega)\vert$ is shaped to pass a desired band of frequencies and attenuate others. The *approximation problem* is to find $\vert H_a(j\Omega)\vert^2$ meeting:
> 1. Passband edge $\Omega_p$ with maximum ripple $A_p$ (dB)
> 2. Stopband edge $\Omega_s$ with minimum attenuation $A_s$ (dB)
> 3. Lowest possible filter order $N$

### 1.2 The Four Classical Approximations – Quick Intuition

| Filter Type | Passband | Stopband | Real-World Analogy |
|-------------|----------|----------|--------------------|
| **Butterworth** | Maximally flat (no ripples) | Monotonic fall | A *perfectly smooth* hill slope — pleasant but slow descent. |
| **Chebyshev Type-I** | Equiripple (ripples allowed) | Monotonic fall | A *roller-coaster* passband, then a smooth dive. |
| **Chebyshev Type-II (Inverse)** | Flat | Equiripple | A *flat road* followed by a wavy guardrail in the stopband. |
| **Elliptic (Cauer)** | Equiripple | Equiripple | A *roller-coaster everywhere* — sharpest transition, but worst phase. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition – "Equiripple vs Monotonic"**
> Equiripple means the response oscillates with equal *maximum* ripple in the band, like a checked flag pattern. Monotonic means it never goes up — only down. A filter with ripples can *tolerate more deviation* and therefore achieves a *steeper transition* for the same order $N$.

### 1.3 Digital Filtering – What and Why

A **digital filter** is a discrete-time system that processes a sampled input $x(n)$ to produce an output $y(n)$ by a difference equation:

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\,x(n-k) - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k\,y(n-k)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition – Digital Filter**
> A digital filter is a numerical algorithm (or hardware realization) that performs mathematical operations on a discrete, sampled signal to reduce or enhance specific frequency components. It is described by the transfer function $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine cleaning a polluted river.
- *Analog filtering* = a physical mesh that traps dirt as water flows through pipes.
- *Digital filtering* = a software recipe that *mathematically subtracts* the dirt pattern from a recorded sample of the water — more flexible, repeatable, and even free if you have a microprocessor.

### 1.4 Why Start with Analog Filters? (The Bridge to Digital)

> [!NOTE]
> Most practical IIR digital filters are designed by first designing an analog prototype $H_a(s)$ and then applying a *transformation* (Bilinear or Impulse Invariance) to obtain $H(z)$. Hence, mastering analog approximations is a **mandatory prerequisite** to digital IIR design — exactly why KTU 2024 places it in Module 1.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 General Magnitude-Squared Specification

Every analog low-pass filter specification is described by three corner parameters:

- $\Omega_p$ → Passband edge frequency (rad/s)
- $\Omega_s$ → Stopband edge frequency (rad/s), with $\Omega_s > \Omega_p$
- $A_p$ → Maximum passband ripple (dB)
- $A_s$ → Minimum stopband attenuation (dB)

The passband tolerance and stopband tolerance are derived as:

$$\varepsilon = \sqrt{10^{A_p/10} - 1} \quad \text{(passband ripple factor)}$$

$$A = 10^{A_s/20} \quad \text{(stopband attenuation factor, used as }\lambda = \sqrt{10^{A_s/10}-1}\text{)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key concept:** A smaller $\varepsilon$ → tighter passband tolerance; a larger $A$ → tighter stopband requirement. Both demand a *higher filter order $N$*.

## 2.2 Butterworth Response – Maximally Flat

The magnitude-squared response is:

$$\vert H_a(j\Omega)\vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{\Omega}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N}}$$

where $\Omega_c$ is the **$-3\text{ dB}$ cutoff frequency** (where magnitude drops to $1/\sqrt{2}$).

**Properties:**
- The first $2N-1$ derivatives of $\vert H_a(j\Omega)\vert^2$ are zero at $\Omega = 0$ and $\Omega = \infty$ → maximally flat at both DC and infinity.
- All-pole filter (no finite zeros).
- $20N$ dB/decade roll-off in the stopband.
- Moderate transition width; excellent phase linearity for low $N$.

## 2.3 Chebyshev Type-I Response – Equiripple Passband

$$ \vert H_a(j\Omega)\vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + \varepsilon^2 \, T_N^2\!\left(\frac{\Omega}{\Omega_p}\right)} $$

where $T_N(x) = \cos\!\big(N\cos^{-1}(x)\big)$ is the Chebyshev polynomial of the first kind of degree $N$.

**Properties:**
- Equiripple in passband: oscillates between $1$ and $1/\sqrt{1+\varepsilon^2}$.
- Monotonic in stopband: rolls off at $20N$ dB/dec.
- All-pole structure.
- For the same $N$ and $\Omega_s/\Omega_p$, gives **steeper** roll-off than Butterworth.

## 2.4 Chebyshev Type-II (Inverse) Response – Equiripple Stopband

$$ \vert H_a(j\Omega)\vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + \varepsilon^2 \left[\dfrac{T_N\!\left(\Omega_r/\Omega\right)}{T_N\!\left(\Omega_r/\Omega_p\right)}\right]^{-2}} $$

Equivalently, the poles of Type-II are the *reciprocal* of Type-I poles w.r.t. $\Omega_r$ (the stopband edge), and finite zeros lie on the $j\Omega$ axis at $\Omega = \Omega_r / \cos\!\left(\frac{(2k-1)\pi}{2N}\right)$.

**Properties:**
- Flat passband (no ripple).
- Equiripple stopband.
- Contains finite zeros → more complex structure.

## 2.5 Elliptic (Cauer) Response – Equiripple in Both Bands

$$ \vert H_a(j\Omega)\vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + \varepsilon^2 \, U_N^2\!\left(\frac{\Omega}{\Omega_p}\right)} $$

where $U_N(\cdot)$ is the **Jacobian elliptic function** of order $N$.

**Properties:**
- Equiripple in *both* passband and stopband.
- **Sharpest** transition for a given $N$ and ripple.
- Contains both poles and finite zeros (rational function).
- **Worst** phase response and most non-linear group delay.

## 2.6 Filter Order Formulas (Minimum $N$ Required)

Let $\Omega_{sp} = \Omega_s / \Omega_p$ (selectivity ratio), and define the discrimination factor:

$$k_1 = \frac{\varepsilon}{\sqrt{10^{A_s/10} - 1}} = \frac{\sqrt{10^{A_p/10}-1}}{\sqrt{10^{A_s/10}-1}}$$

| Filter Type | Minimum Order Formula |
|-------------|----------------------|
| **Butterworth** | $N \geq \dfrac{\log_{10}\!\left(\sqrt{10^{A_s/10}-1}\,\big/\,\sqrt{10^{A_p/10}-1}\right)}{\log_{10}\!\left(\Omega_s / \Omega_p\right)} = \dfrac{\log_{10}(1/k_1)}{\log_{10}\Omega_{sp}}$ |
| **Chebyshev (I or II)** | $N \geq \dfrac{\cosh^{-1}\!\left(1/k_1\right)}{\cosh^{-1}\!\left(\Omega_s/\Omega_p\right)} = \dfrac{\cosh^{-1}\!\left(1/k_1\right)}{\cosh^{-1}\!\left(\Omega_{sp}\right)}$ |
| **Elliptic** | $N \geq \dfrac{\log_{10}(16/\varepsilon^2)}{\log_{10}(1/q)}$ where $q$ is elliptic modular function of $\Omega_{sp}$ (use standard elliptic tables) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always **round up** the calculated $N$ to the next integer — partial fractions are non-realizable!

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition |
|---|---------|--------------------|
| 1 | Butterworth magnitude | $\vert H_a(j\Omega)\vert^2 = \dfrac{1}{1 + (\Omega/\Omega_c)^{2N}}$ |
| 2 | Chebyshev-I magnitude | $\vert H_a(j\Omega)\vert^2 = \dfrac{1}{1 + \varepsilon^2 T_N^2(\Omega/\Omega_p)}$ |
| 3 | Chebyshev-II magnitude | $\vert H_a(j\Omega)\vert^2 = \dfrac{1}{1 + \varepsilon^2 [T_N(\Omega_r/\Omega)]^{-2}}$ |
| 4 | Elliptic magnitude | $\vert H_a(j\Omega)\vert^2 = \dfrac{1}{1 + \varepsilon^2 U_N^2(\Omega/\Omega_p)}$ |
| 5 | Passband ripple factor | $\varepsilon = \sqrt{10^{A_p/10} - 1}$ |
| 6 | Discrimination factor | $k_1 = \sqrt{10^{A_p/10}-1}\,\big/\,\sqrt{10^{A_s/10}-1}$ |
| 7 | Butterworth order | $N \geq \log_{10}(1/k_1) / \log_{10}(\Omega_s/\Omega_p)$ |
| 8 | Chebyshev order | $N \geq \cosh^{-1}(1/k_1) / \cosh^{-1}(\Omega_s/\Omega_p)$ |
| 9 | Butterworth poles | $s_k = \Omega_c \exp\!\big(j(\pi/2 + (2k+1)\pi/(2N))\big),\ k=0,1,\dots,N-1$ (LHP only) |
| 10 | Chebyshev-I poles | $s_k = \Omega_p\big(\sin\theta_k \sinh\alpha + j\cos\theta_k \cosh\alpha\big)$ where $\alpha = (1/N)\sinh^{-1}(1/\varepsilon)$, $\theta_k = (2k+1)\pi/(2N)$ |
| 11 | Digital filter difference eq. | $y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x(n-k) - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y(n-k)$ |
| 12 | Digital filter TF | $H(z) = \dfrac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}$ |
| 13 | Frequency response of digital filter | $H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} h(n) e^{-j\omega n}$ |
| 14 | Phase linearity (linear phase FIR) | $h(n) = \pm h(M-n)$ ⇒ constant group delay $= M/2$ |

## 2.8 Applications of Digital Filtering (Real-World Utility)

- **Telecommunications:** Channel equalization, echo cancellation, noise suppression in 4G/5G baseband.
- **Audio & Music:** MP3/AAC codecs, equalizers (graphic and parametric), hearing aids.
- **Biomedical:** ECG/EEG denoising (50/60 Hz notch filters), pacemaker signal processing.
- **Radar/Sonar:** Matched filtering, pulse compression, Doppler tracking.
- **Image Processing:** 2-D filtering for edge detection, blurring, sharpening.
- **Control Systems:** Anti-aliasing, digital PID, sensor fusion pre-processing.
- **Speech Processing:** Formant tracking, vocoders, voice-activated assistants.
- **Industrial:** Vibration analysis in predictive maintenance, power-line harmonic filtering.

> [!NOTE]
> In every one of these areas, the digital filter is *designed* by first mapping the spec to an analog prototype (Butterworth/Chebyshev/Elliptic) and then converting it to the $z$-domain — which is why this module is foundational.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Butterworth Pole Locations

**Given:** Magnitude-squared specification

$$ \vert H_a(j\Omega)\vert^2 = H_a(s) H_a(-s) \big\vert_{s=j\Omega} = \frac{1}{1 + (-1)^N (s/\Omega_c)^{2N}} $$

Setting the denominator to zero gives the $2N$ pole locations of $H_a(s)H_a(-s)$:

$$1 + (-1)^N \left(\frac{s}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N} = 0 \implies \left(\frac{s}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N} = (-1)^{N+1}$$

Taking the $2N$-th root:

$$\frac{s}{\Omega_c} = e^{j\pi(2k+N+1)/(2N)} = e^{j[\pi/2 + (2k+1)\pi/(2N)]}, \quad k = 0,1,\dots,2N-1$$

Therefore, the $2N$ poles lie uniformly on a circle of radius $\Omega_c$ in the $s$-plane. To make the system **stable and causal**, we retain only the **Left-Half-Plane (LHP)** poles, i.e., those with negative real part:

$$s_k = \Omega_c \exp\!\left(j\left[\frac{\pi}{2} + \frac{(2k+1)\pi}{2N}\right]\right), \quad k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$

**Example for $N=3$, $\Omega_c=1$ rad/s:**

$$ s_0 = e^{j\pi/3} = 0.5 + j0.866 $$
$$ s_1 = e^{j\pi} = -1.0 + j0 $$
$$ s_2 = e^{j5\pi/3} = 0.5 - j0.866 $$

Keeping only LHP poles ($k=1$ already in LHP; $k=0$ has $+0.5$ real part — but we use the symmetric $k=N-1=2$ which is in the LHP, and $k=N=3$ equivalent to $k=0$ reflected). The standard practice is to pick $k=0,1,\dots,N-1$ and reflect: only those with $\text{Re}(s_k) < 0$ are kept. For $N=3$ we get:

$$s_1 = -1.0, \quad s_2 = -0.5 - j0.866 \quad (\text{conjugate pair formed with mirror } s_0)$$

> [!NOTE]
> This explains why Butterworth poles are *uniformly spaced* on a circle — the magnitude response has rotational symmetry of order $2N$ before LHP truncation.

## 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Order of a Butterworth Filter

**Given specification:** $\Omega_p$, $\Omega_s$, $A_p$, $A_s$.

At the passband edge $\Omega = \Omega_p$:

$$ \frac{1}{1 + (\Omega_p/\Omega_c)^{2N}} = 10^{-A_p/10} \implies (\Omega_p/\Omega_c)^{2N} = 10^{A_p/10} - 1 $$

At the stopband edge $\Omega = \Omega_s$:

$$ \frac{1}{1 + (\Omega_s/\Omega_c)^{2N}} = 10^{-A_s/10} \implies (\Omega_s/\Omega_c)^{2N} = 10^{A_s/10} - 1 $$

**Dividing** the second by the first to eliminate $\Omega_c$:

$$ \left(\frac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_p}\right)^{2N} = \frac{10^{A_s/10} - 1}{10^{A_p/10} - 1} $$

Taking $\log_{10}$ on both sides and solving for $N$:

$$ 2N \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_p}\right) = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10^{A_s/10} - 1}{10^{A_p/10} - 1}\right) $$

$$ \boxed{\; N \;\geq\; \frac{\log_{10}\!\left(\sqrt{10^{A_s/10} - 1}\,\big/\,\sqrt{10^{A_p/10} - 1}\right)}{\log_{10}\!\left(\Omega_s/\Omega_p\right)} \;} $$

## 3.3 Worked Example – KTU Exam Style

**Problem:** Design a Butterworth low-pass filter with:
- $f_p = 1.5$ kHz $\Rightarrow \Omega_p = 2\pi(1500) = 9424.78$ rad/s
- $f_s = 3.0$ kHz $\Rightarrow \Omega_s = 2\pi(3000) = 18849.56$ rad/s
- $A_p = 1$ dB, $A_s = 30$ dB

**Step 1:** Compute the ripple and discrimination factors.

$$ \varepsilon = \sqrt{10^{1/10} - 1} = \sqrt{1.2589 - 1} = \sqrt{0.2589} = 0.5088 $$

$$ \sqrt{10^{A_s/10} - 1} = \sqrt{10^{3} - 1} = \sqrt{999} = 31.606 $$

$$ k_1 = \frac{0.5088}{31.606} = 0.01610 $$

**Step 2:** Compute the minimum order.

$$ N \geq \frac{\log_{10}(1/0.01610)}{\log_{10}(18849.56/9424.78)} = \frac{\log_{10}(62.11)}{\log_{10}(2.0)} = \frac{1.793}{0.3010} = 5.96 $$

**Step 3:** Round up.

$$ \boxed{\; N = 6 \;} $$

**Step 4:** Choose $\Omega_c$ such that passband spec is exactly met (or stopband spec, depending on design choice).

$$ \Omega_c = \Omega_p \,(10^{A_p/10} - 1)^{-1/(2N)} = 9424.78 \times (0.2589)^{-1/12} $$

$$ (0.2589)^{-1/12} = e^{-(\ln 0.2589)/12} = e^{1.351/12} = e^{0.1126} = 1.119 $$

$$ \Omega_c = 9424.78 \times 1.119 = 10{,}545 \text{ rad/s} \;(\approx 1.68 \text{ kHz}) $$

**Step 5:** Place $2N = 12$ poles uniformly on the circle of radius $\Omega_c$ and retain LHP poles. Form the transfer function as a product of second-order sections.

$$ H_a(s) = \frac{\Omega_c^N}{\prod_{k=0}^{N-1}(s - s_k)}, \quad s_k \in \text{LHP} $$

This is the final analog prototype ready for *bilinear transformation* to obtain $H(z)$.

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix – Butterworth vs Chebyshev vs Elliptic

| Parameter | Butterworth | Chebyshev-I | Chebyshev-II | Elliptic |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|--------------|----------|
| Passband ripple | None (maximally flat) | Equiripple | None (flat) | Equiripple |
| Stopband ripple | None (monotonic) | Monotonic | Equiripple | Equiripple |
| Transition width (for same $N$) | Widest | Moderate | Moderate | **Narrowest** |
| Phase linearity | **Best** | Poor | Poor | **Worst** |
| Group delay distortion | Lowest | Higher | Higher | Highest |
| Poles only? | Yes | Yes | No (has zeros) | No (has zeros) |
| Order needed (for tight spec) | Highest | Lower | Lower | **Lowest** |
| Typical applications | Audio, instrumentation | Sharp LP/HP filters | Filters needing flat passband | Channel-select, anti-alias |

> [!TIP]
> **Selection Heuristic for KTU Problems**
> 1. Need *flat magnitude + linear phase* → **Butterworth**.
> 2. Need *steep transition + tolerate passband ripple* → **Chebyshev-I**.
> 3. Need *flat passband + sharp stopband* → **Chebyshev-II**.
> 4. Need *minimum order* (e.g., VLSI / hardware-constrained) → **Elliptic**.

## 3.5 From Analog Prototype to Digital Filter (Bridge Concept)

The two standard conversions are:

### (a) Impulse Invariance Method
$$ H(z) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{\text{Res}\{H_a(s), s_k\}}{1 - e^{s_k T} z^{-1}} $$

Suitable for band-limited $h_a(t)$; causes aliasing in higher frequencies → avoid for high-pass filters.

### (b) Bilinear Transformation (Preferred)
$$ s = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}} $$

Maps the entire LHP of the $s$-plane to the inside of the unit circle in the $z$-plane — **always stable**.

**Pre-warping** is required to preserve the critical frequencies $\Omega_p$ and $\Omega_s$:

$$ \Omega' = \frac{2}{T} \tan\!\left(\frac{\Omega T}{2}\right) $$

> [!NOTE]
> This connection is *exactly* why KTU 2024 places analog filter design as Module 1 — it is the foundation for *all* IIR digital filter design tasks in later modules.

## 3.6 Advantages of Digital Filtering over Analog

1. **Programmability & Reconfigurability** – Same hardware (DSP/FPGA) realizes any filter by simply updating coefficients.
2. **Precision & Repeatability** – Coefficients stored in digital memory; no component drift, aging, or temperature sensitivity.
3. **Reliable Performance** – No matching of resistors/capacitors required; LSI fabrication ensures uniformity.
4. **Linear Phase Capability** – FIR filters can have *exactly* linear phase — virtually impossible in analog domain.
5. **Multi-rate & Adaptive Capability** – Easy decimation, interpolation, sample-rate conversion; adaptive algorithms (LMS, RLS) possible.
6. **Storage & Reproducibility** – Filter response can be stored, shared, version-controlled, and reproduced bit-exactly.
7. **Easy to Cascade** – Sections can be cascaded in software without impedance matching or loading effects.
8. **Low-Cost Mass Production** – One DSP chip replaces hundreds of analog components.

> [!WARNING]
> **Limitation** – Digital filters require A/D and D/A converters, are bandwidth-limited by sampling rate, and have finite word-length effects (quantization, overflow, limit cycles). Analog filters excel at very high frequencies (RF/microwave) where sampling is infeasible.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram – Analog Filter Design Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Filter Spec] --> B[Given Op Op_p Op_s Ap As]
    B --> C[Choose Approximation: Butterworth / Chebyshev / Elliptic]
    C --> D[Compute Minimum Order N]
    D --> E[Round Up N to Next Integer]
    E --> F[Compute Cutoff Op_c or Op_p]
    F --> G[Find 2N Poles of Ha s]
    G --> H[Retain LHP Poles Only]
    H --> I[Form Ha s as Product of Quadratic Sections]
    I --> J[Check Gain at Op_dc equals 1]
    J --> K[Analog Prototype Ready Ha s]
    K --> L[Convert to Digital H z via Bilinear or Impulse Invariance]
    L --> M[Realize H z in Direct Form I, II, Cascade or Parallel]
    M --> N[Implement on DSP / FPGA / Microcontroller]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The first half (A → K) is the *analog design* covered in this module. The last two stages (L → N) are the *digital conversion & implementation* that follows in subsequent modules.

## 4.2 Block Diagram – Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Digital Filter System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputChain[Input Signal Chain]
        IN1[Analog Source xa t] --> A1[Anti Alias LPF]
        A1 --> A2[Sampler A to D]
        A2 --> A3[Quantizer]
    end
    A3 --> IN2[Digital Samples x n]
    IN2 --> DSP[Digital Filter H z on DSP]
    DSP --> OUT1[Filtered Output y n]
    OUT1 --> OUT2[D to A Converter]
    OUT2 --> OUT3[Reconstruction LPF]
    OUT3 --> OUT4[Analog Output ya t]
    style DSP fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:2px
    style A1 fill:#FFF0E6,stroke:#CC6600
    style A2 fill:#FFF0E6,stroke:#CC6600
    style A3 fill:#FFF0E6,stroke:#CC6600
    style OUT2 fill:#E6FFE6,stroke:#009900
    style OUT3 fill:#E6FFE6,stroke:#009900
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **blue-highlighted block** is where $H(z)$ — designed from the analog prototype — actually operates. The orange blocks (left) are required to *bring the signal into the digital domain*, and the green blocks (right) reconstruct the analog output. Without the *anti-alias LPF* (orange, leftmost), high-frequency content would fold into the baseband and corrupt the filtered output.

## 4.3 Comparison Flowchart – Choosing the Right Filter Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Define Application] --> Q1{Need Linear Phase?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> BUT[Choose Butterworth for low N, or FIR]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Minimum Order Critical?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Passband Ripple Acceptable?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> CHE1[Chebyshev Type I]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{Stopband Ripple Acceptable?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> CHE2[Chebyshev Type II]
    Q4 -- No --> ELL[Elliptic Cauer]
    Q2 -- No --> Q5{Passband Flatness Required?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> CHE2
    Q5 -- No --> BUT2[Butterworth]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam shortcut:** If a problem says "lowest order for given spec" → use Elliptic. If it says "best phase" → Butterworth. If "ripple only in passband" → Chebyshev-I. If "ripple only in stopband" → Chebyshev-II.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List the magnitude-squared response characteristics of Butterworth, Chebyshev Type-I, and Elliptic filters. Why is Butterworth called "maximally flat"?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Filter | Passband | Stopband | Roll-off |
|--------|----------|----------|----------|
| Butterworth | Maximally flat (no ripple) | Monotonic | $20N$ dB/dec |
| Chebyshev-I | Equiripple of amplitude $1/\sqrt{1+\varepsilon^2}$ | Monotonic | $>20N$ dB/dec |
| Elliptic | Equiripple | Equiripple | Steepest for given $N$ |

Butterworth is called **maximally flat** because its magnitude-squared response $1/[1+(\Omega/\Omega_c)^{2N}]$ has its first $2N-1$ derivatives equal to zero at $\Omega=0$ and $\Omega=\infty$. Hence no other all-pole filter of the same order can have a *flatter* response near DC. **(1 mark for table, 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the derivative justification.)**

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State any three advantages of digital filters over analog filters.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Programmability and Reconfigurability** – A single DSP chip realizes multiple filter responses just by changing coefficients. (1 mark)
2. **Precision and Repeatability** – Coefficients are stored in digital memory; no component tolerance, drift, or temperature effects. (1 mark)
3. **Linear Phase Achievable** – FIR digital filters can be designed with *exactly* linear phase (constant group delay), impossible in analog domain. (1 mark)

*(Other valid answers: easy cascading, low-cost VLSI realization, adaptive capability, easy storage/sharing of filter designs.)*

---

## Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]**
*(a)* Derive the magnitude-squared response of a Butterworth low-pass filter and explain why it is called "maximally flat". Show that for $N=1$, the filter reduces to a simple RC low-pass structure. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* For the specifications: passband edge $f_p = 2$ kHz, stopband edge $f_s = 4$ kHz, $A_p = 2$ dB, $A_s = 25$ dB, determine the order of a Butterworth filter. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

Step 1: Define the magnitude-squared response. (1 mark)

$$ \vert H_a(j\Omega)\vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{\Omega}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N}} $$

Step 2: Expand around $\Omega = 0$ using binomial series. (2 marks)

$$ \vert H_a(j\Omega)\vert^2 = 1 - \left(\frac{\Omega}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N} + \left(\frac{\Omega}{\Omega_c}\right)^{4N} - \cdots $$

Step 3: The first $2N-1$ derivatives of the function w.r.t. $\Omega$ are zero at $\Omega = 0$ and $\Omega = \infty$. This makes it **maximally flat** at both ends — no other all-pole filter of order $N$ is flatter at $\Omega = 0$. (2 marks)

Step 4: For $N = 1$, the response simplifies to: (1 mark)

$$ \vert H_a(j\Omega)\vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + (\Omega/\Omega_c)^2} \;\Longrightarrow\; H_a(s) = \frac{\Omega_c}{s + \Omega_c} $$

This is the standard *first-order RC low-pass transfer function* with $\Omega_c = 1/RC$. (1 mark)

**Model Solution (b):**

Step 1: Convert frequencies to rad/s. (1 mark)

$$ \Omega_p = 2\pi(2000) = 12{,}566.37 \text{ rad/s}, \quad \Omega_s = 2\pi(4000) = 25{,}132.74 \text{ rad/s} $$

Step 2: Compute the passband and stopband ripple factors. (2 marks)

$$ \sqrt{10^{A_p/10} - 1} = \sqrt{10^{0.2} - 1} = \sqrt{0.5849} = 0.7648 $$

$$ \sqrt{10^{A_s/10} - 1} = \sqrt{10^{2.5} - 1} = \sqrt{315.23} = 17.755 $$

Step 3: Compute the minimum order using the Butterworth formula. (3 marks)

$$ N \geq \frac{\log_{10}(17.755 / 0.7648)}{\log_{10}(25{,}132.74 / 12{,}566.37)} = \frac{\log_{10}(23.21)}{\log_{10}(2.0)} = \frac{1.3656}{0.3010} = 4.54 $$

Step 4: Round up to the next integer. (1 mark)

$$ \boxed{\; N = 5 \;} $$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative to Question A*
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper]**
*(a)* Compare Butterworth, Chebyshev Type-I, Chebyshev Type-II, and Elliptic filters in terms of (i) passband behaviour, (ii) stopband behaviour, (iii) order required, and (iv) phase linearity. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Derive the order formula for a Chebyshev Type-I low-pass filter given $A_p$, $A_s$, $\Omega_p$, and $\Omega_s$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

A 4-row comparison table covering all four filters — see Section 3.4. (5 marks for the table, 1 mark for key insight, 1 mark for application mapping.)

**Key insights to include:**
- Butterworth = maximally flat, *worst transition* for given $N$, *best phase*.
- Chebyshev-I = equiripple passband, *better transition* than Butterworth.
- Chebyshev-II = flat passband, equiripple stopband (zeros in stopband).
- Elliptic = equiripple in both, *minimum order* for tightest spec, *worst phase*.

**Model Solution (b):**

Step 1: Start from the Chebyshev-I magnitude-squared response. (1 mark)

$$ \vert H_a(j\Omega)\vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + \varepsilon^2 T_N^2(\Omega/\Omega_p)} $$

Step 2: At the passband edge, $T_N(1) = 1$ ⇒ $\vert H_a(j\Omega_p)\vert^2 = 1/(1+\varepsilon^2)$. Equating to $10^{-A_p/10}$ gives the ripple parameter: (2 marks)

$$ \varepsilon^2 = 10^{A_p/10} - 1 \;\Longrightarrow\; \varepsilon = \sqrt{10^{A_p/10}-1} $$

Step 3: At the stopband edge, $\vert H_a(j\Omega_s)\vert^2 = 1/[1+\varepsilon^2 T_N^2(\Omega_s/\Omega_p)] = 10^{-A_s/10}$. Rearranging: (2 marks)

$$ T_N\!\left(\frac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_p}\right) = \frac{1}{\varepsilon}\sqrt{10^{A_s/10}-1} \equiv D $$

Step 4: Use the identity for $T_N(x)$ when $x > 1$: $T_N(x) = \cosh(N\cosh^{-1}(x))$. Substitute and take $\cosh^{-1}$ on both sides: (1 mark)

$$ N \cosh^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_p}\right) = \cosh^{-1}(D) $$

Step 5: Solve for $N$. (1 mark)

$$ \boxed{\; N \;\geq\; \frac{\cosh^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{10^{A_s/10}-1}}{\sqrt{10^{A_p/10}-1}}\right)}{\cosh^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_p}\right)} \;} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to round up $N$** — a fractional order is unrealizable; always report $\lceil N \rceil$. *(Lose 1 mark)*
> 2. **Confusing $\cosh^{-1}$ with $\log_{10}$** — Chebyshev uses inverse hyperbolic cosine, *not* common log. Butterworth uses $\log_{10}$. *(Lose 2 marks)*
> 3. **Mixing Type-I and Type-II** — Type-I is equiripple *passband*; Type-II is equiripple *stopband*. The order formula is the *same* in form, but the ripple factor $\varepsilon$ is associated with the rippled band. *(Lose 1 mark)*
> 4. **Not converting $f_p, f_s$ to $\Omega_p, \Omega_s$** — the formulas are in rad/s, not Hz. *(Lose 1 mark)*
> 5. **Skipping the analog-to-digital bridge** — for IIR digital filter design questions, marks are reserved for showing the bilinear/impulse-invariance step. *(Lose 2–3 marks)*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Analog filter approximation** is the first step in IIR digital filter design; it produces $H_a(s)$ which is then transformed to $H(z)$.
- **Butterworth** → maximally flat magnitude, monotonic in both bands, $20N$ dB/dec roll-off, best phase, but widest transition.
- **Chebyshev Type-I** → equiripple passband, monotonic stopband, sharper than Butterworth, all-pole.
- **Chebyshev Type-II (Inverse)** → flat passband, equiripple stopband, contains zeros on the $j\Omega$ axis.
- **Elliptic (Cauer)** → equiripple in *both* bands, *minimum order* for tight specs, *worst* phase, contains poles and zeros.
- **Passband ripple factor:** $\varepsilon = \sqrt{10^{A_p/10}-1}$.
- **Butterworth order:** $N \geq \log_{10}(1/k_1) / \log_{10}(\Omega_s/\Omega_p)$.
- **Chebyshev order:** $N \geq \cosh^{-1}(1/k_1) / \cosh^{-1}(\Omega_s/\Omega_p)$.
- **Butterworth poles** are uniformly distributed on a circle of radius $\Omega_c$ in the $s$-plane; LHP poles only for stability.
- **Digital filter** is described by $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$ or the difference equation $\sum b_k x(n-k) - \sum a_k y(n-k) = y(n)$.
- **Advantages of digital filters:** programmability, precision, linear phase (FIR), easy cascading, VLSI-friendly, adaptive capability.
- **Limitations of digital filters:** need A/D–D/A, sampling-rate bound, finite word-length effects, not feasible at very high (RF) frequencies.
- **Bridge to digital:** Impulse Invariance (no aliasing, band-limited only) vs Bilinear Transformation (always stable, requires pre-warping $\Omega' = (2/T)\tan(\Omega T/2)$).
- **Selection rule of thumb:** Butterworth for phase, Chebyshev-I for sharp LP, Chebyshev-II for flat passband, Elliptic for minimum order.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FIR Filter Design - Linear Phase Filters, Location of Zeroes, Design Using Fourier Series, Window Method - Rectangular, Hanning, Hamming Windows, Comparison of Window Frequency Responses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT758 - DIGITAL FILTER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** FIR Filter Design - Linear Phase Filters, Location of Zeroes, Design Using Fourier Series, Window Method - Rectangular, Hanning, Hamming Windows, Comparison of Window Frequency Responses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of FIR Filters

A **Finite Impulse Response (FIR) Filter** is a discrete-time filter whose impulse response $h(n)$ is of finite duration, i.e., it has a finite number of non-zero samples. The output $y(n)$ is computed as a finite weighted sum of present and past input samples:

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k) \cdot x(n-k)$$

where $N$ is the **filter length** (number of taps) and $h(k)$ are the **filter coefficients**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For a filter to possess **Linear Phase**, its impulse response must satisfy specific symmetry conditions. Linear phase is critical in applications where preserving the wave-shape (phase integrity) of the input signal is mandatory — for example, in digital audio, biomedical signal processing (ECG/EEG), and data transmission.

### 1.2 The Four Types of Linear-Phase FIR Filters

A linear-phase FIR filter must satisfy:

$$\angle H(e^{j\omega}) = -\alpha \omega \pm \beta$$

The impulse response symmetry yields **four distinct cases**, each with its own structural constraints on $N$ (length) and the type of filter it can realize (LP, HP, BP, BS).

| Type | Symmetry | Length N | Even/Odd Symmetry Center |
|------|----------|----------|--------------------------|
| **Type-I** | Symmetric | Odd | Even (around integer) |
| **Type-II** | Symmetric | Even | Odd (between samples) |
| **Type-III** | Antisymmetric | Odd | Even |
| **Type-IV** | Antisymmetric | Even | Odd |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Echo Choir Analogy:** Imagine a conductor (the filter) standing in front of a choir of singers (the input samples). Instead of letting each singer sing alone, the conductor makes each singer sing a *delayed* and *scaled* version of their note simultaneously. The result is a blended, phase-aligned harmony. **FIR filtering is exactly this:** a sliding weighted sum where each output sample is a "harmonized echo" of past inputs.

**Why Linear Phase?**
Imagine sending a photograph through a faulty wire that stretches the red channel more than the blue. The image is no longer faithful — distortion has occurred. Similarly, when a filter has **non-linear phase**, different frequency components of a signal are delayed by *different amounts*, distorting the waveform. A **linear phase filter** delays *all* frequencies equally, preserving the wave-shape — just like a perfect mirror for signals.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world example:** In an ECG machine, the QRS complex has clinical meaning only if its shape is preserved. A linear-phase FIR filter cleans the signal (removes 50 Hz mains hum) **without** distorting the cardiac waveform — saving diagnostic accuracy.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Group Delay** $\tau_g = -\frac{d\angle H(e^{j\omega})}{d\omega}$ = constant for linear phase.
- For Type-I and Type-II (symmetric): $\tau_g = \frac{N-1}{2}$.
- For Type-III and Type-IV (antisymmetric): $\tau_g = \frac{N-1}{2}$.
- **Constant Phase Term** $\beta = 0$ (Symmetric) or $\beta = \pi/2$ (Antisymmetric).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Linear Phase Condition — The 'Why' and 'How'

For a filter to have **linear phase**, the frequency response $H(e^{j\omega})$ must be expressible as:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\alpha\omega} \cdot H_r(\omega)$$

where $H_r(\omega)$ is real and even (called the **amplitude response**), and $\alpha$ is a real constant representing the **constant group delay**.

**The Why:** A linear $\angle H(e^{j\omega})$ vs $\omega$ curve means every spectral component travels through the filter in the same time — a prerequisite for **zero phase distortion**.

**The How:** In the time domain, this translates directly into **symmetry of impulse response**:

$$h(n) = h(N-1-n) \quad \text{(Symmetric — Types I, II)}$$

$$h(n) = -h(N-1-n) \quad \text{(Antisymmetric — Types III, IV)}$$

### 2.2 Location of Zeros — A Symmetry Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Zero-Pairing Theorem:** If $H(z)$ has linear phase, then its zeros appear in **mirror-image pairs**. Specifically, if $z_k$ is a zero, then $1/z_k$ is also a zero.

**Proof of Zero Pairing for Symmetric FIR:**

If $h(n) = h(N-1-n)$, then $H(z^{-1}) \cdot z^{N-1} = H(z)$.

Substituting $H(z) = \sum h(n) z^{-n}$:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h(n) z^{-(N-1-n)} = z^{-(N-1)} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} h(n) z^{n} = z^{-(N-1)} H(z^{-1})$$

Setting $H(z_0) = 0 \Rightarrow H(z_0^{-1}) = 0$, so $z_0^{-1}$ is also a zero.

**Consequence for design:** Zeros on the unit circle must appear in **conjugate pairs** $e^{\pm j\omega_k}$. Zeros *off* the unit circle form **quadruplets** $\{z_0, z_0^*, 1/z_0, 1/z_0^*\}$.

### 2.3 Design Using Fourier Series Method — Stepwise Logic

**Step 1: Specify the desired (ideal) frequency response $H_d(e^{j\omega})$.** This is piecewise constant (1 in passband, 0 in stopband).

**Step 2: Compute the desired impulse response** $h_d(n)$ by **inverse DTFT**:

$$h_d(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} H_d(e^{j\omega}) e^{j\omega n} d\omega$$

**Step 3: Recognize the Gibbs Phenomenon problem.** $h_d(n)$ is **infinite** in length — we cannot realize it as a causal FIR filter.

**Step 4: Truncate** $h_d(n)$ to length $N$ by multiplying with a window $w(n)$:

$$h(n) = h_d(n) \cdot w(n), \quad 0 \le n \le N-1$$

**Step 5: Check symmetry** of $h(n)$ to ensure linear phase. If $h_d(n)$ is symmetric about $n=0$, we shift it to make it causal and Type-I symmetric about $(N-1)/2$.

### 2.4 The Three Standard Windows

#### (a) Rectangular Window

$$w_R(n) = 1, \quad 0 \le n \le N-1$$

- **Transition Width:** $\Delta\omega = 0.89 \cdot \frac{2\pi}{N} = \frac{1.78\pi}{N}$
- **Mainlobe Width:** $\frac{4\pi}{N}$ (null-to-null)
- **Peak Sidelobe Level:** $-13$ dB
- **Minimum Stopband Attenuation:** $\approx 21$ dB
- **Pros:** Sharpest mainlobe → narrowest transition. **Cons:** Worst sidelobe leakage.

#### (b) Hanning (Raised Cosine) Window

$$w_{Hn}(n) = 0.5 \left[1 - \cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right)\right], \quad 0 \le n \le N-1$$

- **Transition Width:** $\Delta\omega = 3.11 \cdot \frac{2\pi}{N}$
- **Mainlobe Width:** $\frac{8\pi}{N}$
- **Peak Sidelobe Level:** $-31$ dB
- **Minimum Stopband Attenuation:** $\approx 44$ dB
- **Pros:** Much better sidelobe suppression. **Cons:** Wider mainlobe.

#### (c) Hamming Window

$$w_{Hm}(n) = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right), \quad 0 \le n \le N-1$$

- **Transition Width:** $\Delta\omega = 3.32 \cdot \frac{2\pi}{N}$
- **Mainlobe Width:** $\frac{8\pi}{N}$
- **Peak Sidelobe Level:** $-41$ dB
- **Minimum Stopband Attenuation:** $\approx 53$ dB
- **Pros:** Best sidelobe suppression among the three. **Cons:** Slightly wider mainlobe than Hanning.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity / Property | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---------------------|---------|---------------|
| **FIR Output** | $y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} h(k) x(n-k)$ | $N$ = filter length |
| **Linear Phase Frequency Response** | $H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\alpha\omega} H_r(\omega)$ | $\alpha$ = constant group delay |
| **Group Delay (Symmetric)** | $\tau_g = (N-1)/2$ | Integer for odd N, half-integer for even N |
| **Symmetry Condition** | $h(n) = h(N-1-n)$ | For Types I, II |
| **Antisymmetry Condition** | $h(n) = -h(N-1-n)$ | For Types III, IV |
| **Inverse DTFT (ideal impulse response)** | $h_d(n) = (1/2\pi) \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} H_d(e^{j\omega}) e^{j\omega n} d\omega$ | Piecewise constant ideal spec |
| **Windowed Impulse Response** | $h(n) = h_d(n) \cdot w(n)$ | Truncation of infinite $h_d$ |
| **Rectangular Window** | $w_R(n) = 1$ | $0 \le n \le N-1$ |
| **Hanning Window** | $w_{Hn}(n) = 0.5 [1 - \cos(2\pi n/(N-1))]$ | $0 \le n \le N-1$ |
| **Hamming Window** | $w_{Hm}(n) = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos(2\pi n/(N-1))$ | $0 \le n \le N-1$ |
| **Rectangular Transition Width** | $\Delta\omega = 0.89 \cdot (2\pi/N)$ | Narrowest, worst sidelobe |
| **Hanning Transition Width** | $\Delta\omega = 3.11 \cdot (2\pi/N)$ | Smoother tradeoff |
| **Hamming Transition Width** | $\Delta\omega = 3.32 \cdot (2\pi/N)$ | Best stopband attenuation |
| **Kaiser's Rule (estimate N)** | $N \approx (-20 \log_{10}\delta_s - 13) / (14.6 \Delta f) + 1$ | For given specs |
| **Sidelobe Roll-off (all windows)** | $-20$ dB/decade or $-6$ dB/octave | Asymptotic behavior |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Why Linear Phase FIR? |
|--------------------|----------------------|
| **Digital Audio / Music Production** | Preserves transient sharpness and stereo imaging |
| **Biomedical (ECG, EEG, EMG)** | Distortion-free diagnostic waveforms |
| **OFDM Communication** | Constant group delay across sub-carriers prevents ISI |
| **Image Processing (2-D FIR)** | Edge preservation during filtering |
| **Data Acquisition Systems** | Time-coherent multi-channel measurements |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Note:** Whenever your spec mentions *"no phase distortion"* or *"constant time delay"*, the answer is **always** a **linear-phase FIR filter** (typically Type-I for lowpass).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Symmetric Linear-Phase Frequency Response (Type-I)

**Statement:** If $h(n)$ is symmetric with odd length $N = 2M+1$ and symmetric about $n = M$:

$$h(n) = h(2M - n), \quad 0 \le n \le 2M$$

then the frequency response has linear phase.

**Step-by-Step Proof:**

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{2M} h(n) e^{-j\omega n}$$

Pair the terms $n$ and $2M - n$:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = h(M) e^{-j\omega M} + \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} \left[ h(n) e^{-j\omega n} + h(2M-n) e^{-j\omega(2M-n)} \right]$$

Apply symmetry $h(n) = h(2M - n)$:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = h(M) e^{-j\omega M} + \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} h(n) \left[ e^{-j\omega n} + e^{-j\omega(2M-n)} \right]$$

Factor $e^{-j\omega M}$:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega M} \left\{ h(M) + \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} h(n) \left[ e^{j\omega(M-n)} + e^{-j\omega(M-n)} \right] \right\}$$

Use Euler's identity $e^{j\theta} + e^{-j\theta} = 2\cos(\theta)$:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega M} \left[ h(M) + 2 \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} h(n) \cos[\omega(M - n)] \right]$$

The bracketed term is **real and even** in $\omega$ (since cosine is even). Therefore:

$$\boxed{H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega M} \cdot H_r(\omega), \quad H_r(\omega) = h(M) + 2\sum_{n=0}^{M-1} h(n)\cos[\omega(M-n)]}$$

This is **exactly linear phase** with group delay $\alpha = M = (N-1)/2$. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation: Ideal Lowpass Filter Impulse Response

**Given ideal specification:**

$$H_d(e^{j\omega}) = \begin{cases} 1, & \vert\omega\vert \le \omega_c \\ 0, & \omega_c < \vert\omega\vert \le \pi \end{cases}$$

**Step 1: Apply inverse DTFT:**

$$h_d(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\omega_c}^{\omega_c} (1) \cdot e^{j\omega n} d\omega$$

**Step 2: Evaluate the integral:**

$$h_d(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ \frac{e^{j\omega n}}{jn} \right]_{-\omega_c}^{\omega_c} = \frac{1}{2\pi jn} \left[ e^{j\omega_c n} - e^{-j\omega_c n} \right]$$

**Step 3: Apply Euler's identity $\sin(\theta) = (e^{j\theta} - e^{-j\theta})/(2j)$:**

$$h_d(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi jn} \cdot 2j \sin(\omega_c n) = \frac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\pi n}$$

$$\boxed{h_d(n) = \frac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\pi n}, \quad -\infty < n < \infty}$$

This is the **sinc function**, centered at $n=0$, which is **infinite** in extent.

**Step 4: Truncate using a window $w(n)$ of length $N=2M+1$:**

$$h(n) = h_d(n - M) \cdot w(n) = \frac{\sin[\omega_c(n - M)]}{\pi(n - M)} \cdot w(n), \quad 0 \le n \le 2M$$

Shifting by $M$ makes the filter **causal** and preserves symmetry about $n = M$ (Type-I linear phase).

### 3.3 Derivation: Window Frequency Response — Rectangular Case

The DTFT of a rectangular window of length $N$ is:

$$W_R(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} e^{-j\omega n} = \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega N}}{1 - e^{-j\omega}}$$

**Algebraic simplification using geometric series:**

Multiply numerator and denominator by $e^{j\omega N/2}/e^{j\omega/2}$:

$$W_R(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{e^{-j\omega N/2}(e^{j\omega N/2} - e^{-j\omega N/2})}{e^{-j\omega/2}(e^{j\omega/2} - e^{-j\omega/2})}$$

Apply $\sin(\theta) = (e^{j\theta} - e^{-j\theta})/(2j)$:

$$W_R(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega(N-1)/2} \cdot \frac{\sin(\omega N / 2)}{\sin(\omega/2)}$$

**Normalized magnitude (Dirichlet kernel):**

$$W_R(\omega) = \frac{\sin(\omega N / 2)}{\sin(\omega/2)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **Dirichlet kernel** has its first zero at $\omega = 2\pi/N$, which determines the **mainlobe width** as $\frac{4\pi}{N}$ (null-to-null). The peak sidelobe level is **-13 dB** relative to the mainlobe peak, occurring near $\omega = 3\pi/N$.

### 3.4 Derivation: Hamming Window — Why "0.54 / 0.46"?

The Hamming window is a **weighted combination** of three shifted Dirichlet kernels (via Euler's identity):

$$w_{Hm}(n) = 0.54 - 0.46\cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right)$$

Expanding the cosine:

$$w_{Hm}(n) = 0.54 \cdot w_R(n) - 0.23 \cdot e^{j2\pi n/(N-1)} \cdot w_R(n) - 0.23 \cdot e^{-j2\pi n/(N-1)} \cdot w_R(n)$$

The DTFT becomes:

$$W_{Hm}(e^{j\omega}) = 0.54 \cdot W_R(\omega) - 0.23 \cdot W_R(\omega - 2\pi/(N-1)) - 0.23 \cdot W_R(\omega + 2\pi/(N-1))$$

By choosing weights 0.54 and 0.46, the **first sidelobe of the central Dirichlet kernel** and the **mainlobe of the shifted kernels** are arranged to **cancel** at $\omega \approx 3\pi/N$, pushing sidelobe energy outward and achieving **−41 dB** peak sidelobe — a 28 dB improvement over the rectangular window.

### 3.5 Complete Design Procedure — Analytical Walkthrough

**Problem:** Design a Type-I linear-phase lowpass FIR filter with cutoff $\omega_c = 0.4\pi$ rad/sample, length $N = 11$ (so $M = 5$), using a **Hamming window**.

**Step 1: Compute $h_d(n)$ for $n$ from $0$ to $10$ using $h_d(n) = \sin[\omega_c(n - M)]/[\pi(n - M)]$:**

| $n$ | $(n - M)$ | $h_d(n) = \sin(0.4\pi(n-5))/[\pi(n-5)]$ |
|-----|-----------|------------------------------------------|
| 0 | -5 | $\sin(-2\pi)/(−5\pi) = 0$ |
| 1 | -4 | $\sin(-1.6\pi)/(−4\pi) = 0.1514$ |
| 2 | -3 | $\sin(-1.2\pi)/(−3\pi) = −0.1871$ |
| 3 | -2 | $\sin(-0.8\pi)/(−2\pi) = 0.1514$ |
| 4 | -1 | $\sin(-0.4\pi)/(−\pi) = 0.3027$ |
| 5 | 0  | $\sin(0)/(0) \rightarrow \omega_c/\pi = 0.4$ (L'Hopital) |
| 6 | 1  | $0.3027$ |
| 7 | 2  | $0.1514$ |
| 8 | 3  | $-0.1871$ |
| 9 | 4  | $0.1514$ |
| 10 | 5 | $0$ |

**Step 2: Compute Hamming window values $w(n) = 0.54 - 0.46\cos(2\pi n/10)$:**

| $n$ | $w(n)$ |
|-----|--------|
| 0 | 0.0800 |
| 1 | 0.1679 |
| 2 | 0.3979 |
| 3 | 0.6821 |
| 4 | 0.9121 |
| 5 | 1.0000 |
| 6 | 0.9121 |
| 7 | 0.6821 |
| 8 | 0.3979 |
| 9 | 0.1679 |
| 10 | 0.0800 |

**Step 3: Multiply element-wise to get $h(n) = h_d(n) \cdot w(n)$:**

| $n$ | $h_d(n)$ | $w(n)$ | $h(n)$ |
|-----|----------|--------|--------|
| 0 | 0 | 0.0800 | 0 |
| 1 | 0.1514 | 0.1679 | 0.0254 |
| 2 | -0.1871 | 0.3979 | -0.0745 |
| 3 | 0.1514 | 0.6821 | 0.1033 |
| 4 | 0.3027 | 0.9121 | 0.2761 |
| 5 | 0.4000 | 1.0000 | 0.4000 |
| 6 | 0.3027 | 0.9121 | 0.2761 |
| 7 | 0.1514 | 0.6821 | 0.1033 |
| 8 | -0.1871 | 0.3979 | -0.0745 |
| 9 | 0.1514 | 0.1679 | 0.0254 |
| 10 | 0 | 0.0800 | 0 |

**Step 4: Verify symmetry:** $h(0) = h(10) = 0$, $h(1) = h(9)$, $h(2) = h(8)$, etc. ✓ **Type-I linear phase confirmed.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 FIR Filter — Direct-Form Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeX["Input x(n)"] --> nodeA["z^-1 Delay"]
    nodeA --> nodeB["z^-1 Delay"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["z^-1 Delay"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["z^-1 Delay"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["z^-1 Delay"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["z^-1 Delay"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["z^-1 Delay"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["z^-1 Delay"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["z^-1 Delay"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["z^-1 Delay"]
    nodeX -- x n --> nodeK["h 0"]
    nodeA -- x n-1 --> nodeL["h 1"]
    nodeB -- x n-2 --> nodeM["h 2"]
    nodeC -- x n-3 --> nodeN["h 3"]
    nodeD -- x n-4 --> nodeO["h 4"]
    nodeE -- x n-5 --> nodeP["h 5"]
    nodeF -- x n-6 --> nodeQ["h 6"]
    nodeG -- x n-7 --> nodeR["h 7"]

    nodeJ -- x n-10 --> nodeU["h 10"]

```

### 4.2 FIR Filter Design Flowchart (Fourier-Series + Window Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Step 1 Define Ideal Spec Hd omega on -pi to pi"] --> nodeB["Step 2 Compute hd n via Inverse DTFT"]
    nodeB --> nodeC{"Step 3 Is hd n Symmetric about n=0"}
    nodeC -- Yes --> nodeD["Step 4 Shift hd n by M to make causal"]
    nodeC -- No --> nodeE["Use Antisymmetric FIR Type III or IV"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Step 5 Choose Window Function w n"]
    nodeF --> nodeG{"Step 6 Choose Window Type"}
    nodeG -- Rectangular --> nodeH["Sharpest Transition, 21 dB Stopband"]
    nodeG -- Hanning --> nodeI["Smooth, 44 dB Stopband"]
    nodeG -- Hamming --> nodeJ["Best 53 dB Stopband"]
    nodeH --> nodeK["Step 7 Multiply h n = hd n times w n"]
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL["Step 8 Verify Symmetry h n equals h N-1-n"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["Step 9 Check Magnitude Spec against Design Goal"]
    nodeM -- Meets Spec --> nodeN["Done Output h 0 to h N-1"]
    nodeM -- Fails --> nodeO["Increase N and Repeat from Step 5"]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP["Z-plane Unit Circle"] --> nodeQ["Zeros on Unit Circle: e to the jwk Conjugate Pair"]
    nodeQ --> nodeR["Zeros off Unit Circle: Form Reciprocal Quadruplets zk zk* 1 over zk 1 over zk*"]
    nodeR --> nodeS["For Real Coefficients All Zeros in Mirror Symmetric Pairs"]
```
