It allows the **multiplication of cascaded filter gains** to become simple addition. **Conversion of 60 dB to linear factor:** $$ \begin{aligned} A_s &= 60 \text{ dB} \\ \delta_s &= 10^{-A_s / 20} = 10^{-60/20} = 10^{-3} = 0.001 \end{aligned} $$ Hence the stopband signal is reduced to **0.1%** (a factor of 1/1000) of the passband signal level. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Module 3, Choice Option 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]** (a) **[7 Marks]** A digital low-pass filter is to be designed with the following specifications: - Passband edge: $f_p = 1500$ Hz - Stopband edge: $f_s = 2000$ Hz - Passband ripple: $A_p = 1$ dB - Stopband attenuation: $A_s = 40$ dB - Sampling rate: $F_s = 8000$ Hz Compute the digital frequency specifications $\omega_p$, $\omega_s$, and the linear ripple factors $\delta_p$, $\delta_s$. Draw and label the tolerance scheme. (b) **[7 Marks]** Convert the same specifications to analog form (assuming impulse invariance technique with $T = 1/F_s$) and determine the analog transition width $\Delta\Omega$. State two practical applications where such a filter is essential. #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Step 1 — Convert analog frequencies to digital normalized frequencies:** $$ \begin{aligned} \omega_p &= 2\pi \cdot \frac{f_p}{F_s} = 2\pi \cdot \frac{1500}{8000} = 2\pi \cdot 0.1875 = 0.375\pi \text{ rad/sample} \\ \omega_s &= 2\pi \cdot \frac{f_s}{F_s} = 2\pi \cdot \frac{2000}{8000} = 2\pi \cdot 0.25 = 0.5\pi \text{ rad/sample} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Convert dB ripples to linear factors:** $$ \begin{aligned} \delta_p &= \frac{10^{A_p/20} - 1}{10^{A_p/20} + 1} = \frac{10^{0.05} - 1}{10^{0.05} + 1} = \frac{1.1220 - 1}{1.1220 + 1} = \frac{0.1220}{2.1220} \approx 0.0575 \\ \delta_s &= 10^{-A_s/20} = 10^{-2} = 0.01 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Tolerance scheme (description for the exam):** On a graph of $|H(e^{j\omega})|$ versus $\omega$ (x-axis from $0$ to $\pi$): - Draw a **horizontal line at $1 + \delta_p = 1.0575$** and another at $1 - \delta_p = 0.9425$ — these bound the passband. - Draw a **horizontal line at $\delta_s = 0.01$** — the stopband ceiling. - Mark $\omega_p = 0.375\pi$ and $\omega_s = 0.5\pi$ on the x-axis. - The transition band is the gap between them: $\Delta\omega = 0.5\pi - 0.375\pi = 0.125\pi$ rad/sample. **Digital specification block:** | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | $\omega_p$ | $0.375\pi$ rad/sample | | $\omega_s$ | $0.5\pi$ rad/sample | | $\delta_p$ | $0.0575$ | | $\delta_s$ | $0.01$ | | $A_p$ | $1$ dB | | $A_s$ | $40$ dB | #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — Compute the analog frequencies using $T = 1/F_s = 1/8000 = 0.125$ ms:** $$ \begin{aligned} \Omega_p &= \frac{\omega_p}{T} = 2\pi f_p = 2\pi \cdot 1500 = 3000\pi \text{ rad/sec} \approx 9424.78 \text{ rad/sec} \\ \Omega_s &= 2\pi f_s = 2\pi \cdot 2000 = 4000\pi \text{ rad/sec} \approx 12566.37 \text{ rad/sec} \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Transition width in analog domain:** $$ \Delta\Omega = \Omega_s - \Omega_p = 12566.37 - 9424.78 \approx 3141.59 \text{ rad/sec} \;\;(\text{equivalently } 1000\pi) $$ **Step 3 — Two practical applications:** 1. **Audio crossover networks** in loudspeakers: separating low-frequency (woofer, below 1500 Hz) from mid/high-frequency (tweeter, above 2000 Hz) components. 2. **Anti-aliasing filter** in a digital audio system (e.g., before an ADC sampling speech at 8 kHz) — this is the most canonical application, ensuring no frequency above $F_s/2 = 4000$ Hz passes through. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Module 3, Choice Option 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]** (a) **[7 Marks]** Explain the concept of a *linear-phase* FIR filter. For a length-9 linear-phase FIR filter, compute the group delay and show that the phase response is $\angle H(e^{j\omega}) = -4\omega$. (b) **[7 Marks]** An FIR low-pass filter is designed using the Kaiser window with $A_s = 50$ dB and transition width $\Delta f = 500$ Hz at a sampling rate $F_s = 10$ kHz. Calculate the required filter order $N$ and the Kaiser $\beta$ parameter. Use the empirical formula. #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Concept:** A **linear-phase** FIR filter is one whose phase response is a *linear* function of frequency. This means *all* frequency components are delayed by the same amount of time, called the **group delay** $\tau$, so the filter preserves the *shape* of the input waveform (no phase distortion). For an FIR filter of length $N$ (with symmetric impulse response), the group delay is: $$ \tau = \frac{N - 1}{2} $$ **For $N = 9$:** $$ \tau = \frac{9 - 1}{2} = \frac{8}{2} = 4 \text{ samples} $$ **Phase response:** The frequency response of a linear-phase FIR filter can be written as: $$ H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega \tau} \cdot \tilde{H}(\omega) $$ where $\tilde{H}(\omega)$ is a *real, even* function. Hence the *phase* is: $$ \angle H(e^{j\omega}) = -\omega \tau = -\omega \cdot 4 = -4\omega $$ This is a *straight line through the origin* with slope $-4$ — a textbook linear-phase response. Any input signal gets delayed by exactly 4 samples, regardless of its frequency content. #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — Compute the normalized transition width $\Delta f / F_s$:** $$ \frac{\Delta f}{F_s} = \frac{500}{10000} = 0.05 $$ **Step 2 — Use the empirical Kaiser order formula:** $$ N \approx \frac{-20 \log_{10}\!\sqrt{\delta_p \delta_s} - 13}{14.6 \cdot (\Delta f / F_s)} $$ We need $\delta_p$ and $\delta_s$. Since only $A_s$ is given, we approximate $\delta_p$ for a moderate design as $\delta_p = 0.05$ (≈ 0.92 dB ripple). $\delta_s = 10^{-50/20} = 10^{-2.5} \approx 0.00316$. $$ \begin{aligned} N &\approx \frac{-20 \log_{10}\!\sqrt{0.05 \times 0.00316} - 13}{14.6 \times 0.05} \\ &\approx \frac{-20 \log_{10}\!\sqrt{0.000158} - 13}{0.73} \\ &\approx \frac{-20 \log_{10}(0.01257) - 13}{0.73} \\ &\approx \frac{-20 \times (-1.9005) - 13}{0.73} \\ &\approx \frac{38.01 - 13}{0.73} \\ &\approx \frac{25.01}{0.73} \\ &\approx 34.26 \\ \end{aligned} $$ Round up to the next integer: **$N = 35$**. **Step 3 — Compute Kaiser $\beta$ for $A_s = 50$ dB:** Since $A_s = 50 > 50$? No — $A_s = 50$ is at the boundary. Use the formula for $50 \le A_s \le 21$ — actually for $A_s > 50$, $\beta = 0.1102(A_s - 8.7)$. For $A_s = 50$, we use the lower-range formula since $50 \le 50$: $$ \begin{aligned} \beta &= 0.5842 \cdot (A_s - 21)^{0.4} + 0.07886 \cdot (A_s - 21) \\ &= 0.5842 \cdot (29)^{0.4} + 0.07886 \cdot 29 \\ &= 0.5842 \cdot 3.691 + 2.287 \\ &\approx 2.157 + 2.287 \\ &\approx 4.444 \end{aligned} $$ So **$\beta \approx 4.44$** and **$N = 35$**. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Lose Marks — A KTU Examiner's Honest List** > 1. **Forgetting the sign convention**: $A_s$ is a *positive* dB value, but $\log_{10}(\delta_s)$ is *negative* since $\delta_s < 1$. Students write $+20 \log_{10}(\delta_s)$ and get a wrong (negative) attenuation. **Always write $A_s = -20 \log_{10}(\delta_s)$.** > 2. **Confusing $\omega_p$ with $f_p$**: $\omega$ is in *radians per sample*, $f$ is in *Hz*. Missing the factor $2\pi$ or $F_s$ costs 2–3 marks instantly. > 3. **Not drawing the tolerance scheme** when explicitly asked. Even a rough sketch with the four parameters labelled earns full marks. > 4. **Forgetting to round $N$ up to the nearest integer** in Kaiser-window problems. $N$ must be **odd** for a Type-I linear-phase low-pass filter. > 5. **Units mismatch**: Mixing analog $\Omega$ (rad/sec) with digital $\omega$ (rad/sample) in the same equation. Always state units explicitly. > 6. **Group delay sign error**: Phase is $-\omega \tau$, *not* $+\omega \tau$. The minus sign preserves causality. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - A **filter specification** is a four-number contract: $\omega_p$, $\omega_s$, $A_p$, $A_s$ (digital) or $\Omega_p$, $\Omega_s$, $A_p$, $A_s$ (analog). - **Passband ripple** $A_p$ is the peak-to-peak magnitude variation in the passband, expressed in dB. The conversion is $A_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1 + \delta_p}{1 - \delta_p}\right)$. - **Stopband attenuation** $A_s$ is the *minimum* rejection in the stopband. Conversion: $A_s = -20 \log_{10}(\delta_s)$. - The **tolerance scheme** is the *graphical form* of the specification — always draw it in exams. - The **transition band width** $\Delta\omega = \omega_s - \omega_p$ directly controls the *filter order* and *sharpness*. Narrower transition = higher $N$ = more computations. - **Digital normalized frequency**: $\omega = 2\pi f / F_s$ with the full Nyquist range $\omega \in [-\pi, +\pi]$. - **Bilinear transform frequency warping**: $\Omega = (2/T)\tan(\omega/2)$ — used to convert analog prototype specs to digital. - **Linear-phase FIR** has constant group delay $\tau = (N-1)/2$; phase is $\angle H = -\omega \tau$ (a straight line). - **Kaiser window** empirical formulas for $N$ and $\beta$ are the *most-tested* design equations in Module 3. - **Inverse conversions** ($\delta_p$ from $A_p$, $\delta_s$ from $A_s$) are required before plugging into MATLAB/Python design functions. - Practical ripples: $A_p \approx 0.1$ to $3$ dB; $A_s \approx 30$ to $80$ dB. - Filter order $N$ **increases** when $A_p \downarrow$, $A_s \uparrow$, or $\Delta\omega \downarrow$ — remember this single trend to solve most design trade-off MCQs. - Always **round $N$ up to an odd integer** for Type-I linear-phase FIR low-pass filters.

topics.title: Conversion to Digital Responses - Impulse Invariant and Bi-linear Transformations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT758 - DIGITAL FILTER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Conversion to Digital Responses - Impulse Invariant and Bi-linear Transformations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Digital Filter Design, **analog-to-digital filter transformations** are mathematical mappings that convert a continuous-time transfer function $H_a(s)$ (in the $s$-domain) into an equivalent discrete-time transfer function $H(z)$ (in the $z$-domain). This is essential because many practical digital filter design problems begin with well-established analog prototype filters (Butterworth, Chebyshev, Elliptic), which must then be translated into the digital domain for real-time implementation on DSP processors.

The KTU 2024 Scheme specifically focuses on two dominant transformation techniques:

1. **Impulse Invariant Transformation (IIT)** — A direct time-domain sampling approach where the digital filter's impulse response $h[n]$ equals the sampled version of the analog filter's impulse response $h_a(t)$.

2. **Bilinear Transformation (BLT)** — A conformal algebraic mapping that substitutes a rational function of $z$ for the Laplace variable $s$, providing a one-to-one mapping between the $j\Omega$-axis and the unit circle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The module emphasizes the **mapping relationships** ($s \rightarrow z$), the **frequency response preservation** characteristics, the **aliasing phenomenon** in IIT, and the **frequency warping** correction in BLT using pre-warping.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a beautifully crafted **vinyl LP record** (analog filter) that contains a perfect musical waveform. You want to enjoy this same music on a **modern digital player** (digital filter).

- **Impulse Invariant Transformation** is like using a microphone to *record the sound* of the LP at uniform time intervals (sampling). The music's shape is preserved, but if the original contains high frequencies above the sampling threshold, you get *aliasing* (distorted, ghostly reflections of the original sound). It is *time-domain faithful* but *frequency-domain risky*.

- **Bilinear Transformation** is like using a *professional transcoding engineer* who mathematically re-encodes the entire LP into a digital master. No aliasing occurs because the entire analog spectrum is *squeezed* (warped) into the digital spectrum. However, the engineer must *pre-adjust* certain frequencies (pre-warping) to compensate for the squeezing, ensuring the critical cutoff points remain musically accurate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Choose IIT when analog filter is strictly band-limited (e.g., anti-aliasing pre-filter is present). Choose BLT when precise frequency response at the edges is critical and aliasing must be eliminated at all costs.

### 1.3 Critical Standard Metrics

- **Sampling Period $T$** (in **seconds**) — controls the mapping scale and aliasing severity.
- **Sampling Frequency $F_s = 1/T$** (in **Hz**) — must satisfy the **Nyquist criterion** $F_s \geq 2\Omega_{max}$ for IIT to avoid aliasing.
- **Pre-warping Frequency $\Omega_p$** (in **rad/sec**) — mandatory correction parameter in BLT.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Impulse Invariant Transformation (IIT) — Operational Logic

The Impulse Invariant Method works on the principle that if $H_a(s)$ is expanded using **partial fractions**, the impulse response $h_a(t)$ becomes a sum of decaying exponentials. Sampling $h_a(t)$ at $t = nT$ yields a discrete sequence $h[n]$ whose $Z$-transform gives $H(z)$.

**Step-by-step logic of IIT:**

- **Step 1:** Express the analog transfer function $H_a(s)$ in partial fraction expansion form.
- **Step 2:** Identify each analog pole $p_i$ in the $s$-plane.
- **Step 3:** Apply the mapping rule: each analog pole at $s = p_i$ maps to a digital pole at $z_i = e^{p_i T}$.
- **Step 4:** Construct $H(z)$ by replacing each analog term $\frac{R_i}{s - p_i}$ with its digital counterpart $\frac{R_i T}{1 - e^{p_i T} z^{-1}}$.
- **Step 5:** Verify that $h[n] = T \cdot h_a(nT)$ for all $n \geq 0$.

**The 'Why' behind IIT:**

It preserves the *time-domain shape* of the impulse response exactly (up to scaling by $T$). It is mathematically elegant and yields filters whose transient behavior mirrors the analog prototype. However, because the sampling operation inherently *replicates* the spectrum at multiples of $F_s$, aliasing is unavoidable unless $H_a(j\Omega)$ is strictly band-limited.

### 2.2 Bilinear Transformation (BLT) — Operational Logic

The Bilinear Transformation uses a **first-order rational approximation** of the integration operator $1/s$. By applying the trapezoidal rule for numerical integration to analog filter differential equations, a one-to-one algebraic map emerges.

**Step-by-step logic of BLT:**

- **Step 1:** Substitute $s = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{z - 1}{z + 1}$ directly into the analog transfer function $H_a(s)$.
- **Step 2:** Simplify algebraically to obtain the digital transfer function $H(z)$.
- **Step 3:** Pre-warp the critical frequencies using $\Omega_{p,new} = \frac{2}{T} \tan\left(\frac{\Omega_{p,old} T}{2}\right)$ BEFORE designing the analog prototype.
- **Step 4:** Design the analog filter using the pre-warped frequencies.
- **Step 5:** Apply the BLT substitution to obtain the final $H(z)$.

**The 'Why' behind BLT:**

It provides a **conformal mapping** — the entire imaginary axis $s = j\Omega$ (from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$) compresses into exactly one traversal of the unit circle $z = e^{j\omega}$ (from $-\pi$ to $+\pi$). This guarantees *zero aliasing* but introduces *frequency warping* (a non-linear compression) that must be corrected for critical design frequencies.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter / Concept** | **Impulse Invariant Transformation** | **Bilinear Transformation** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Core Mapping Equation | $z = e^{sT}$ | $s = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{z-1}{z+1}$ |
| Inverse Mapping | $s = \frac{1}{T} \ln(z)$ | $z = \frac{1 + sT/2}{1 - sT/2}$ |
| Pole Mapping Rule | $z_i = e^{p_i T}$ | $z_i = \frac{1 + p_i T/2}{1 - p_i T/2}$ |
| Stability Condition | $\vert p_i \vert < 0 \Rightarrow \vert z_i \vert < 1$ | $\text{Re}(p_i) < 0 \Rightarrow \vert z_i \vert < 1$ |
| Frequency Relationship | $\omega = \Omega T$ (linear, with aliasing) | $\Omega = \frac{2}{T} \tan\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$ (warped) |
| Pre-warping Formula | Not required | $\Omega_p = \frac{2}{T} \tan\left(\frac{\omega_p T}{2}\right)$ |
| Aliasing Effect | **Yes** — present if not band-limited | **No** — guaranteed absent |
| Order Preservation | Preserves order $N$ | Preserves order $N$ |
| Best Suited For | Low-pass / band-limited filters | All filter types (LP, HP, BP, BS) |
| Computational Complexity | Low (partial fractions) | Moderate (rational substitution) |
| Magnitude Response Match | Poor near $\omega = \pi$ | Excellent at pre-warped frequencies |
| Phase Response | Linear-phase preserved for $h(t)$ shape | Non-linear warping introduced |

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability Equivalence:** Both transformations map the *Left-Half $s$-Plane* (LHP, stable analog region) into the *Interior of the Unit Circle* (stable digital region) — a critical property ensuring that stable analog filters always produce stable digital filters.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Impulse Invariant Method** is heavily used in **speech processing** and **biomedical signal processing** (ECG, EEG filtering) where the analog front-end already includes a strong anti-aliasing low-pass filter, and the time-domain pulse shape must be preserved (e.g., simulating a matched filter for radar pulse detection).
- **Bilinear Transformation** is the **industry standard** for designing digital audio equalizers, parametric filters, and digital crossover networks in loudspeaker systems, where precise control over cutoff frequencies and the absence of aliasing artifacts are paramount.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Impulse Invariant Transformation (Pole Mapping)

**Starting Point:** Consider an analog transfer function with $N$ simple poles in partial fraction form:

$$H_a(s) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{R_i}{s - p_i}$$

**Step 1:** The inverse Laplace transform gives the analog impulse response:

$$h_a(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} R_i e^{p_i t} u(t)$$

**Step 2:** Sample $h_a(t)$ at uniform intervals $t = nT$:

$$h[n] = h_a(nT) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} R_i e^{p_i nT} u[n] = \sum_{i=1}^{N} R_i \left(e^{p_i T}\right)^n u[n]$$

**Step 3:** Recognize that $e^{p_i T}$ is the discrete-time pole, and apply the $Z$-transform pair:
- Time-domain: $R_i (e^{p_i T})^n u[n]$
- $Z$-domain: $\dfrac{R_i}{1 - e^{p_i T} z^{-1}}$

**Step 4:** Sum all contributions to obtain $H(z)$. The $T$ factor emerges from the sampling scaling relationship $h[n] = T \cdot h_a(nT)$ (for proper amplitude preservation under Riemann integration approximation):

$$H(z) = T \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{R_i}{1 - e^{p_i T} z^{-1}}$$

**Step 5:** Conclude the **pole mapping rule** — each analog pole $p_i$ in the $s$-plane maps to a digital pole $z_i = e^{p_i T}$ in the $z$-plane.

### 3.2 Derivation: Bilinear Transformation (Trapezoidal Integration Approximation)

**Starting Point:** Consider the analog integrator transfer function $H_a(s) = \frac{1}{s}$ corresponding to $y(t) = \int_{0}^{t} x(\tau) d\tau$.

**Step 1:** Apply the **Trapezoidal Rule** for numerical integration between samples:

$$y(nT) = y((n-1)T) + \frac{T}{2}\left[x(nT) + x((n-1)T)\right]$$

**Step 2:** Take the $Z$-transform of both sides (using the time-shift property):

$$Y(z) = z^{-1} Y(z) + \frac{T}{2}\left[X(z) + z^{-1} X(z)\right]$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the ratio $Y(z)/X(z)$:

$$Y(z)\left[1 - z^{-1}\right] = \frac{T}{2} X(z)\left[1 + z^{-1}\right]$$

$$\frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{T}{2} \cdot \frac{1 + z^{-1}}{1 - z^{-1}}$$

**Step 4:** Equate the discrete integrator to the analog integrator $1/s$ and solve for $s$:

$$\frac{1}{s} = \frac{T}{2} \cdot \frac{1 + z^{-1}}{1 - z^{-1}} = \frac{T}{2} \cdot \frac{z + 1}{z - 1}$$

**Step 5:** Invert the relationship to obtain the standard BLT substitution:

$$s = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{z - 1}{z + 1}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Frequency Warping in BLT

**Starting Point:** Substitute $s = j\Omega$ and $z = e^{j\omega}$ into the BLT equation:

$$j\Omega = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{e^{j\omega} - 1}{e^{j\omega} + 1}$$

**Step 1:** Use Euler's identity: $e^{j\omega} - 1 = e^{j\omega/2}(e^{j\omega/2} - e^{-j\omega/2}) = 2j e^{j\omega/2} \sin(\omega/2)$

**Step 2:** Similarly, $e^{j\omega} + 1 = e^{j\omega/2}(e^{j\omega/2} + e^{-j\omega/2}) = 2 e^{j\omega/2} \cos(\omega/2)$

**Step 3:** Substitute back:

$$j\Omega = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{2j e^{j\omega/2} \sin(\omega/2)}{2 e^{j\omega/2} \cos(\omega/2)} = j \cdot \frac{2}{T} \tan\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$$

**Step 4:** Equate real and imaginary parts:

$$\boxed{\Omega = \frac{2}{T} \tan\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)}$$

**Step 5:** Inverse the relationship to find $\omega$ in terms of $\Omega$:

$$\omega = 2 \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\Omega T}{2}\right)$$

### 3.4 Analytical Worked Example — BLT with Pre-Warping

**Problem:** Design a digital low-pass filter with passband edge at $\omega_p = 0.2\pi$ rad and sampling period $T = 1$ second, using a 2nd-order Butterworth analog prototype. Use Bilinear Transformation.

**Step 1:** Compute the pre-warped analog frequency:

$$\Omega_p = \frac{2}{T} \tan\left(\frac{\omega_p}{2}\right) = 2 \tan\left(\frac{0.2\pi}{2}\right) = 2 \tan(0.1\pi)$$

$$\Omega_p = 2 \tan(0.31416) = 2 \times 0.32492 = 0.64984 \text{ rad/sec}$$

**Step 2:** The 2nd-order normalized Butterworth prototype is:

$$H_a(s) = \frac{1}{s^2 + \sqrt{2} s + 1}$$

**Step 3:** De-normalize by substituting $s \rightarrow s/\Omega_p$:

$$H_a(s) = \frac{\Omega_p^2}{s^2 + \sqrt{2} \Omega_p s + \Omega_p^2}$$

Substituting $\Omega_p = 0.64984$:

$$H_a(s) = \frac{0.4223}{s^2 + 0.9190 s + 0.4223}$$

**Step 4:** Apply BLT substitution $s = \frac{2(z-1)}{z+1}$ (since $T = 1$):

$$s^2 = \frac{4(z-1)^2}{(z+1)^2}, \quad s = \frac{2(z-1)}{z+1}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute into the denominator:

$$\frac{4(z-1)^2}{(z+1)^2} + \frac{1.838(z-1)}{(z+1)} + 0.4223$$

**Step 6:** Multiply through by $(z+1)^2$ and expand to obtain $H(z)$. After algebraic simplification (omitted for brevity, but routine expansion yields):

$$H(z) = \frac{0.0701(1 + 2z^{-1} + z^{-2})}{1 - 1.1818 z^{-1} + 0.6058 z^{-2}}$$

**Step 7:** Verify stability — both denominator coefficients are positive and the discriminant is negative, confirming complex conjugate poles strictly inside the unit circle.

### 3.5 Analytical Worked Example — Impulse Invariant Transformation

**Problem:** Convert $H_a(s) = \dfrac{2}{(s+1)(s+2)}$ into $H(z)$ using IIT with $T = 0.5$ sec.

**Step 1:** Partial fraction expansion:

$$\frac{2}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{A}{s+1} + \frac{B}{s+2}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for residues:
- $A = \left.\frac{2}{s+2}\right|_{s=-1} = \frac{2}{1} = 2$
- $B = \left.\frac{2}{s+1}\right|_{s=-2} = \frac{2}{-1} = -2$

**Step 3:** Apply the IIT formula $H(z) = T \sum \dfrac{R_i}{1 - e^{p_i T} z^{-1}}$:

$$H(z) = 0.5 \left[\frac{2}{1 - e^{-0.5} z^{-1}} + \frac{-2}{1 - e^{-1.0} z^{-1}}\right]$$

**Step 4:** Simplify numerically ($e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$, $e^{-1.0} = 0.3679$):

$$H(z) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.6065 z^{-1}} - \frac{1}{1 - 0.3679 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 5:** Combine over a common denominator:

$$H(z) = \frac{(1 - 0.3679 z^{-1}) - (1 - 0.6065 z^{-1})}{(1 - 0.6065 z^{-1})(1 - 0.3679 z^{-1})}$$

$$H(z) = \frac{0.2386 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.9744 z^{-1} + 0.2231 z^{-2}}$$

**Step 6:** Both poles $|z_1| = 0.6065 < 1$ and $|z_2| = 0.3679 < 1$ — the digital filter is stable.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Overall Transformation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Analog Prototype H_a of s] --> B{Design Goal Identified}
    B --> C[Low Pass or Band Limited]
    B --> D[Any Filter Type Required]
    C --> E[Impulse Invariant Method]
    D --> F[Bilinear Transformation]
    E --> G[Partial Fraction Expansion]
    G --> H[Map Poles z_i equals e to power p_i T]
    H --> I[Construct H of z]
    F --> J[Pre Warp Critical Frequencies]
    J --> K[Substitute s equals 2 over T times z minus 1 over z plus 1]
    K --> I
    I --> L[Verify Stability]
    L --> M[Final Digital Filter H of z]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — s-Plane to z-Plane Mapping Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S_DOMAIN[Analog S Plane]
        S1[Origin s equals 0]
        S2[Stable LHP Pole]
        S3[Imaginary Axis j Omega]
        S4[Unstable RHP Pole]
        S5[Negative Infinity]
        S6[Positive Infinity]
    end
    subgraph Z_DOMAIN[Digital Z Plane]
        Z1[Center z equals 0]
        Z2[Inside Unit Circle Stable]
        Z3[Unit Circle Boundary]
        Z4[Outside Unit Circle Unstable]
        Z5[Point z equals minus 1]
        Z6[Point z equals plus 1]
    end
    S1 --> Z1
    S2 --> Z2
    S3 --> Z3
    S4 --> Z4
    S5 --> Z5
    S6 --> Z6
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — BLT Pre-Warping Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Digital Specifications omega_p omega_s] --> B[Compute Pre Warped Analog Frequencies]
    B --> B1[Omega_p equals 2 over T times tan of omega_p T over 2]
    B --> B2[Omega_s equals 2 over T times tan of omega_s T over 2]
    B1 --> C[Design Analog Prototype H_a of s]
    B2 --> C
    C --> D[Apply BLT Substitution]
    D --> D1[s replaced by 2 over T times z minus 1 over z plus 1]
    D1 --> E[Simplify and Normalize Gain]
    E --> F[Final Digital Transfer Function H of z]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Stability Region Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IIT_MAPPING[Impulse Invariant Mapping]
        I1[Analog Stable LHP Strip] --> I2[Infinite Number of Strips in s Plane]
        I2 --> I3[Each Strip Maps to Z Plane]
        I3 --> I4[Overlapping Creates Aliasing]
    end
    subgraph BLT_MAPPING[Bilinear Transformation Mapping]
        M1[Entire j Omega Axis minus infinity to infinity] --> M2[One to One Map]
        M2 --> M3[Traverses Unit Circle Once]
        M3 --> M4[No Aliasing Possible]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]:** State the Impulse Invariant Method of digitizing an analog filter. Mention its main limitation.

**Model Answer:**

The Impulse Invariant Method is a technique for converting an analog transfer function $H_a(s)$ into a digital transfer function $H(z)$ such that the impulse response of the digital filter is a uniformly sampled version of the analog filter's impulse response, i.e., $h[n] = T \cdot h_a(nT)$.

The pole mapping rule is: each analog pole at $s = p_i$ maps to a digital pole at $z_i = e^{p_i T}$. The transformation formula is:

$$H(z) = T \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{R_i}{1 - e^{p_i T} z^{-1}}$$

**Main Limitation:** The method suffers from *aliasing* of the frequency response because the sampling process replicates the spectrum periodically. If the analog filter is not strictly band-limited to below $F_s/2$, the replicated spectra overlap and distort the digital magnitude response, particularly near $\omega = \pi$.

---

> **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]:** What is frequency warping in the Bilinear Transformation? Why is pre-warping necessary?

**Model Answer:**

**Frequency Warping** is the non-linear compression of the analog frequency axis $\Omega$ into the digital frequency axis $\omega$ that occurs during the Bilinear Transformation, governed by the relation:

$$\Omega = \frac{2}{T} \tan\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$$

This means that equal increments in $\omega$ do not correspond to equal increments in $\Omega$ — low frequencies are stretched while high frequencies are compressed.

**Necessity of Pre-Warping:** Because of this non-linear mapping, if a digital filter is designed to have a cutoff at $\omega_p$, the corresponding analog prototype would have a *different* actual cutoff after the substitution. Pre-warping compensates for this by *anticipating* the warping: the analog prototype is designed with a pre-warped frequency $\Omega_p = \frac{2}{T}\tan(\omega_p T/2)$ so that *after* the BLT substitution, the digital filter achieves the *exact* desired cutoff $\omega_p$.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]:**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** An analog filter has the transfer function $H_a(s) = \dfrac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)}$. Convert it into a digital filter using the Impulse Invariant Method with sampling period $T = 1$ second. Also determine whether the resulting digital filter is stable.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the Bilinear Transformation technique for analog-to-digital filter conversion. Derive the frequency warping relation and explain the concept of pre-warping with a suitable example.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Apply partial fraction expansion to $H_a(s) = \dfrac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)}$:

$$H_a(s) = \frac{A}{s+1} + \frac{B}{s+2}$$

**Step 2:** Determine residues:
- $A = \left.\dfrac{1}{s+2}\right|_{s=-1} = \dfrac{1}{1} = 1$
- $B = \left.\dfrac{1}{s+1}\right|_{s=-2} = \dfrac{1}{-1} = -1$

So, $H_a(s) = \dfrac{1}{s+1} - \dfrac{1}{s+2}$.

**Step 3:** Apply the IIT formula $H(z) = T \sum \dfrac{R_i}{1 - e^{p_i T}z^{-1}}$ with $T = 1$:

- $p_1 = -1 \Rightarrow e^{p_1 T} = e^{-1} = 0.3679$
- $p_2 = -2 \Rightarrow e^{p_2 T} = e^{-2} = 0.1353$

$$H(z) = 1 \cdot \left[\frac{1}{1 - 0.3679 z^{-1}} - \frac{1}{1 - 0.1353 z^{-1}}\right]$$

**Step 4:** Combine into a single rational function:

$$H(z) = \frac{(1 - 0.1353 z^{-1}) - (1 - 0.3679 z^{-1})}{(1 - 0.3679 z^{-1})(1 - 0.1353 z^{-1})}$$

$$H(z) = \frac{0.2326 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5032 z^{-1} + 0.0498 z^{-2}}$$

**Step 5:** Stability check — the digital poles are the roots of $1 - 0.5032 z^{-1} + 0.0498 z^{-2} = 0$, i.e., $z^2 - 0.5032 z + 0.0498 = 0$.

Solving: $z = \dfrac{0.5032 \pm \sqrt{0.2532 - 0.1992}}{2} = \dfrac{0.5032 \pm j0.2325}{2} = 0.2516 \pm j0.1162$.

Pole magnitudes: $|z_1| = |z_2| = \sqrt{0.2516^2 + 0.1162^2} = \sqrt{0.0633 + 0.0135} = \sqrt{0.0768} = 0.2771$.

Since $|z_1|, |z_2| = 0.2771 < 1$, **the digital filter is stable**. ✓

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Definition of BLT:**
The Bilinear Transformation is a conformal mapping from the analog $s$-plane to the digital $z$-plane defined by the substitution:

$$s = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{z - 1}{z + 1}$$

**Step 2 — Derivation of frequency warping:**
Setting $s = j\Omega$ and $z = e^{j\omega}$:

$$j\Omega = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{e^{j\omega} - 1}{e^{j\omega} + 1}$$

Using Euler's identity:

$$j\Omega = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{2j \sin(\omega/2)}{2 \cos(\omega/2)} = j \cdot \frac{2}{T} \tan\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$$

Equating imaginary parts:

$$\Omega = \frac{2}{T} \tan\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Pre-warping concept:**
Because of the non-linear tangent relation, critical frequencies must be *pre-warped* *before* designing the analog prototype. The pre-warping formula is:

$$\Omega_{critical} = \frac{2}{T} \tan\left(\frac{\omega_{critical} T}{2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Illustrative example:**
Suppose a digital low-pass filter must have $\omega_p = 0.4\pi$ rad with $T = 1$ sec.

Pre-warped analog frequency: $\Omega_p = 2 \tan(0.2\pi) = 2 \tan(0.6283) = 2 \times 0.7265 = 1.4531$ rad/sec.

The analog prototype is then designed with cutoff $\Omega_p = 1.4531$ rad/sec, *not* with $\omega_p = 0.4\pi$ directly. After applying BLT, the resulting $H(z)$ will have its -3 dB point *exactly* at $0.4\pi$ rad, as required.

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023]:**
>
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Design a digital low-pass Butterworth filter using the Bilinear Transformation with the following specifications: Passband edge $\omega_p = 0.25\pi$ rad, Sampling period $T = 0.5$ sec, filter order $N = 2$. Determine $H(z)$.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the Impulse Invariant Method and the Bilinear Transformation in terms of aliasing, frequency warping, and applicability to high-pass filter design.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Step 1:** Pre-warp the passband edge frequency:

$$\Omega_p = \frac{2}{T} \tan\left(\frac{\omega_p}{2}\right) = \frac{2}{0.5} \tan\left(\frac{0.25\pi}{2}\right) = 4 \tan(0.125\pi)$$

$$\Omega_p = 4 \tan(0.3927) = 4 \times 0.4142 = 1.6568 \text{ rad/sec}$$

**Step 2:** The 2nd-order normalized Butterworth low-pass prototype is:

$$H_{norm}(s) = \frac{1}{s^2 + 1.4142 s + 1}$$

**Step 3:** De-normalize using $s \rightarrow s/\Omega_p$:

$$H_a(s) = \frac{\Omega_p^2}{s^2 + 1.4142 \Omega_p s + \Omega_p^2}$$

Substituting $\Omega_p = 1.6568$:

- $\Omega_p^2 = 2.7450$
- $1.4142 \times \Omega_p = 2.3430$

$$H_a(s) = \frac{2.7450}{s^2 + 2.3430 s + 2.7450}$$

**Step 4:** Apply BLT substitution $s = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{z-1}{z+1} = 4 \cdot \frac{z-1}{z+1}$:

$$s^2 = 16 \cdot \frac{(z-1)^2}{(z+1)^2}, \quad s = 4 \cdot \frac{z-1}{z+1}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute into the denominator:

$$\text{Denom} = 16 \cdot \frac{(z-1)^2}{(z+1)^2} + 2.3430 \cdot 4 \cdot \frac{z-1}{z+1} + 2.7450$$

Multiply through by $(z+1)^2$:

$$16(z-1)^2 + 9.372(z-1)(z+1) + 2.7450(z+1)^2$$

**Step 6:** Expand each term:
- $16(z-1)^2 = 16(z^2 - 2z + 1) = 16z^2 - 32z + 16$
- $9.372(z^2 - 1) = 9.372 z^2 - 9.372$
- $2.7450(z+1)^2 = 2.7450(z^2 + 2z + 1) = 2.7450 z^2 + 5.490 z + 2.7450$

**Step 7:** Sum all terms:

$$(16 + 9.372 + 2.7450)z^2 + (-32 + 5.490)z + (16 - 9.372 + 2.7450)$$

$$= 28.117 z^2 - 26.510 z + 9.373$$

**Step 8:** Divide by $28.117$ to normalize the denominator to monic form in $z^2$:

$$z^2 - 0.9429 z + 0.3334$$

**Step 9:** Convert to negative powers of $z$ and compute numerator gain normalization. The final digital transfer function is:

$$H(z) = \frac{0.0976(1 + 2z^{-1} + z^{-2})}{1 - 0.9429 z^{-1} + 0.3334 z^{-2}}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

| **Comparison Criterion** | **Impulse Invariant Method** | **Bilinear Transformation** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Aliasing** | Aliasing is inherent due to sampling. Suitable only for band-limited analog filters. | No aliasing — entire $j\Omega$ axis maps one-to-one onto unit circle. |
| **Frequency Warping** | No warping; frequency relation is linear: $\omega = \Omega T$. | Non-linear warping: $\Omega = \frac{2}{T}\tan(\omega/2)$. Requires pre-warping. |
| **High-Pass Filter Design** | Not suitable — high-pass filters are not band-limited, leading to severe aliasing. | Highly suitable — high-pass characteristics map cleanly. |
| **Band-Pass / Band-Stop** | Limited use — aliasing distorts passband/stopband edges. | Excellent — all filter types map correctly. |
| **Time-Domain Fidelity** | Preserves impulse response shape exactly. | Impulse response shape is altered by the non-linear warping. |
| **Stability** | Preserved (LHP $\rightarrow$ unit disk interior). | Preserved (LHP $\rightarrow$ unit disk interior). |
| **Computational Effort** | Lower (only partial fraction expansion). | Moderate (full algebraic substitution). |
| **Phase Response** | Linear phase preserved in low-frequency region. | Phase becomes non-linear due to warping. |

**Conclusion:** For general-purpose digital filter design (LP, HP, BP, BS), **Bilinear Transformation is preferred**. The Impulse Invariant Method is reserved for specialized cases where the analog prototype is strictly band-limited and the time-domain pulse shape must be preserved exactly (e.g., matched filters, certain biomedical applications).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $T$ scaling factor in IIT:** The formula $H(z) = T \sum \frac{R_i}{1 - e^{p_iT}z^{-1}}$ includes a multiplier $T$. Students often drop this factor, losing **1 mark** for amplitude scaling.
> 2. **Omitting the pre-warping step in BLT:** A common error is to substitute $s = \frac{2}{T}\frac{z-1}{z+1}$ directly into the normalized analog filter *without* first pre-warping the critical frequencies. This produces a digital filter with a *wrong* cutoff, costing **2 to 3 marks**.
> 3. **Incorrect partial fraction expansion:** Sign errors in residue computation directly cascade into a wrong $H(z)$. Always verify by combining fractions and checking against the original.
> 4. **Stability verification missing:** Many students forget to verify that $|z_i| < 1$. Examiners specifically look for this — at least **1 mark** is reserved for it.
> 5. **Confusing the two mappings:** Writing $z = e^{sT}$ as the IIT formula is correct, but writing $s = e^{zT}$ (the inverse) as the *substitution rule* is a classic error worth **1 mark deduction**.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Impulse Invariant Method** uses the mapping $z = e^{sT}$, with the digital pole $z_i = e^{p_i T}$. The impulse response is preserved through sampling $h[n] = T \cdot h_a(nT)$.
- ✅ **Bilinear Transformation** uses the algebraic substitution $s = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{z-1}{z+1}$, providing a one-to-one, aliasing-free mapping.
- ✅ **Aliasing is the Achilles' heel of IIT** — restrict it to strictly band-limited analog prototypes.
- ✅ **Frequency warping is the price of BLT** — the tangent relation $\Omega = \frac{2}{T}\tan(\omega/2)$ compresses the entire analog frequency axis into the digital range $(-\pi, \pi)$.
- ✅ **Pre-warping is mandatory in BLT** for any critical frequency that must match a digital specification: $\Omega_{new} = \frac{2}{T}\tan(\omega_{old} T / 2)$.
- ✅ **Stability is preserved** by both methods — LHP in $s$-plane $\Rightarrow$ interior of unit circle in $z$-plane.
- ✅ **Filter order is preserved** by both methods — an $N$th-order analog filter becomes an $N$th-order digital filter.
- ✅ **For BLT, always multiply through by $(z+1)^N$** (where $N$ is the filter order) to clear denominators and obtain $H(z)$ in standard form.
- ✅ **Quick decision rule:** If you need high-pass, band-pass, or band-stop — use **BLT**. If you need exact pulse shape preservation with a guaranteed band-limited analog — use **IIT**.
- ✅ **Remember the inverse BLT:** $z = \frac{1 + sT/2}{1 - sT/2}$ for completeness in derivations.
- ✅ **KTU favorite trick question:** "Why is IIT not used for high-pass filters?" — Answer: because high-pass filters are inherently not band-limited, causing severe aliasing distortion of the magnitude response.
- ✅ **Numerical tip:** Always compute $e^{-aT}$ using a calculator for IIT, and $\tan(\cdot)$ for BLT pre-warping — examiners reward correct numerical substitution.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frequency Transformation in Analog Domain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT758 - DIGITAL FILTER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Frequency Transformation in Analog Domain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frequency Transformation in the Analog Domain** is a systematic algebraic substitution technique used in IIR filter design where a normalized analog lowpass prototype transfer function $H_{LP}(s)$ is mathematically remapped to a different filter type (highpass, bandpass, or bandstop) by replacing the complex frequency variable $s$ with a rational function of $s$. This process preserves the prototype's magnitude response shape while shifting and rescaling its critical frequencies to the user-specified passband and stopband edges.

In the standard KTU 2024 Scheme design flow, the **Bilinear Transformation** method begins by designing a **normalized analog lowpass prototype** (cutoff $\Omega_c = 1$ rad/sec) using Butterworth, Chebyshev, or Elliptic approximations. **Frequency transformations** are then applied **before** the bilinear mapping $s \to \frac{2}{T} \frac{1-z^{-1}}{1+z^{-1}}$ to convert this prototype into the desired filter type.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3, PERAT758):** The four canonical analog-to-analog transformations are:
> 1. Lowpass $\rightarrow$ Lowpass (LP-LP)
> 2. Lowpass $\rightarrow$ Highpass (LP-HP)
> 3. Lowpass $\rightarrow$ Bandpass (LP-BP)
> 4. Lowpass $\rightarrow$ Bandstop / Band-reject (LP-BS)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

**Analogy: The Audio Equalizer Tuner**

Imagine a piano tuner who has perfected the tuning of **one single middle-C note** (the normalized prototype). Now, the tuner is asked to produce the *exact same harmonic quality* at a different pitch — say, a high A note. Instead of building a brand new tuning mechanism, the tuner simply **mathematically maps** the frequencies of the middle-C tuning pattern to the new A note's frequency range. The "shape" of the sound (its tonal character) is preserved, but the **location on the frequency spectrum is shifted**.

This is precisely what frequency transformation does:
- The **shape** of the magnitude response is preserved.
- The **cutoff / passband / stopband edges** are shifted to user-defined values $\Omega_p, \Omega_s, \Omega_u, \Omega_l$.
- The mathematical operation is a **substitution** of the variable $s$ with a new rational function of $s$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Metric — Notation Convention (KTU Standard):**
> - $\Omega$ = Analog frequency in **radians/second**.
> - $\Omega_p$ = Passband edge frequency.
> - $\Omega_s$ = Stopband edge frequency.
> - $\Omega_u$ = **Upper** passband edge (in BP/BS context) or new cutoff (in LP context).
> - $\Omega_l$ = **Lower** passband edge (in BP/BS context).
> - Prototype cutoff is normalized to **$\Omega_c = 1$ rad/sec**.

## 1.3 The Need for Frequency Transformation

Designing a separate analog prototype for every filter type (LP, HP, BP, BS) would require re-deriving the Butterworth/Chebyshev polynomial coefficients each time. Frequency transformation allows the designer to:
1. **Design once** — a single normalized lowpass prototype.
2. **Reuse the prototype** by simple algebraic substitution.
3. **Preserve approximation characteristics** — the ripple, roll-off, and stopband attenuation remain intact.

This is the **cornerstone of IIR filter design** as mandated in Module 3 of PERAT758.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Transformation Philosophy — Step-by-Step Logic

The fundamental principle behind analog frequency transformation can be broken down into four logical steps:

- **Step 1 — Prototype Design:** A normalized analog lowpass filter $H_{LP}(s)$ is designed with prototype cutoff $\Omega_c = 1$ rad/sec. The order $N$ is determined from the passband/stopband specifications.

- **Step 2 — Frequency Mapping Definition:** A mathematical mapping $\Omega_{proto} \rightarrow \Omega_{new}$ is defined. The corresponding algebraic substitution is $s \rightarrow f(s)$, where $f(s)$ is a rational function.

- **Step 3 — Algebraic Substitution:** Replace every occurrence of $s$ in $H_{LP}(s)$ with $f(s)$ to obtain the new filter transfer function $H_{new}(s) = H_{LP}(f(s))$.

- **Step 4 — Apply Bilinear Transformation:** Map the resulting analog filter $H_{new}(s)$ to the digital domain $H(z)$ using $s = \frac{2}{T}\frac{1-z^{-1}}{1+z^{-1}}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why substitute and not simply re-design?**
> The transformation guarantees that the **magnitude response shape is preserved exactly** — if the prototype is Chebyshev Type-I with 1 dB ripple, the transformed filter will also be Chebyshev Type-I with 1 dB ripple. Re-designing from scratch would re-evaluate the order $N$ and might yield a different topology.

## 2.2 The Four Canonical Transformations

### 2.2.1 Lowpass-to-Lowpass (LP-LP)

- **Frequency Mapping:** $\Omega_{proto} = \Omega / \Omega_u$
- **Substitution:** $s \rightarrow s / \Omega_u$
- **Effect:** The cutoff at $\Omega = 1$ in the prototype is shifted to $\Omega = \Omega_u$ in the new filter.

### 2.2.2 Lowpass-to-Highpass (LP-HP)

- **Frequency Mapping:** $\Omega_{proto} = -\Omega_u / \Omega$
- **Substitution:** $s \rightarrow \Omega_u / s$
- **Effect:** DC ($\Omega = 0$) in the prototype maps to $\Omega = \infty$ in the new filter, and $\Omega = \infty$ in the prototype maps to $\Omega = 0$. Thus, the passband is flipped from low to high frequencies.

### 2.2.3 Lowpass-to-Bandpass (LP-BP)

- **Frequency Mapping:** $\Omega_{proto} = \frac{\Omega^2 - \Omega_l \Omega_u}{\Omega(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}$
- **Substitution:** $s \rightarrow \frac{s^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u}{s(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}$
- **Effect:** A single cutoff at $\Omega = 1$ in the prototype expands into **two** passband edges $\Omega_l$ and $\Omega_u$. The order of the filter **doubles**.

### 2.2.4 Lowpass-to-Bandstop (LP-BS)

- **Frequency Mapping:** $\Omega_{proto} = \frac{\Omega(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}{\Omega^2 - \Omega_l \Omega_u}$
- **Substitution:** $s \rightarrow \frac{s(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}{s^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u}$
- **Effect:** A single cutoff at $\Omega = 1$ in the prototype creates **two** stopband edges $\Omega_l$ and $\Omega_u$. The filter passes all frequencies except the band $[\Omega_l, \Omega_u]$. Order of the filter **doubles**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Transformation** | **Substitution $s \rightarrow$** | **New Cutoff / Edges** | **Order Multiplier** | **Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| LP $\rightarrow$ LP | $s / \Omega_u$ | $\Omega_u$ | 1 (same) | Audio bass control |
| LP $\rightarrow$ HP | $\Omega_u / s$ | $\Omega_u$ | 1 (same) | Treble boost / DC blocker |
| LP $\rightarrow$ BP | $\dfrac{s^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u}{s(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}$ | $\Omega_l, \Omega_u$ | 2 (doubles) | Radio IF selection |
| LP $\rightarrow$ BS | $\dfrac{s(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}{s^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u}$ | $\Omega_l, \Omega_u$ | 2 (doubles) | Notch filter for 50 Hz hum |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Constants:**
> - **Prototype cutoff $\Omega_c$ is always = 1 rad/sec** in the normalized design.
> - **Bandwidth $B = \Omega_u - \Omega_l$** appears in both BP and BS substitutions.
> - **Center frequency** for BP/BS is given by $\Omega_0 = \sqrt{\Omega_l \Omega_u}$ (geometric mean).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications:** BP transformations are used to design IF (Intermediate Frequency) filters in superheterodyne receivers.
- **Biomedical Signal Processing:** BS transformations create notch filters to remove 50/60 Hz powerline interference from ECG/EEG signals.
- **Audio Engineering:** HP transformations design DC-blocking filters and high-frequency emphasis circuits.
- **Vibration Analysis:** BP transformations isolate resonant frequencies in mechanical structures.
- **Audio Crossovers:** LP and HP transformations together create 2-way speaker crossover networks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of LP-LP Transformation

**Starting Point:** The normalized prototype has magnitude response defined over $\Omega_{proto} \in [0, 1]$ in the passband.

**Goal:** Map the prototype's passband edge at $\Omega_{proto} = 1$ to a new edge at $\Omega = \Omega_u$.

**Step 1 — Define the frequency scaling relationship:**

We require a linear scaling such that $\Omega_{proto} = 1$ corresponds to $\Omega = \Omega_u$. Since the magnitude response is a function of $s = j\Omega$ and $s^2 = -\Omega^2$, the response depends on $\Omega^2$. Therefore, the scaling must also be in terms of $\Omega^2$:

$$\Omega_{proto}^2 = \frac{\Omega^2}{\Omega_u^2}$$

**Step 2 — Convert frequency relation to Laplace variable relation:**

Since $s_{proto} = j\Omega_{proto}$ and $s = j\Omega$, we can write $s_{proto} = s / \Omega_u$.

**Step 3 — Apply substitution to the prototype transfer function:**

$$H_{LP}(s_{proto}) = H_{LP}\left(\frac{s}{\Omega_u}\right) = H_{new}(s)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this works:** Replacing $s$ with $s / \Omega_u$ inside $H_{LP}$ is mathematically equivalent to evaluating $H_{LP}$ at $s/\Omega_u$, which corresponds to evaluating it at the scaled frequency $\Omega_{proto} = \Omega / \Omega_u$. The passband is therefore redefined.

**Example:** If $H_{LP}(s) = \frac{1}{s+1}$ (1st-order Butterworth), then:

$$H_{new}(s) = H_{LP}\left(\frac{s}{\Omega_u}\right) = \frac{1}{\frac{s}{\Omega_u}+1} = \frac{\Omega_u}{s + \Omega_u}$$

The new cutoff is at $\Omega = \Omega_u$ (verified by setting $s = j\Omega_u$ giving magnitude $= \frac{\Omega_u}{\sqrt{2}\Omega_u} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$).

## 3.2 Derivation of LP-HP Transformation

**Goal:** Map the prototype's passband (low frequencies) to the **high frequencies** of the new filter. Specifically, we need a frequency mapping that sends:
- $\Omega_{proto} = 0 \rightarrow \Omega = \infty$ (DC in prototype becomes high-freq in new)
- $\Omega_{proto} = \infty \rightarrow \Omega = 0$ (high-freq in prototype becomes DC in new)
- $\Omega_{proto} = 1 \rightarrow \Omega = \Omega_u$ (cutoff in prototype becomes cutoff in new)

**Step 1 — Propose the mapping:**

A transformation of the form $s_{proto} = K / s$ achieves the inversion property. To set the cutoff at $\Omega_u$:

$$\Omega_{proto} = \frac{\Omega_u}{\Omega}$$

**Step 2 — Verify the boundary conditions:**

- $\Omega \to 0$: $\Omega_{proto} \to \infty$ ✓
- $\Omega \to \infty$: $\Omega_{proto} \to 0$ ✓
- $\Omega = \Omega_u$: $\Omega_{proto} = 1$ ✓

**Step 3 — Convert to Laplace variable:**

$$s_{proto} = j\Omega_{proto} = j \frac{\Omega_u}{\Omega} = \frac{\Omega_u}{j\Omega} = \frac{\Omega_u}{s}$$

**Step 4 — Final substitution:**

$$\boxed{s \rightarrow \frac{\Omega_u}{s}}$$

**Example:** For the same prototype $H_{LP}(s) = \frac{1}{s+1}$:

$$H_{HP}(s) = \frac{1}{\frac{\Omega_u}{s}+1} = \frac{s}{s + \Omega_u}$$

This is a standard 1st-order highpass filter with cutoff at $\Omega_u$ ✓.

## 3.3 Derivation of LP-BP Transformation

**Goal:** Convert a single cutoff at $\Omega = 1$ into two passband edges $\Omega_l$ and $\Omega_u$. The transformation must be **order-doubling** because a bandpass filter requires twice as many poles.

**Step 1 — Define the desired mapping:**

We require a function that:
- Maps $\Omega = \sqrt{\Omega_l \Omega_u}$ (geometric center) to $\Omega_{proto} = 0$ (prototype's DC).
- Maps $\Omega = \Omega_l$ and $\Omega = \Omega_u$ to $\Omega_{proto} = 1$ (prototype's cutoff).
- Maps $\Omega = 0$ and $\Omega = \infty$ to $\Omega_{proto} = \infty$.

**Step 2 — Propose a bilinear-in-$\Omega^2$ mapping:**

A general bilinear mapping in $\Omega^2$ is:

$$\Omega_{proto} = \frac{a\Omega^2 + b}{c\Omega}$$

For this to map $\Omega_l$ and $\Omega_u$ both to 1, and the center to 0, the numerator must vanish at $\Omega^2 = \Omega_l \Omega_u$:

$$\Omega_{proto} = \frac{\Omega^2 - \Omega_l \Omega_u}{B \cdot \Omega}$$

where $B$ is the **bandwidth** to be determined.

**Step 3 — Solve for $B$ using the cutoff condition:**

Setting $\Omega = \Omega_u$ and requiring $\Omega_{proto} = 1$:

$$1 = \frac{\Omega_u^2 - \Omega_l \Omega_u}{B \cdot \Omega_u} = \frac{\Omega_u - \Omega_l}{B}$$

Therefore $B = \Omega_u - \Omega_l$.

**Step 4 — Convert to Laplace variable substitution:**

$$\Omega_{proto} = \frac{\Omega^2 - \Omega_l \Omega_u}{\Omega(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)} = \frac{-\Omega^2 - \Omega_l \Omega_u}{j\Omega(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)} \cdot \frac{-1}{1}$$

Using $s = j\Omega$, so $\Omega = s/j$ and $\Omega^2 = -s^2$:

$$s_{proto} = j\Omega_{proto} = j \cdot \frac{-(s^2) - \Omega_l \Omega_u}{j \cdot (s/j)(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}$$

$$s_{proto} = \frac{-(s^2) - \Omega_l \Omega_u}{(s/j)(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)} = \frac{-(s^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u)}{(s/j)(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}$$

$$\boxed{s \rightarrow \frac{s^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u}{s(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order Doubling Verification:** The substitution $s \rightarrow \frac{s^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u}{s(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}$ is a **rational function of degree 2/1**. When applied to an N-th order prototype, the resulting BP filter has order $2N$. This is fundamental and frequently tested in KTU exams.

**Example:** $H_{LP}(s) = \frac{1}{s+1}$ becomes:

$$H_{BP}(s) = \frac{1}{\frac{s^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u}{s(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)} + 1} = \frac{s(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}{s^2 + s(\Omega_u - \Omega_l) + \Omega_l \Omega_u}$$

This is a 2nd-order bandpass with center frequency $\Omega_0 = \sqrt{\Omega_l \Omega_u}$ and quality factor $Q = \frac{\sqrt{\Omega_l \Omega_u}}{\Omega_u - \Omega_l}$ ✓.

## 3.4 Derivation of LP-BS Transformation

**Goal:** Convert a single cutoff at $\Omega = 1$ into two **stopband** edges $\Omega_l$ and $\Omega_u$.

**Step 1 — Use the LP-BP relationship:** The bandstop transformation is the **frequency inverse** of the bandpass transformation. We can derive it by taking the reciprocal mapping:

$$\Omega_{proto} = \frac{\Omega(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}{\Omega^2 - \Omega_l \Omega_u}$$

**Step 2 — Verify the boundary conditions:**

- $\Omega = \sqrt{\Omega_l \Omega_u}$: $\Omega_{proto} = \infty$ (stopband center, prototype sees $\infty$) ✓
- $\Omega = \Omega_l$ or $\Omega_u$: $\Omega_{proto} = 1$ (stopband edges correspond to prototype cutoff) ✓
- $\Omega = 0$ or $\infty$: $\Omega_{proto} = 0$ (DC and high-freq are passed) ✓

**Step 3 — Convert to Laplace substitution:**

Using the same $s = j\Omega$ algebra:

$$\boxed{s \rightarrow \frac{s(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}{s^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u}}$$

**Example:** $H_{LP}(s) = \frac{1}{s+1}$ becomes:

$$H_{BS}(s) = \frac{1}{\frac{s(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}{s^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u} + 1} = \frac{s^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u}{s^2 + s(\Omega_u - \Omega_l) + \Omega_l \Omega_u}$$

The numerator is $s^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u$, which gives **two zeros** at $\pm j\sqrt{\Omega_l \Omega_u}$, confirming the bandstop behavior ✓.

## 3.5 Summary of Pole-Zero Behavior

| **Transformation** | **Pole at $s_0$ in Prototype** | **Resulting in New Filter** | **Zero Behavior** |
|---|---|---|---|
| LP-LP | $s_0 = \sigma_0 + j\Omega_0$ | Pole at $s_0 \cdot \Omega_u$ | No new zeros |
| LP-HP | $s_0$ | Poles at $\Omega_u / s_0$ | A zero at $s = 0$ is added per pole |
| LP-BP | $s_0$ | Two poles at roots of $s^2 - \Omega_u s_0 s + \Omega_l \Omega_u = 0$ | A zero at $s = 0$ is added per pole |
| LP-BS | $s_0$ | Two poles at roots of $s^2 s_0 - s(\Omega_u - \Omega_l) + \Omega_l \Omega_u s_0 = 0$ | Two zeros at $\pm j\sqrt{\Omega_l \Omega_u}$ per pole |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Complete IIR Filter Design Flow with Frequency Transformation

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the full design pipeline mandated by KTU Module 3, showing where analog frequency transformation fits into the overall IIR design methodology.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Digital Specifications] --> B[Step 2: Apply Pre-warping to find analog edges Ωp and Ωs]
    B --> C[Step 3: Determine filter order N from prototype formulas]
    C --> D[Step 4: Design Normalized Analog Lowpass Prototype H_LP s with cutoff = 1]
    D --> E{Step 5: Target Filter Type}
    E --> F1[LP-LP: s → s / Ωu]
    E --> F2[LP-HP: s → Ωu / s]
    E --> F3[LP-BP: s → s² + ΩlΩu over s·Bu]
    E --> F4[LP-BS: s → s·Bu over s² + ΩlΩu]
    F1 --> G[Step 6: Obtain H_target s]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Step 7: Apply Bilinear Transformation s = 2 over T times 1 minus zinv over 1 plus zinv]
    H --> I[Step 8: Realize H z via Direct Form I or II]
    I --> J[Final Digital Filter]
```

## 4.2 Effect of Each Transformation on the s-Plane Pole Distribution

The following block diagram conceptually maps how poles migrate on the s-plane under each transformation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P[Prototype s-plane: pole at s0]
        P1[Pole s0 = sigma0 plus j Omega0]
    end
    subgraph Q1[LP-LP Result]
        Q11[Pole shifts radially to Omegau times s0]
    end
    subgraph Q2[LP-HP Result]
        Q21[Pole inverts to Omegau over s0]
        Q22[New zero at origin added]
    end
    subgraph Q3[LP-BP Result]
        Q31[Two poles from quadratic in s]
        Q32[New zero at origin added]
    end
    subgraph Q4[LP-BS Result]
        Q41[Two poles from quadratic in s]
        Q42[Two zeros on j-omega axis at plus minus j sqrt Omegal Omegau]
    end
    P --> Q1
    P --> Q2
    P --> Q3
    P --> Q4
```

## 4.3 Frequency Response Shape Preservation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Prototype Magnitude Response: shape F of Omega] --> B{Transformation Type}
    B --> C1[LP-LP: F becomes F of Omega over Ωu]
    B --> C2[LP-HP: F becomes F of Ωu over Omega, axis flipped]
    B --> C3[LP-BP: F becomes F of Omega minus mapped, two passbands]
    B --> C4[LP-BS: F becomes F of mapped Omega, two stopbands]
    C1 --> D[Shape PRESERVED: same ripple, same rolloff rate]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E[Key property of frequency transformation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note:** The block diagrams above serve as a visual map of the conceptual flow. In a KTU examination, students are expected to draw the **magnitude response sketches** showing how the prototype's lowpass shape morphs into HP, BP, and BS shapes after transformation. Always label the cutoff frequencies $\Omega_u, \Omega_l$ and the prototype's $\Omega = 1$ on the sketches.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Short Answer `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define analog frequency transformation. List the four standard analog-to-analog transformations used in IIR filter design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — KTU valuation key pattern):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Analog frequency transformation is the process of converting a normalized lowpass analog prototype filter $H_{LP}(s)$ into another filter type (highpass, bandpass, or bandstop) by substituting the complex frequency variable $s$ with a rational function of $s$. This substitution shifts the cutoff/edge frequencies to user-specified values while preserving the magnitude response shape.

**Four Transformations (1.5 marks):**
1. Lowpass-to-Lowpass (LP-LP): $s \rightarrow s/\Omega_u$
2. Lowpass-to-Highpass (LP-HP): $s \rightarrow \Omega_u/s$
3. Lowpass-to-Bandpass (LP-BP): $s \rightarrow \frac{s^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u}{s(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}$
4. Lowpass-to-Bandstop (LP-BS): $s \rightarrow \frac{s(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}{s^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u}$

---

### Question 2: Short Answer `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Why does the lowpass-to-bandpass transformation double the order of the filter? Explain with reference to the substitution formula.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The LP-BP substitution is:

$$s \rightarrow \frac{s^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u}{s(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}$$

This is a **rational function of degree 2/1** in $s$ (numerator is quadratic, denominator is linear). When this substitution is applied to an N-th order prototype transfer function $H_{LP}(s) = \frac{P_N(s)}{Q_N(s)}$, the resulting transfer function has degree $2N$ in both numerator and denominator. Physically, a single cutoff frequency at $\Omega = 1$ in the prototype expands into **two** passband edges $\Omega_l$ and $\Omega_u$ in the new filter, requiring twice as many reactive components (poles) to define the new response shape. Thus, if the prototype is order $N = 3$, the resulting bandpass filter is of order **6**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1 (–2 marks):** Students often confuse the bandwidth expression. The correct bandwidth is $B = \Omega_u - \Omega_l$, **NOT** $\Omega_l - \Omega_u$ or $\sqrt{\Omega_u - \Omega_l}$. Sign errors here propagate through the entire derivation.
> - **Common Mistake #2 (–1 mark):** Forgetting that the prototype is **normalized** ($\Omega_c = 1$). Substituting $\Omega_c \neq 1$ in the formula leads to incorrect transformations.
> - **Common Mistake #3 (–2 marks):** In LP-BP, students often write $s \rightarrow \frac{s^2 - \Omega_l \Omega_u}{s B}$ with a **minus sign in the numerator**. The correct form is $s^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u$ in the numerator.
> - **Common Mistake #4 (–1 mark):** When asked for the digital filter, students sometimes apply the bilinear transform to $H_{LP}(s)$ directly without first performing the analog frequency transformation. **Always transform first in the analog domain, then apply the bilinear mapping.**

### Question A (14 Marks) — Module 3 Internal Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Derive the lowpass-to-highpass frequency transformation. State the substitution rule clearly and explain why it inverts the frequency axis. [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Required mapping (2 marks):** We need a transformation such that:
- $\Omega_{proto} = 0$ corresponds to $\Omega = \infty$
- $\Omega_{proto} = \infty$ corresponds to $\Omega = 0$
- $\Omega_{proto} = 1$ corresponds to $\Omega = \Omega_u$

**Step 2 — Propose the mapping (2 marks):** The transformation $\Omega_{proto} = \Omega_u / \Omega$ satisfies all three conditions. Verification:
- $\Omega \to 0$: $\Omega_{proto} \to \infty$ ✓
- $\Omega \to \infty$: $\Omega_{proto} \to 0$ ✓
- $\Omega = \Omega_u$: $\Omega_{proto} = 1$ ✓

**Step 3 — Convert to Laplace variable (2 marks):** Since $s_{proto} = j\Omega_{proto}$ and $s = j\Omega$:

$$s_{proto} = j \cdot \frac{\Omega_u}{\Omega} = \frac{\Omega_u}{j\Omega} = \frac{\Omega_u}{s}$$

**Step 4 — Final substitution rule and inversion explanation (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{s \rightarrow \frac{\Omega_u}{s}}$$

This substitution **inverts** the frequency axis because for every point $s_0$ in the prototype's s-plane, the new filter has a pole at $\Omega_u / s_0$. The DC point ($s = 0$) of the prototype, which is in the passband, maps to $\infty$ in the new filter (i.e., the high-frequency region), while the high-frequency region of the prototype maps to the low-frequency region of the new filter.

---

**(b) A normalized 1st-order Butterworth lowpass prototype is given by $H_{LP}(s) = \frac{1}{s+1}$. Using the lowpass-to-highpass transformation, derive the highpass filter transfer function with cutoff $\Omega_u = 100$ rad/sec. [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the substitution (1 mark):** The LP-HP transformation is:

$$s \rightarrow \frac{\Omega_u}{s} = \frac{100}{s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the substitution (3 marks):**

$$H_{HP}(s) = H_{LP}\left(\frac{100}{s}\right) = \frac{1}{\frac{100}{s} + 1}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify (2 marks):**

$$H_{HP}(s) = \frac{1}{\frac{100 + s}{s}} = \frac{s}{s + 100}$$

**Step 4 — Verification and interpretation (1 mark):** Setting $s = j\Omega$ and $\Omega = 100$:

$$\vert H_{HP}(j100) \vert = \frac{100}{\sqrt{100^2 + 100^2}} = \frac{100}{100\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

This confirms the cutoff is at $\Omega_u = 100$ rad/sec ✓. The filter has:
- A **zero at $s = 0$** (blocks DC).
- A **pole at $s = -100$** in the left half s-plane (stable).

$$\boxed{H_{HP}(s) = \frac{s}{s + 100}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Module 3 Internal Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Derive the lowpass-to-bandpass transformation formula. Show that the filter order doubles as a result. [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the design goal (1 mark):** The transformation should map:
- $\Omega = \sqrt{\Omega_l \Omega_u} \rightarrow \Omega_{proto} = 0$ (center to DC)
- $\Omega = \Omega_l$ and $\Omega = \Omega_u \rightarrow \Omega_{proto} = 1$ (edges to cutoff)
- $\Omega = 0$ and $\Omega = \infty \rightarrow \Omega_{proto} = \infty$

**Step 2 — Propose a bilinear-in-$\Omega^2$ mapping (2 marks):** A general form is:

$$\Omega_{proto} = \frac{\Omega^2 - \Omega_l \Omega_u}{B \cdot \Omega}$$

**Step 3 — Determine the bandwidth $B$ (2 marks):** Setting $\Omega = \Omega_u$ and requiring $\Omega_{proto} = 1$:

$$1 = \frac{\Omega_u^2 - \Omega_l \Omega_u}{B \cdot \Omega_u} = \frac{\Omega_u - \Omega_l}{B} \implies B = \Omega_u - \Omega_l$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Laplace substitution and explain order doubling (2 marks):** Using $s = j\Omega$:

$$s_{proto} = j \cdot \frac{\Omega^2 - \Omega_l \Omega_u}{(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)\Omega} = \frac{-\Omega^2 - \Omega_l \Omega_u}{j\Omega(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)/j} = \frac{s^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u}{s(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}$$

$$\boxed{s \rightarrow \frac{s^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u}{s(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}}$$

**Order Doubling Explanation:** This substitution is a rational function of degree **2 in $s$** in the numerator and degree **1 in $s$** in the denominator. When applied to an N-th order prototype $H_{LP}(s)$, the resulting numerator and denominator both become polynomials of degree $2N$. Physically, a single cutoff at $\Omega = 1$ in the prototype expands into two passband edges $\Omega_l$ and $\Omega_u$, requiring twice the number of poles.

---

**(b) A normalized 2nd-order Butterworth lowpass prototype is $H_{LP}(s) = \frac{1}{s^2 + \sqrt{2}s + 1}$. Apply the lowpass-to-bandpass transformation to design a bandpass filter with $\Omega_l = 1000$ rad/sec and $\Omega_u = 4000$ rad/sec. [7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the bandwidth (1 mark):**

$$B = \Omega_u - \Omega_l = 4000 - 1000 = 3000 \text{ rad/sec}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the center frequency (1 mark):**

$$\Omega_l \Omega_u = 1000 \times 4000 = 4 \times 10^6$$

$$\Omega_0 = \sqrt{\Omega_l \Omega_u} = 2000 \text{ rad/sec}$$

**Step 3 — State the substitution (1 mark):**

$$s \rightarrow \frac{s^2 + 4 \times 10^6}{3000 \cdot s}$$

**Step 4 — Apply substitution to denominator (3 marks):**

$$s^2 + \sqrt{2}s + 1 \rightarrow \left(\frac{s^2 + 4 \times 10^6}{3000 s}\right)^2 + \sqrt{2}\left(\frac{s^2 + 4 \times 10^6}{3000 s}\right) + 1$$

Let $x = \frac{s^2 + 4 \times 10^6}{3000 s}$. Then the denominator is $x^2 + \sqrt{2}x + 1$.

Multiplying through by $(3000 s)^2 = 9 \times 10^6 s^2$:

$$\text{Denom} = (s^2 + 4 \times 10^6)^2 + 3000\sqrt{2} \cdot s(s^2 + 4 \times 10^6) + 9 \times 10^6 s^2$$

**Step 5 — Final transfer function (1 mark):**

$$H_{BP}(s) = \frac{9 \times 10^6 s^2}{(s^2 + 4 \times 10^6)^2 + 3000\sqrt{2} s(s^2 + 4 \times 10^6) + 9 \times 10^6 s^2}$$

The resulting filter is of order **4** (2 × 2) as expected, with two passband edges at $\Omega_l = 1000$ and $\Omega_u = 4000$ rad/sec ✓.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ☐ **Definition:** Frequency transformation = substitution of $s$ in a normalized LP prototype to obtain another filter type.

- ☐ **Prototype is always normalized:** $\Omega_c = 1$ rad/sec. All transformations assume this.

- ☐ **LP-LP substitution:** $s \rightarrow s/\Omega_u$ — **same order**, cutoff shifts to $\Omega_u$.

- ☐ **LP-HP substitution:** $s \rightarrow \Omega_u/s$ — **same order**, **adds a zero at origin** per pole.

- ☐ **LP-BP substitution:** $s \rightarrow \frac{s^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u}{s(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}$ — **order doubles**, adds zero at origin.

- ☐ **LP-BS substitution:** $s \rightarrow \frac{s(\Omega_u - \Omega_l)}{s^2 + \Omega_l \Omega_u}$ — **order doubles**, adds two zeros on $j\Omega$ axis at $\pm j\sqrt{\Omega_l \Omega_u}$.

- ☐ **Bandwidth formula:** $B = \Omega_u - \Omega_l$ (positive, NOT absolute difference).

- ☐ **Center frequency:** $\Omega_0 = \sqrt{\Omega_l \Omega_u}$ — the geometric mean, used in BP/BS contexts.

- ☐ **Design flow order:** Digital specs → Pre-warp to analog → Find order N → Design LP prototype → **Apply analog frequency transformation** → Apply bilinear transform to get $H(z)$.

- ☐ **Shape preservation:** The ripple, roll-off rate, and stopband attenuation of the prototype are preserved exactly in the transformed filter.

- ☐ **Common pitfall:** Never apply the bilinear transform before the frequency transformation. The order is **always** frequency transformation **first**, then bilinear mapping.

- ☐ **Stability preservation:** All four transformations map the left-half s-plane to the left-half s-plane, ensuring stability is preserved.

- ☐ **Butterworth prototypes:** $H_{LP}(s) = \frac{1}{D_N(s)}$ where $D_N(s)$ has poles on the unit circle in the s-plane (for normalized design).

- ☐ **KTU exam weightage:** Frequency transformation typically carries 7–10 marks in ESE Module 3 and is often a sub-part of a 14-mark design question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of LP and HP Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT758 - DIGITAL FILTER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of LP and HP Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Digital Signal Processing (DSP), **filtering** refers to the selective modification of a signal's frequency spectrum. A **Digital Filter** is a discrete-time linear time-invariant (LTI) system described by the difference equation:

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x(n-k) - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y(n-k)$$

A **Low Pass (LP) Filter** passes signals with frequencies below a specified cutoff frequency $\omega_c$ and attenuates those above it. The magnitude response satisfies:
$$\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert \approx 1 \quad \text{for } \vert \omega \vert \le \omega_c$$
$$\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert \approx 0 \quad \text{for } \omega_c < \vert \omega \vert \le \pi$$

A **High Pass (HP) Filter** does the opposite — it passes frequencies **above** $\omega_c$ and stops those **below** it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** The module emphasizes the design of LP and HP digital filters using **(a) the Window method for FIR filters** and **(b) the Bilinear Transformation method for IIR filters**, including the analog Butterworth prototype approach and the LP-to-HP spectral frequency transformation.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture)

Imagine you are at a music concert holding a special **acoustic curtain**:
- An **LP Filter** curtain only allows **deep, slow bass vibrations** to pass through (think of the rumble of a kick drum) while blocking the sharp, high-pitched notes of a cymbal. It is like a "bass-only" curtain.
- An **HP Filter** curtain is the opposite — it lets the **crisp, sharp cymbal crashes** pass, but blocks the heavy bass notes. It is a "treble-only" curtain.

Mathematically, this curtain is a "frequency-selective window" applied to the spectrum of the signal. The job of a digital filter designer is to **mathematically build this curtain** to match a desired frequency response.

### 1.3 Design Approaches in KTU Syllabus

| Filter Type | Design Method | Key Tool |
|---|---|---|
| FIR LP / HP | Window Method (Time-Domain) | Rectangular, Hanning, Hamming, Blackman, Kaiser |
| IIR LP / HP | Frequency Transformation (Bilinear Transform) | Butterworth Prototype + Spectral Mapping |

> [!NOTE]
> **Passband (PB)** = frequencies passed; **Stopband (SB)** = frequencies blocked; **Transition Band (TB)** = the gap in between. A perfect brick-wall filter has zero transition band — but it is **non-causal** and cannot be realized in practice.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 FIR Filter Design Using the Window Method — Step-by-Step Logic

The window method converts an **ideal (infinite-duration) impulse response** into a **finite-duration causal filter** by truncating it with a window function $w(n)$.

**Step 1 — Start with the ideal frequency response:**

For an ideal **LP filter** with cutoff $\omega_c$:

$$H_d(e^{j\omega}) = \begin{cases} 1, & \vert \omega \vert \le \omega_c \\ 0, & \omega_c < \vert \omega \vert \le \pi \end{cases}$$

For an ideal **HP filter** with cutoff $\omega_c$:

$$H_d(e^{j\omega}) = \begin{cases} 0, & \vert \omega \vert < \omega_c \\ 1, & \omega_c \le \vert \omega \vert \le \pi \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Take the inverse DTFT to get the ideal impulse response $h_d(n)$:**

For the **LP** filter:

$$h_d(n) = \frac{\sin\bigl(\omega_c(n-\alpha)\bigr)}{\pi(n-\alpha)}, \quad \alpha = \frac{N-1}{2}$$

For the **HP** filter:

$$h_d(n) = \delta(n-\alpha) - \frac{\sin\bigl(\omega_c(n-\alpha)\bigr)}{\pi(n-\alpha)}$$

where $\delta(n-\alpha)$ is the shifted unit impulse.

**Step 3 — Multiply by a window $w(n)$ of length $N$:**

$$h(n) = h_d(n) \cdot w(n), \quad 0 \le n \le N-1$$

**Step 4 — Realize $h(n)$ as the filter coefficients.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the window?** The ideal $h_d(n)$ is **non-causal and infinite in length**. Multiplying by $w(n)$ in the time domain is equivalent to **convolving** $H_d(e^{j\omega})$ with $W(e^{j\omega})$ in the frequency domain. This smears the sharp cutoff into a realistic transition band and introduces ripples.

### 2.2 Standard Window Functions (KTU High-Yield)

| Window | Equation $w(n)$, $0 \le n \le N-1$ | Transition Width | Min. Stopband Attenuation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rectangular | $1$ | $0.89 \cdot \frac{2\pi}{N}$ | **−21 dB** |
| Hanning | $0.5 - 0.5\cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right)$ | $3.11 \cdot \frac{2\pi}{N}$ | **−44 dB** |
| Hamming | $0.54 - 0.46\cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right)$ | $3.32 \cdot \frac{2\pi}{N}$ | **−53 dB** |
| Blackman | $0.42 - 0.5\cos\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right) + 0.08\cos\left(\frac{4\pi n}{N-1}\right)$ | $5.56 \cdot \frac{2\pi}{N}$ | **−74 dB** |
| Kaiser | $\dfrac{I_0\!\left[\beta\sqrt{1-\bigl(\frac{2n}{N-1}-1\bigr)^2}\right]}{I_0(\beta)}$ | Depends on $\beta$ | Up to **−90 dB** |

> [!NOTE]
> **$I_0(\cdot)$** is the zeroth-order modified Bessel function of the first kind. The Kaiser window is a **parametric** window — by tuning $\beta$, you can trade off between main-lobe width and side-lobe level.

### 2.3 IIR Filter Design — Bilinear Transformation of Analog Prototypes

The **Bilinear Transform (BLT)** maps the $s$-plane (analog) to the $z$-plane (digital) using:

$$s = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$$

This compresses the entire analog frequency axis $\Omega \in (-\infty, \infty)$ into the unit circle $\omega \in (-\pi, \pi)$. To compensate for the frequency warping (non-linear compression), we use **pre-warping**:

$$\Omega = \frac{2}{T} \tan\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The critical pre-warping step is what makes BLT work for discrete-time design.** We choose the analog cutoff $\Omega_c$ to match the digital cutoff $\omega_c$ **exactly**, not by substituting directly. This guarantees the digital filter meets the spec at the edge frequency.

### 2.4 Butterworth Analog LP Prototype

The **$N$-th order Butterworth LP filter** has a magnitude-squared response:

$$\vert H_a(j\Omega) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{\Omega}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N}}$$

Properties:
- Maximally flat (no ripples) in both passband and stopband.
- At $\Omega = \Omega_c$, magnitude is $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = -3\,\mathbf{dB}$ (the −3 dB point).
- The order $N$ required for a given passband ripple $A_p$ and stopband attenuation $A_s$:

$$N \ge \frac{0.5 \log\!\left(\dfrac{10^{0.1 A_s} - 1}{10^{0.1 A_p} - 1}\right)}{\log\!\left(\dfrac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_p}\right)}$$

The poles of $H_a(s)$ are located on a circle of radius $\Omega_c$ in the $s$-plane, and only the left-half-plane (LHP) poles are selected for stability.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Use |
|---|---|---|
| FIR LP impulse | $h_d(n) = \dfrac{\sin\bigl(\omega_c(n-\alpha)\bigr)}{\pi(n-\alpha)}$, $\alpha = \frac{N-1}{2}$ | Ideal LP |
| FIR HP impulse | $h_d(n) = \delta(n-\alpha) - \dfrac{\sin\bigl(\omega_c(n-\alpha)\bigr)}{\pi(n-\alpha)}$ | Ideal HP |
| Kaiser parameter | $\beta = 0.1102(A_s - 8.7)$ for $A_s > 50$ dB | Window design |
| Bilinear transform | $s = \dfrac{2}{T}\cdot\dfrac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$ | IIR mapping |
| Pre-warping | $\Omega = \dfrac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{2}\right)$ | IIR frequency correction |
| Butterworth order | $N \ge \dfrac{0.5\log\!\left[\frac{10^{0.1A_s}-1}{10^{0.1A_p}-1}\right]}{\log(\Omega_s/\Omega_p)}$ | Order calculation |
| LP → HP transform | $s \longrightarrow \dfrac{\Omega_c \cdot \Omega'_c}{s}$ | Spectral inversion |
| Chebyshev I ripple | $A_p = 10\log_{10}\!\left[1 + \varepsilon^2\right]$ | Passband ripple |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** LP filters remove high-frequency noise (e.g., sensor data smoothing, audio de-essing). HP filters remove DC offsets and low-frequency drift (e.g., ECG baseline wander removal, audio DC blocking).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Ideal LP Impulse Response via Inverse DTFT

The DTFT of a rectangular frequency-domain window gives the **sinc function**. Starting from the ideal LP magnitude response:

$$H_d(e^{j\omega}) = \begin{cases} 1, & \vert \omega \vert \le \omega_c \\ 0, & \omega_c < \vert \omega \vert \le \pi \end{cases}$$

Apply the inverse DTFT integral:

$$h_d(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} H_d(e^{j\omega}) e^{j\omega n}\, d\omega$$

Since $H_d$ is even in $\omega$, split the integral:

$$h_d(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\omega_c}^{\omega_c} 1 \cdot e^{j\omega n}\, d\omega$$

Integrate:

$$h_d(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\left[\frac{e^{j\omega n}}{jn}\right]_{-\omega_c}^{\omega_c}$$

Evaluate the limits:

$$h_d(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi jn}\bigl[e^{j\omega_c n} - e^{-j\omega_c n}\bigr]$$

Apply Euler's identity $e^{j\theta} - e^{-j\theta} = 2j\sin\theta$:

$$h_d(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi jn} \cdot 2j\sin(\omega_c n) = \frac{\sin(\omega_c n)}{\pi n}$$

To make the filter **causal** (i.e., $h(n) = 0$ for $n < 0$), shift by $\alpha = \frac{N-1}{2}$:

$$h_d(n) = \frac{\sin\bigl(\omega_c(n - \alpha)\bigr)}{\pi(n - \alpha)}, \quad 0 \le n \le N-1$$

> [!NOTE]
> When $n = \alpha$, the expression is a $0/0$ form, evaluated by L'Hôpital's rule giving $h_d(\alpha) = \omega_c/\pi$. Always handle this center-tap value **separately** in coding or numerical evaluation.

### 3.2 Derivation: Ideal HP Impulse Response

The HP filter is constructed as:

$$H_{HP}(e^{j\omega}) = 1 - H_{LP}(e^{j\omega})$$

In the time domain, the spectrum of "1" corresponds to a unit impulse $\delta(n)$ shifted to the center tap $\alpha$:

$$h_{HP}(n) = \delta(n - \alpha) - h_{LP}(n)$$

Expanding:

$$h_{HP}(n) = \delta(n - \alpha) - \frac{\sin\bigl(\omega_c(n - \alpha)\bigr)}{\pi(n - \alpha)}$$

This is a direct **spectral subtraction**: an all-pass filter minus a low-pass filter leaves only the high frequencies.

### 3.3 Derivation: Bilinear Transformation Pre-Warping

Start with the BLT mapping $s = j\Omega$ and $z = e^{j\omega}$:

$$j\Omega = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega}}{1 + e^{-j\omega}}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $e^{j\omega/2}$:

$$j\Omega = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{e^{j\omega/2} - e^{-j\omega/2}}{e^{j\omega/2} + e^{-j\omega/2}} = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{2j\sin(\omega/2)}{2\cos(\omega/2)}$$

$$j\Omega = j \cdot \frac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$$

Equate imaginary parts:

$$\Omega = \frac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$$

This non-linear tangent relationship **warps** the analog frequencies. To make the digital filter meet the specification **exactly at** $\omega_c$:

$$\Omega_c = \frac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\omega_c}{2}\right)$$

Use $\Omega_c$ in your analog prototype design.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Design Choices Compared

| Criterion | FIR (Window) | IIR (Bilinear) |
|---|---|---|
| Stability | Always stable (all-zero, no poles) | Stable only if poles inside unit circle |
| Phase | **Linear phase** possible (symmetric $h(n)$) | **Non-linear phase** (severe distortion) |
| Computational cost | Higher (large $N$ for sharp cutoff) | Lower (small $N$ suffices) |
| Memory requirements | Large ($N$ taps) | Small (few $b_k$, $a_k$) |
| Noise sensitivity | Low | Higher (coefficient quantization) |
