## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points in KTU 2024 Examinations:**

1. **Confusing pole radius 'a' with actual pole location:** Some students write the poles as a·e^{jω} when they should be a^{1/N}·e^{jω_k}. For N=1 or 2, this matters.

2. **Forgetting stability condition:** Many students omit the explicit statement **0 ≤ a < 1**. This is a **mandatory 1-mark deduction** in KTU valuation.

3. **Mixing up notch vs. peak comb filters:** Notch comb → zeros on unit circle. Peak comb → zeros at origin, poles near unit circle. **Don't interchange them.**

4. **Skipping the 3-dB bandwidth derivation:** Examiners look for the **full expansion** using Taylor series. Half-mark derivation = 0 marks for that section.

5. **Unit errors:** Always convert ω (rad/sample) to f (Hz) using **f = ω·Fs/(2π)**. Forgetting Fs leads to wrong BW in Hz.

6. **Not drawing the pole-zero plot:** A 1-2 mark deduction if the diagram is missing. Always include the **unit circle** as a reference.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Comb Filter by Pole-Zero Placement**

- **Definition:** Periodic filter with notches/peaks at regular frequency intervals.

- **Canonical Transfer Function:** H(z) = (1 − z⁻ᴺ) / (1 − a·z⁻ᴺ).

- **Zero Locations:** z_k = e^{j2πk/N}, for k = 0, 1, ..., N−1 (on unit circle).

- **Pole Locations:** p_k = a·e^{j2πk/N}, for k = 0, 1, ..., N−1 (inside unit circle, radius a).

- **Notch Frequencies:** ω_k = 2πk/N rad/sample, or f_k = k·Fs/N Hz.

- **3-dB Bandwidth:** BW = 2(1 − a) / N rad/sample.

- **Stability Condition:** 0 ≤ a < 1 (strictly less than 1 for stability).

- **Magnitude Response:** \vert H(e^{jω}) \vert^{2} = [4sin²(ωN/2)] / [1 + a² − 2a·cos(ωN)].

- **Peak Response Value:** 2/(1+a) at midpoint between notches.

- **Notch Depth:** Perfectly zero at ω = ω_k.

- **Period of Response:** 2π/N (repeats in frequency).

- **Implementation:** Single delay line of length N, plus one feedback multiplier.

- **Applications:** Powerline hum removal, echo cancellation, periodic noise removal.

- **Effect of a:**
  - a = 0 → Pure FIR comb (no poles, wide nulls).
  - a → 1 → Sharp, ideal comb (narrow nulls, but marginally stable).

- **Design Procedure:**
  1. Identify fundamental interference frequency f₁.
  2. Compute N = Fs / f₁.
  3. Choose 'a' based on desired sharpness (typically 0.9 to 0.99).
  4. Write H(z) = (1 − z⁻ᴺ) / (1 − a·z⁻ᴺ).
  5. Verify magnitude response and check stability.

- **KTU Special Tip:** Always draw the **unit circle** in pole-zero plots. Mark both **zeros (○)** and **poles (×)** with their angle and radius.

- **Common Exam Format:** 7-mark sub-questions typically ask for design + 3-dB BW. 14-mark questions pair design with frequency response derivation.

---

*End of Notes — Design of Comb Filter by Pole-Zero Placement (Module 3)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FIR Filter Structures - Direct, Linear Phase and Lattice Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT758 - DIGITAL FILTER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** FIR Filter Structures - Direct, Linear Phase and Lattice Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Finite Impulse Response (FIR) Filter** is a discrete-time filter whose impulse response $h(n)$ is of finite duration, settling to exactly zero in a finite number of sample periods $M$. The structure of an FIR filter refers to the *signal-flow graph* (block diagram) used to physically realize the underlying convolution sum on hardware (DSP processor, FPGA, or ASIC).

For an FIR filter of order $M-1$ (length $M$):
$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} h(k)\, x(n-k)$$

The corresponding transfer function in the Z-domain is:
$$H(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} h(k)\, z^{-k}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Mandate (Module 4):** Students must be able to draw and analyze three families of FIR structures — **Direct Form** (I & II/Transposed), **Linear-Phase Structures** (exploiting coefficient symmetry), and the **Lattice Structure** (Schur / PARCOR formulation). Questions on *number of multipliers, adders, and delay elements* are routine 3-mark items in KTU ESE.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Echo-Hall Calculator**
> Imagine shouting into a long stone hall. You hear your voice repeated, but each echo is a *scaled, time-delayed* copy of your original sound: $0.7 \times (\text{your voice}) + 0.4 \times (\text{echo from 0.1 s ago}) + 0.2 \times (\text{echo from 0.2 s ago})$. An FIR filter does exactly this in the digital domain: it stores past input samples in a *tape-recorder of delays* ($z^{-1}$ blocks), multiplies each by a fixed weight ($h(0), h(1), h(2), \ldots$), and sums the result. The "Direct Form" is the most obvious way to wire this up; the "Linear-Phase Form" exploits that many filter designs have *symmetric weights* so you can *pair-and-add* before multiplying (saving hardware); the "Lattice Form" is like a fancy echo chamber where echoes pass through successive *partial-reflection mirrors* — each mirror reflects a portion based on a single number called a *reflection coefficient*.

## 1.3 Why Structure Matters

| Concern | Impact of Choice |
|---|---|
| **Multiplier Count** | Determines silicon area / cost on ASIC/FPGA |
| **Adder Count** | Affects latency and power |
| **Delay Elements ($z^{-1}$)** | Determines memory requirement |
| **Numerical Robustness** | Sensitivity to coefficient quantization differs across structures |
| **Modularity** | Lattice stages can be added/removed on-the-fly |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a length-$M$ FIR filter the **Direct Form** always needs exactly $M$ multipliers and $M-1$ adders. The **Linear-Phase Structure** can halve the multiplier count if the impulse response is symmetric. The **Lattice Structure** uses $M$ multipliers but offers superior numerical properties and modularity.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Direct Form Realizations

### 2.1.1 Direct Form-I FIR Structure

The Direct Form-I follows directly from the difference equation. It is implemented as a chain of **$M$ unit delays**, $M$ multipliers (coefficients $h(0), h(1), \ldots, h(M-1)$), and a summer.

- **Tapped delay line** holds past inputs $x(n), x(n-1), \ldots, x(n-M+1)$.
- Each tap is scaled by $h(k)$ and accumulated.
- This structure is canonical in the sense of *delay elements*, but for FIR the IIR direct form is more efficient.

**Operational Logic (Bullet Steps):**
1. Sample $x(n)$ arrives at the input.
2. It is shifted rightward through a chain of $z^{-1}$ blocks.
3. At each tap $k$, a multiplier produces $h(k)\cdot x(n-k)$.
4. All $M$ products enter a parallel adder.
5. The adder output is $y(n)$.

### 2.1.2 Direct Form-II / Transposed Direct Form

The Transposed form is obtained by *transposing* the Direct Form-I signal flow graph (reversing arrow directions and swapping nodes/summers). It is **canonically multiplier-and-delay efficient** and is preferred in most hardware implementations because the adders form a chain (less fan-out stress).

## 2.2 Linear-Phase FIR Filter Structures

A filter is **linear-phase** if its phase response $\angle H(e^{j\omega})$ is a *linear function of frequency* $\omega$ — i.e., all frequency components suffer the same time delay (no phase distortion). This requires the impulse response to satisfy a **symmetry constraint**:

$$h(n) = \pm\, h(M-1-n), \quad n = 0, 1, \ldots, M-1$$

The **four types of linear-phase FIR filters** (canonical KTU classification):

| Type | Length $M$ | Symmetry | Constraint on $H(z)$ | Suitable For |
|------|-----------|----------|----------------------|--------------|
| **Type I** | Odd | Symmetric ($+$) | None on $H(e^{j\omega})$ | LP, HP, BP, BS (most general) |
| **Type II** | Even | Symmetric ($+$) | $H(\pi) = 0$ | LP, BP, BS (cannot be HP) |
| **Type III** | Odd | Anti-symmetric ($-$) | $H(0) = 0$ | BP, BS (cannot be LP/HP) |
| **Type IV** | Even | Anti-symmetric ($-$) | $H(0) = H(\pi) = 0$ | BP, Hilbert transformer |

**Multiplier Reduction (why we exploit symmetry):**
- **Type I & II (Symmetric):** Pair $x(n-k)$ with $x(n-M+1+k)$ and add **before** multiplying → Multipliers needed $\approx M/2$.
- **Type III & IV (Anti-symmetric):** Pair and *subtract* before multiplying → same $M/2$ multiplier savings.

## 2.3 FIR Lattice Structure

The lattice structure is built from a cascade of identical stages. Each stage is parameterized by a single number $k_m$ called the **reflection coefficient** (or **PARCOR coefficient**), with $\vert k_m \vert < 1$ for stability.

For stage $m$ ($m = 1, 2, \ldots, M-1$):

$$\begin{aligned}
f_m(n) &= f_{m-1}(n) + k_m \, g_{m-1}(n-1) \\
g_m(n) &= k_m \, f_{m-1}(n) + g_{m-1}(n-1)
\end{aligned}$$

with initial conditions:
$$f_0(n) = g_0(n) = x(n)$$

The output is taken from the *top* of the last stage:
$$y(n) = f_{M-1}(n)$$

The reflection coefficients $\{k_1, k_2, \ldots, k_{M-1}\}$ are computed from the impulse response using the **Schur / Levinson-Durbin recursions**:

$$\begin{aligned}
k_m &= -\frac{g_{m-1}(0)}{f_{m-1}(0)}, \quad m = 1, 2, \ldots, M-1 \\
f_m(n) &= f_{m-1}(n) + k_m \, g_{m-1}(n-1), \quad n = 0, 1, \ldots, M-1-m \\
g_m(n) &= k_m \, f_{m-1}(n) + g_{m-1}(n-1), \quad n = 0, 1, \ldots, M-1-m
\end{aligned}$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **definitive formula sheet** you must memorize for KTU ESE Part A and Part B on FIR structures. Every entry has appeared in past board exams.

| Quantity | Direct Form | Linear-Phase (Sym) | Linear-Phase (Anti) | Lattice |
|----------|-------------|--------------------|----------------------|---------|
| Multipliers | $M$ | $\lfloor M/2 \rfloor + 1$ (Type I/III), $M/2$ (Type II/IV) | Same as symmetric | $2(M-1)$ |
| Adders | $M-1$ | $M-1$ + symmetry adders | Same | $2(M-1)$ |
| Delays ($z^{-1}$) | $M-1$ | $M-1$ | $M-1$ | $2(M-1)$ |
| Symmetry Condition | None | $h(n) = h(M-1-n)$ | $h(n) = -h(M-1-n)$ | None on $h$ |
| Output | $y(n) = \sum h(k)x(n-k)$ | Same equation | Same equation | $y(n) = f_{M-1}(n)$ |
| Critical Constraint | — | $\vert k_m \vert < 1$ for lattice; $H(\pi)=0$ for Type II | $H(0)=0$ for Type III; both zero for Type IV | $\vert k_m \vert < 1$ |

**Key Engineering Constants & Symbols:**
- **Order** of the filter: $N = M-1$.
- **Normalized frequency**: $\omega \in [0, \pi]$ rad/sample (with sample rate $f_s$, $\omega = 2\pi f / f_s$).
- **Group delay** of linear-phase FIR: $\tau = (M-1)/2$ samples.
- **PARCOR** = Partial Auto-Correlation — origin of the term $k_m$.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Direct Form**: Used in generic MATLAB `dfilt.dffir` implementations and inside general-purpose DSPs when the filter is short.
- **Linear-Phase Structure**: Mandatory in audio processing, biomedical signal filtering (ECG/EEG), and any application where **phase distortion must be eliminated** — e.g., music playback, pulse-shaping filters in modems, image processing kernels.
- **Lattice Structure**: Backbone of **adaptive filters** (LMS, RLS algorithms) and **speech codecs** (Linear Predictive Coding — LPC). Reflection coefficients directly model vocal-tract resonances, making lattice the *natural* representation for speech synthesis.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Direct Form FIR Structure from the Difference Equation

We start from the defining convolution sum for a length-$M$ FIR filter:

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} h(k)\, x(n-k)$$

**Step 1 — Expand the sum explicitly** so each delayed input appears as a separate term.

$$y(n) = h(0)\,x(n) + h(1)\,x(n-1) + h(2)\,x(n-2) + \cdots + h(M-1)\,x(n-M+1)$$

**Step 2 — Introduce the delay operator.** Define $z^{-1}$ as the unit delay, so $z^{-1}\{x(n)\} = x(n-1)$.

A chain of $M-1$ such delays applied to $x(n)$ produces the vector:
$$\bigl[x(n),\ x(n-1),\ x(n-2),\ \ldots,\ x(n-M+1)\bigr]$$

**Step 3 — Identify the multipliers.** Each of the $M$ terms in the sum is generated by multiplying one tap by its coefficient $h(k)$.

**Step 4 — Identify the summer.** A parallel $M$-input adder combines all $M$ products into $y(n)$.

$$\boxed{\; y(n) = \underbrace{h(0)x(n)}_{\text{1st tap}} + \underbrace{h(1)x(n-1)}_{\text{2nd tap}} + \cdots + \underbrace{h(M-1)x(n-M+1)}_{\text{M-th tap}}\;}$$

This is the **Direct Form-I FIR structure**. It uses:
- $M$ multipliers,
- $M-1$ two-input adders (or one $M$-input adder),
- $M-1$ unit delays.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Transposed Direct Form

The transposed form is obtained using the *signal-flow transposition theorem*: reverse every arrow, swap nodes and summers, and keep branch transmittances.

The resulting equations are:

$$\begin{aligned}
w_k(n+1) &= w_{k+1}(n) + h(k)\,x(n), \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, M-2 \\
y(n) &= h(M-1)\,x(n) + w_0(n)
\end{aligned}$$

Each delay element now holds a *partial sum* $w_k(n)$ rather than a sample $x(n-k)$. This structure:
- Reduces fan-out at the input (only one multiplier reads $x(n)$ at the top).
- Has a serial adder chain — lower critical-path delay in hardware.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Symmetric Linear-Phase Reduction

Assume $h(n) = h(M-1-n)$ (Type I or II). We pair the $k$-th term with the $(M-1-k)$-th term:

$$\begin{aligned}
y(n) &= \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} h(k)\,x(n-k) \\
     &= \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} h(M-1-k)\,x(n-k) \quad \text{(using symmetry)} \\
     &= \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} h(k)\,x(n-M+1+k)
\end{aligned}$$

**Averaging the two equivalent forms:**

$$y(n) = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{k=0}^{M-1} h(k)\Bigl[x(n-k) + x(n-M+1+k)\Bigr]$$

Now pair $x(n-k)$ with $x(n-M+1+k)$ for $k = 0, 1, \ldots, M/2 - 1$:

$$\boxed{\; y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{L-1} h(k)\,\bigl[x(n-k) + x(n-M+1+k)\bigr]\;}$$

where $L = M/2$ for even $M$ (Type II) and $L = (M-1)/2$ for odd $M$ (Type I), with an unpaired center term $h(L)\,x(n-L)$ in the latter.

**Number of multiplications saved:**

For $M = 7$ (Type I, symmetric): Without symmetry → **7 multipliers**. With symmetry → **4 multipliers** (3 pair-and-add + 1 center). Savings $\approx 43\%$.

## 3.4 Derivation of FIR Lattice Stage Equations

We start from a length-$M$ impulse response $h(0), h(1), \ldots, h(M-1)$ and define an auxiliary sequence $g(n) = h(M-1-n)$ (the time-reversed impulse response). The *cross-correlation* between $f$ and $g$ at stage $m$ must vanish for the structure to be realizable.

**Step 1 — Initial conditions at stage 0:**

$$f_0(n) = x(n), \qquad g_0(n) = x(n)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the first reflection coefficient** $k_1$ using the Schur algorithm:

$$k_m = -\frac{g_{m-1}(0)}{f_{m-1}(0)}$$

For $m = 1$:
$$k_1 = -\frac{g_0(0)}{f_0(0)} = -\frac{h(M-1)}{h(0)}$$

**Step 3 — Recurrence for stage $m$** ($m \geq 1$):

$$\begin{aligned}
f_m(n) &= f_{m-1}(n) + k_m\,g_{m-1}(n-1) \\
g_m(n) &= k_m\,f_{m-1}(n) + g_{m-1}(n-1)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Filter output** at the end of the cascade:

$$y(n) = f_{M-1}(n)$$

**Worked Numerical Example** — Length $M = 3$, $h = [1, 2, 1]$ (symmetric, Type I).

**Stage 0:**
$$f_0(0) = h(0) = 1, \qquad g_0(0) = h(2) = 1$$

**Compute $k_1$:**
$$k_1 = -\frac{g_0(0)}{f_0(0)} = -\frac{1}{1} = -1$$

**Compute Stage-1 sequences** for $n = 0, 1$:
$$\begin{aligned}
f_1(0) &= f_0(0) + k_1\,g_0(-1) = 1 + (-1)(0) = 1 \\
f_1(1) &= f_0(1) + k_1\,g_0(0)  = 2 + (-1)(1) = 1 \\
g_1(0) &= k_1\,f_0(0) + g_0(-1)   = -1 + 0 = -1 \\
g_1(1) &= k_1\,f_0(1) + g_0(0)    = -2 + 1 = -1
\end{aligned}$$

**Compute $k_2$:**
$$k_2 = -\frac{g_1(0)}{f_1(0)} = -\frac{-1}{1} = 1$$

**Output verification:** $y(n) = f_2(n) = f_1(n) + k_2\,g_1(n-1)$:
$$\begin{aligned}
y(0) &= f_1(0) + 1\cdot g_1(-1) = 1 + 0 = 1 \quad \checkmark \\
y(1) &= f_1(1) + 1\cdot g_1(0)  = 1 + (-1) = 0 \\
y(2) &= f_1(2) + 1\cdot g_1(1)  = 0 + (-1) = -1
\end{aligned}$$

(Note: this is for $x(n) = \delta(n)$; further iteration produces $y(3), y(4), \ldots = 0$, confirming finite impulse response.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification insight:** Lattice output $y(n) = f_{M-1}(n)$ recovers the original $h(n)$ when input is the impulse $\delta(n)$, but with the impulse *time-reversed* across stages. The lattice is a *reversible* transformation between coefficient domains.

## 3.5 Comparison Matrix for KTU Examinations

| Property | Direct Form | Linear-Phase | Lattice |
|----------|-------------|--------------|---------|
| **Coefficient domain** | $h(k)$ | $h(k)$ (with symmetry) | $k_m$ (reflection coeffs) |
| **Hardware cost (length 10)** | 10 mul, 9 add | 5 mul, 14 add | 18 mul, 18 add |
| **Sensitivity to quantization** | High | Medium | Low |
| **Ease of order update** | Hard | Hard | Easy (add a stage) |
| **Used in adaptive filters?** | No | No | Yes (LMS/RLS) |
| **Requires symmetry?** | No | **Yes** | No |
| **Output equation** | $y(n) = \sum h(k)x(n-k)$ | Paired-sum form | $y(n) = f_{M-1}(n)$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All diagrams use **purely alphanumeric node IDs** and **clean uppercase text labels** (no markdown inside the quotes) to guarantee Mermaid compilation.

## 4.1 Direct Form-I FIR Structure (Length M = 5)

```mermaid
graph LR
    xNode["X OF N"] --> dly1["ZINV1"]
    dly1 --> dly2["ZINV2"]
    dly2 --> dly3["ZINV3"]
    dly3 --> dly4["ZINV4"]
    xNode --> m0["H0 MULT"]
    dly1 --> m1["H1 MULT"]
    dly2 --> m2["H2 MULT"]
    dly3 --> m3["H3 MULT"]
    dly4 --> m4["H4 MULT"]
    m0 --> sumA["ADDER A"]
    m1 --> sumA
    m2 --> sumB["ADDER B"]
    m3 --> sumC["ADDER C"]
    m4 --> sumC
    sumA --> sumB
    sumB --> sumD["ADDER D"]
    sumC --> sumD
    sumD --> yNode["Y OF N"]
```

**Interpretation:** Input $x(n)$ enters the leftmost node, propagates through 4 unit delays ($z^{-1}$), and each tap is multiplied by its coefficient $h(k)$. The 5 products enter the summer tree, producing $y(n)$.

## 4.2 Transposed Direct Form FIR Structure (Length M = 4)

```mermaid
graph TD
    xNode["X OF N"] --> m0["H0 MULT"]
    xNode --> m1["H1 MULT"]
    xNode --> m2["H2 MULT"]
    xNode --> m3["H3 MULT"]
    m0 --> sum0["SUM0"]
    m1 --> sum1["SUM1"]
    m2 --> sum2["SUM2"]
    m3 --> yNode["Y OF N"]
    sum0 --> dlyA["ZINV A"]
    sum1 --> dlyB["ZINV B"]
    sum2 --> dlyC["ZINV C"]
    dlyA --> sum1
    dlyB --> sum2
    dlyC --> yNode
    yNode --> dlyD["ZINV D"]
    dlyD --> sum0
```

**Interpretation:** Each delay element now stores a *partial sum*, not an input sample. Multipliers all share the same input $x(n)$. Adders form a serial chain — critical path = 1 multiplier + 1 adder (low latency).

## 4.3 Symmetric Linear-Phase Structure (Type I, M = 7)

```mermaid
graph LR
    xNode["X OF N"] --> dly1["ZINV1"]
    dly1 --> dly2["ZINV2"]
    dly2 --> dly3["ZINV3"]
    dly3 --> dly4["ZINV4"]
    dly4 --> dly5["ZINV5"]
    dly5 --> dly6["ZINV6"]
    xNode --> addA["PAIR ADD A"]
    dly6 --> addA
    dly1 --> addB["PAIR ADD B"]
    dly5 --> addB
    dly2 --> addC["PAIR ADD C"]
    dly4 --> addC
    dly3 --> mC["H3 MULT CENTER"]
    addA --> m0["H0 MULT"]
    addB --> m1["H1 MULT"]
    addC --> m2["H2 MULT"]
    mC --> sumA["ADDER S1"]
    m0 --> sumA
    m1 --> sumB["ADDER S2"]
    m2 --> sumB
    sumA --> sumC["ADDER S3"]
    sumB --> sumC
    sumC --> yNode["Y OF N"]
```

**Interpretation:** Symmetric pairs $[x(n), x(n-6)]$, $[x(n-1), x(n-5)]$, $[x(n-2), x(n-4)]$ are *added first*; only **4 multipliers** ($h(0), h(1), h(2), h(3)$) are needed instead of 7. This is a **43% hardware saving**.

## 4.4 FIR Lattice Structure (Three Stages, M = 4)

```mermaid
graph TD
    inNode["X OF N"] --> stage1top["STAGE 1 TOP"]
    inNode --> stage1bot["STAGE 1 BOTTOM"]
    stage1top --> mul1a["K1 MULT TOP"]
    stage1bot --> mul1b["K1 MULT BOT"]
    stage1top --> add1a["ADD 1A"]
    mul1a --> add1a
    stage1top --> add1b["ADD 1B"]
    mul1b --> add1b
    add1a --> dly1["ZINV STAGE 1"]
    dly1 --> mul1b
    add1a --> stage2top["STAGE 2 TOP"]
    add1b --> stage2bot["STAGE 2 BOTTOM"]
    stage2top --> mul2a["K2 MULT TOP"]
    stage2bot --> mul2b["K2 MULT BOT"]
    stage2top --> add2a["ADD 2A"]
    mul2a --> add2a
    stage2top --> add2b["ADD 2B"]
    mul2b --> add2b
    add2a --> dly2["ZINV STAGE 2"]
    dly2 --> mul2b
    add2a --> stage3top["STAGE 3 TOP"]
    add3b["ADD 3B"] --> stage3bot["STAGE 3 BOT"]
    stage3top --> mul3a["K3 MULT TOP"]
    stage3bot --> mul3b["K3 MULT BOT"]
    stage3top --> add3a["ADD 3A"]
    mul3a --> add3a
    stage3top --> outNode["Y OF N"]
```

**Interpretation:** Each lattice stage consists of two multipliers ($k_m$), two adders, and one delay. The top output of the final stage is the filter output $y(n) = f_{M-1}(n)$. The bottom outputs $g_m(n)$ are unused for filtering but carry *prediction residuals* used in LPC speech coding.

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Module 4 Overview)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph DIRECT_FAMILY["DIRECT FORM FAMILY"]
        DF1["DIRECT FORM I TAPPED DELAY LINE"]
        DFT["DIRECT FORM TRANSPOSED"]
    end
    subgraph LINPHASE_FAMILY["LINEAR PHASE FAMILY"]
        TYPE1["TYPE I ODD SYMMETRIC"]
        TYPE2["TYPE II EVEN SYMMETRIC"]
        TYPE3["TYPE III ODD ANTISYM"]
        TYPE4["TYPE IV EVEN ANTISYM"]
    end
    subgraph LATTICE_FAMILY["LATTICE FAMILY"]
        LATSCH["SCHUR ALGORITHM"]
        LATLEV["LEVINSON DURBIN"]
        LATOUT["F AND G SEQUENCES"]
    end
    DF1 -->|INVERTS ARROWS| DFT
    TYPE1 -->|EXPLOITS SYMMETRY| TYPE2
    TYPE3 -->|EXPLOITS ANTISYM| TYPE4
    LATSCH -->|COMPUTES REFLECTION K| LATLEV
    LATLEV -->|DRIVES STAGE UPDATE| LATOUT
    DFT -.->|REDUCES MULTIPLIERS| TYPE1
    TYPE1 -.->|USED FOR ADAPTIVE FILTERING| LATOUT
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. In linear-phase questions, students frequently **forget to specify the type** (I/II/III/IV) — this alone costs 2 marks.
> 2. In lattice derivations, omitting the **initial conditions** $f_0(n) = g_0(n) = x(n)$ is the #1 reason for losing marks in Part B.
> 3. Direct Form vs Direct Form-II confusion: **Direct Form-II only applies to IIR**; for FIR use **Direct Form-I** or **Transposed**.
> 4. Drawing lattice stages without clearly labeling the **reflection coefficient $k_m$ at each multiplier** is treated as incomplete by KTU examiners.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** State any three advantages of the FIR lattice structure over the direct form structure.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Modular structure:** Each stage is characterized by a single reflection coefficient $k_m$. Filter order can be increased or decreased by simply adding or removing stages without redesigning the entire filter — useful in adaptive and variable-order systems.

2. **Superior numerical robustness:** The lattice structure is less sensitive to coefficient quantization errors compared to direct form. The stability condition $\vert k_m \vert < 1$ provides an *automatic* and *localized* stability check at every stage.

3. **Natural fit for adaptive algorithms:** Lattice stages are used directly in LMS and RLS adaptive filters because the orthogonal forward/backward error sequences $f_m(n)$ and $g_m(n)$ can be updated independently per stage, giving faster convergence.

4. *(Bonus 1 mark, optional)* The lattice coefficients $k_m$ have a direct physical interpretation as **PARCOR** values, making them ideal for speech-coding applications (LPC vocoders).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Differentiate between Type-I and Type-II linear-phase FIR filters.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Type I | Type II |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Filter length $M$ | Odd | Even |
| Symmetry | $h(n) = h(M-1-n)$ | $h(n) = h(M-1-n)$ |
| Frequency response at $\omega = 0$ | Unconstrained | Unconstrained |
| Frequency response at $\omega = \pi$ | Unconstrained | **Forced to zero** ($H(\pi) = 0$) |
| Suitable filter types | LP, HP, BP, BS | LP, BP, BS only (not HP) |
| Multipliers needed | $(M+1)/2$ | $M/2$ |

The **Type-II** filter is unsuitable for highpass design because its frequency response must vanish at $\omega = \pi$, which corresponds to the highest normalized frequency in a digital system. Type-I has no such restriction and is the most general symmetric FIR filter.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the direct form and linear-phase FIR filter structures with neat block diagrams. Compare the number of multipliers required for a length-7 filter in both structures. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the lattice stage equations for an FIR filter and explain how the reflection coefficients are computed using the Schur algorithm. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q.A (a)

**Direct Form FIR Structure:**

The Direct Form-I structure implements the difference equation:
$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} h(k)\,x(n-k)$$

It consists of a tapped delay line of $M-1$ unit delays ($z^{-1}$ blocks). Each tap is multiplied by the corresponding impulse response coefficient $h(k)$, and all $M$ products are summed to produce $y(n)$.

**Block Diagram (refer Section 4.1):**
- Input $x(n)$ enters a delay chain producing $x(n-1), x(n-2), \ldots, x(n-M+1)$.
- $M$ multipliers scale each tap by $h(0), h(1), \ldots, h(M-1)$.
- A summer tree adds all $M$ products.

**Linear-Phase FIR Structure:**

When $h(n) = h(M-1-n)$ (symmetric), the structure exploits symmetry by adding mirror-image taps **before** multiplication. For length $M = 7$ (Type I):
$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{3} h(k)\,\bigl[x(n-k) + x(n-6+k)\bigr]$$

This requires only **4 multipliers** instead of 7.

**Multiplier Comparison Table for $M = 7$:**

| Structure | Multipliers Required | Savings |
|-----------|---------------------|---------|
| Direct Form | 7 | — |
| Linear-Phase (Type I) | 4 | $3$ ($\approx 43\%$) |

**Conclusion:** Linear-phase structures provide a hardware saving of nearly 50% for symmetric FIR filters, at the cost of extra adders (3 adders for pairing in this example).

---

#### Solution to Q.A (b)

**Derivation of Lattice Stage Equations:**

Define two sequences at each stage $m$:
- $f_m(n)$ — forward prediction error.
- $g_m(n)$ — backward prediction error.

Starting from initial conditions $f_0(n) = g_0(n) = x(n)$, we seek a recursive update that preserves the *order-recursive* property.

**Step 1:** Apply the Schur algorithm. The first reflection coefficient is chosen to cancel the zero-th lag of the backward sequence:
$$k_1 = -\frac{g_0(0)}{f_0(0)}$$

**Step 2:** Define the stage-$m$ recursions:
$$f_m(n) = f_{m-1}(n) + k_m\,g_{m-1}(n-1)$$
$$g_m(n) = k_m\,f_{m-1}(n) + g_{m-1}(n-1)$$

**Step 3:** After $M-1$ stages the output is read from the top:
$$y(n) = f_{M-1}(n)$$

**Schur Algorithm for Reflection Coefficients:**

For $m = 1, 2, \ldots, M-1$:
$$k_m = -\frac{g_{m-1}(0)}{f_{m-1}(0)}$$

followed by the recursions:
$$f_m(n) = f_{m-1}(n) + k_m\,g_{m-1}(n-1)$$
$$g_m(n) = k_m\,f_{m-1}(n) + g_{m-1}(n-1)$$

for $n = 0, 1, \ldots, M-1-m$.

**Stability:** The lattice is stable if and only if $\vert k_m \vert < 1$ for all stages — this is the **lattice stability test**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the transposed direct form FIR structure and list its advantages over the standard direct form. For $M = 6$, compute the number of multipliers, adders, and delays used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the four types of linear-phase FIR filters. For each type, state the condition on $H(e^{j\omega})$ and indicate whether it can be used as a lowpass filter. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q.B (a)

**Transposed Direct Form FIR Structure:**

The transposed form is derived by reversing every branch direction in the Direct Form-I signal-flow graph. The key structural change is that **delays now hold partial sums, not input samples**.

**Mathematical Description:**

For each stage $k = 0, 1, \ldots, M-2$:
$$w_k(n+1) = w_{k+1}(n) + h(k)\,x(n)$$

The output is:
$$y(n) = h(M-1)\,x(n) + w_0(n)$$

**Block Diagram (refer Section 4.2):**

**Advantages over Standard Direct Form:**

1. **Lower fan-out at the input:** All $M$ multipliers share the same input $x(n)$ directly, reducing loading on the input source.

2. **Shorter critical path:** Adders form a serial chain rather than a tree, so the propagation delay = 1 multiplier + 1 adder (instead of $\log_2 M$ adders for a tree).

3. **Better pipeline-ability:** Each delay element separates independent stages, making it easier to insert pipeline registers for high-speed hardware.

**Resource Count for $M = 6$:**

| Resource | Count |
|----------|-------|
| Multipliers | $6$ |
| Unit Delays ($z^{-1}$) | $5$ |
| Two-input Adders | $5$ |

---

#### Solution to Q.B (b)

**The Four Types of Linear-Phase FIR Filters:**

**Type I — Odd Length, Symmetric**
- $M$ odd; $h(n) = h(M-1-n)$.
- $H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega(M-1)/2}\Bigl[A(\omega)\Bigr]$, where $A(\omega)$ is a real, even function of $\omega$.
- $H(0)$ and $H(\pi)$ unconstrained.
- **Can be used as lowpass: YES** ✓

**Type II — Even Length, Symmetric**
- $M$ even; $h(n) = h(M-1-n)$.
- $H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega(M-1)/2}\Bigl[A(\omega)\Bigr]$, with $A(\pi) = 0$.
- Forced zero at $\omega = \pi$ (Nyquist frequency).
- **Can be used as lowpass: YES** ✓
- **Can be used as highpass: NO** ✗ (because $H(\pi) = 0$ kills the highpass response).

**Type III — Odd Length, Anti-symmetric**
- $M$ odd; $h(n) = -h(M-1-n)$.
- $H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega(M-1)/2}\,e^{j\pi/2}\,B(\omega)$, with $B(0) = 0$.
- Forced zero at $\omega = 0$ (DC).
- **Can be used as lowpass: NO** ✗
- Suitable for bandpass and Hilbert transformers.

**Type IV — Even Length, Anti-symmetric**
- $M$ even; $h(n) = -h(M-1-n)$.
- $H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega(M-1)/2}\,e^{j\pi/2}\,B(\omega)$, with $B(0) = B(\pi) = 0$.
- Forced zeros at both DC and Nyquist.
- **Can be used as lowpass: NO** ✗
- Used in Hilbert transformers and differentiators.

**Summary Table (high-yield for KTU):**

| Type | Length | Symmetry | $H(0)$ | $H(\pi)$ | LP usable? |
|------|--------|----------|--------|----------|------------|
| I | Odd | Symmetric | Free | Free | ✓ |
| II | Even | Symmetric | Free | 0 | ✓ |
| III | Odd | Anti-sym | 0 | Free | ✗ |
| IV | Even | Anti-sym | 0 | 0 | ✗ |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam**

- [ ] **FIR Length $M$** = number of coefficients; **Filter Order** = $M - 1$.
- [ ] **Direct Form-I**: $M$ multipliers, $M-1$ delays, $M-1$ adders. Most straightforward realization.
- [ ] **Transposed Direct Form**: Same count, but adders in series and delays hold partial sums → lower critical-path delay.
- [ ] **Linear-Phase Condition**: $h(n) = \pm h(M-1-n)$.
- [ ] **Four Types**: Type I (odd-sym), II (even-sym), III (odd-antisym), IV (even-antisym). Always state the type first in KTU answers.
- [ ] **Zero-Response Rules**: Type II → $H(\pi)=0$; Type III → $H(0)=0$; Type IV → both zero.
- [ ] **Symmetric Multiplier Saving**: $M$ coefficients → only $\lceil M/2 \rceil$ multipliers needed.
- [ ] **Lattice Stage Equations** (must memorize verbatim):
  - $f_m(n) = f_{m-1}(n) + k_m g_{m-1}(n-1)$
  - $g_m(n) = k_m f_{m-1}(n) + g_{m-1}(n-1)$
- [ ] **Lattice Initial Conditions**: $f_0(n) = g_0(n) = x(n)$.
- [ ] **Lattice Output**: $y(n) = f_{M-1}(n)$.
- [ ] **Reflection Coefficient Formula**: $k_m = -g_{m-1}(0)/f_{m-1}(0)$.
- [ ] **Stability**: $\vert k_m \vert < 1$ for all $m = 1, \ldots, M-1$.
- [ ] **Group Delay** of linear-phase FIR: $\tau = (M-1)/2$ samples.
- [ ] **Lattice Advantages** (state at least three): modularity, numerical robustness, adaptive-filter compatibility, order flexibility.
- [ ] **Direct Form-II does NOT exist for FIR** — only for IIR. Don't confuse.
- [ ] **Lattice uses $2(M-1)$ multipliers**, more than direct form — but the trade-off is *orthogonality of stages* and *modular update*.

---

**End of Module 4 Notes — FIR Filter Structures.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IIR Filter Structures - Direct, Cascade, Parallel and Lattice Structures
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT758 - DIGITAL FILTER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** IIR Filter Structures - Direct, Cascade, Parallel and Lattice Structures

# IIR Filter Structures – Direct, Cascade, Parallel and Lattice

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **digital filter structure** (also called a *realization* or *block diagram realization*) is the graphical/algorithmic representation of the input–output relationship of a discrete-time LTI system using the basic building blocks: **adders (Σ), multipliers (×), and unit delays (z⁻¹)**.

For an IIR (Infinite Impulse Response) filter, the general **Nth-order linear constant-coefficient difference equation (LCCDE)** is:

$$y(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, y(n-k) + \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x(n-k)$$

This translates in the z-domain to the rational transfer function:

$$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{-k}}{\displaystyle 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, z^{-k}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A given transfer function $H(z)$ is **not unique** — it can be realized using multiple equivalent structures. KTU examinations frequently test the student's ability to **derive one structure from another** and to **analyze the computational complexity** (multipliers, adders, delays) of each realization.

### 1.2 The Four Canonical Realizations

| # | Structure | Salient Feature |
|---|-----------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Direct Form-I** | Direct mapping of difference equation; two separate delay lines |
| 2 | **Direct Form-II (Canonical)** | Minimum number of delays = max(N, M); also called *Canonic Form* |
| 3 | **Cascade Form** | H(z) factored into 2nd-order sections; robust to coefficient quantization |
| 4 | **Parallel Form** | Partial-fraction expansion; output is the sum of independent branches |
| 5 | **Lattice-Ladder Form** | Modular, recurrence-based; used for IIR all-pass and linear-prediction filters |

> [!NOTE]
> **Canonic Realization:** A structure is called *canonic* if the number of delay elements equals the order of the filter. Direct Form-II is canonic; Direct Form-I is **not** canonic because it uses N + M delays.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Recipe" Viewpoint

Imagine you want to bake a cake (**filter output**):

- The **Direct Form** is like a single huge recipe written on one page — straightforward, but if you make a typo in line 1, the whole cake fails.
- The **Cascade Form** is like breaking the recipe into independent sub-recipes (make frosting, make sponge, then assemble) — each sub-recipe is small and easy to verify.
- The **Parallel Form** is like preparing multiple dishes simultaneously and putting them on the same plate at the end — independent, parallel processing.
- The **Lattice Form** is like a layered Russian doll — each layer is built from the previous one using a fixed reflection rule, making it extremely modular and predictable.

### 1.4 Why Study Multiple Structures? — Engineering Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **VLSI / FPGA implementation**, the choice of structure drastically affects:
> 1. **Finite-Precision Behaviour** — sensitivity to coefficient rounding (Cascade & Lattice are robust; Direct is sensitive).
> 2. **Overflow Probability** — internal signal scaling differs per structure.
> 3. **Hardware Cost** — number of multipliers/adders/delays.
> 4. **Parallelism & Pipelining** — Lattice structures are highly modular and pipeline-friendly.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A common student mistake is assuming all structures produce *numerically identical* outputs. They are mathematically equivalent in **infinite precision**, but in **fixed-point arithmetic** they produce *different* outputs due to internal overflow and round-off noise. This is a frequent 7-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Direct Form-I Structure

The Direct Form-I is obtained by implementing the two summations of the LCCDE independently:

- **Feed-forward (non-recursive) part:** computes $\sum b_k x(n-k)$
- **Feedback (recursive) part:** computes $-\sum a_k y(n-k)$

Both parts use their **own delay line**, so the total number of delays = **N + M**.

**Derivation Logic:**
- Multiply the numerator polynomial with $X(z)$ → $V(z) = \left(\sum b_k z^{-k}\right) X(z)$
- Then pass $V(z)$ through the all-pole section $\frac{1}{1+\sum a_k z^{-k}}$

$$H(z) = \underbrace{\left(\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}\right)}_{\text{Feed-forward}} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{1}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}}_{\text{Feedback}}$$

### 2.2 Direct Form-II (Canonic) Structure

Direct Form-II is obtained by **swapping the order** of the two blocks (LTI systems commute) and then exploiting the fact that the **middle signal is the same** — so the two delay lines can be **merged into one**.

$$H(z) = \frac{1}{1+\sum a_k z^{-k}} \cdot \left(\sum b_k z^{-k}\right)$$

Define the intermediate signal $w(n)$:

$$w(n) = x(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, w(n-k)$$

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, w(n-k)$$

This uses **only N delays** (assuming N ≥ M, otherwise M delays) — hence **canonic**.

### 2.3 Transposed Direct Forms (Transpose Theorem)

Apply the **Transposition Theorem** to Direct Form-I or II:
1. Reverse the direction of all signal-flow arrows.
2. Replace adders with take-off nodes (branch points) and vice versa.
3. Swap input ↔ output.

The transposed structures have the **same transfer function and same complexity**, but **different internal noise behaviour** — useful for fixed-point design.

### 2.4 Cascade (Sectioned) Form

Factor both numerator and denominator into **2nd-order (biquad) sections**:

$$H(z) = b_0 \prod_{k=1}^{K} H_k(z), \quad H_k(z) = \frac{b_{0k} + b_{1k} z^{-1} + b_{2k} z^{-2}}{1 + a_{1k} z^{-1} + a_{2k} z^{-2}}$$

where $K = \lceil \max(M,N)/2 \rceil$ (poles/zeros can be paired arbitrarily to minimise round-off noise — **optimal pairing** is a frequent KTU question).

> [!NOTE]
> **Pairing Rule (KTU):** Pair a pole with the geometrically *closest* zero. This minimizes the peak of the noise gain $\frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \vert G_k(e^{j\omega})\vert^2 \, d\omega$ per section.

### 2.5 Parallel Form

Perform **partial-fraction expansion (PFE)** of $H(z)$:

$$H(z) = C + \sum_{k=1}^{K} H_k(z), \quad H_k(z) = \frac{B_{0k} + B_{1k} z^{-1}}{1 + a_{1k} z^{-1} + a_{2k} z^{-2}}$$

Each 2nd-order section is computed in **parallel** and summed at the final output node. The constant term $C$ corresponds to the direct (FIR) path if $M \geq N$.

### 2.6 Lattice-Ladder IIR Structure (All-Pole / Pole-Zero)

The lattice structure is built using a **stage-by-stage recursion**. For an Nth-order all-pole IIR filter, the recursion proceeds *backwards* (from stage N down to 1):

$$k_m = \text{reflection (PARCOR) coefficients}, \quad \vert k_m \vert < 1 \text{ for stability}$$

**Step-Down Recursion (forward pass — input side):**

$$f_m(n) = f_{m-1}(n) + k_m \, g_{m-1}(n-1)$$

$$g_m(n) = k_m \, f_{m-1}(n) + g_{m-1}(n-1)$$

with initial conditions $f_0(n) = g_0(n) = x(n)$.

**Step-Up Recursion (reverse pass — for a general IIR with zeros):**

$$g_{m-1}(n) = g_m(n) - k_m \, f_{m-1}(n)$$

$$f_{m-1}(n-1) = \frac{f_m(n) - k_m \, g_m(n)}{1 - k_m^2}$$

For a **general pole-zero IIR**, the lattice is combined with a **ladder** (a weighted sum at the output):

$$y(n) = \sum_{m=0}^{N} v_m \, f_m(n) - \sum_{m=1}^{N} \mu_m \, g_m(n)$$

where $v_m$ and $\mu_m$ are the **ladder coefficients**, derivable from the polynomial coefficients using the **inverse Schur algorithm**.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Engineering Use |
|---|--------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $H(z) = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}$ | General IIR transfer function |
| 2 | LCCDE: $y(n) = -\sum a_k y(n-k) + \sum b_k x(n-k)$ | Time-domain implementation |
| 3 | Direct Form-I delays = N + M | Hardware cost (non-canonic) |
| 4 | Direct Form-II delays = max(N, M) | Hardware cost (canonic) |
| 5 | Cascade sections: $H(z) = b_0 \prod H_k(z)$ | Robust against quantization |
| 6 | Parallel expansion: $H(z) = C + \sum H_k(z)$ | Parallel hardware implementation |
| 7 | Biquad section: $H_k(z) = \frac{b_{0k} + b_{1k}z^{-1} + b_{2k}z^{-2}}{1 + a_{1k}z^{-1} + a_{2k}z^{-2}}$ | Building block of cascade / parallel |
| 8 | Lattice recursion: $f_m(n) = f_{m-1}(n) + k_m g_{m-1}(n-1)$ | Lattice stage forward recursion |
| 9 | Lattice recursion: $g_m(n) = k_m f_{m-1}(n) + g_{m-1}(n-1)$ | Lattice stage forward recursion |
| 10 | Stability bound: $\vert k_m \vert < 1 \; \forall m$ | All-pole IIR stability criterion |
| 11 | Ladder output: $y(n) = \sum v_m f_m(n) - \sum \mu_m g_m(n)$ | General pole-zero lattice output |
| 12 | Transpose: reverse arrows + swap in/out | Alternative structure (same $H(z)$) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Direct Form-II from Direct Form-I

Let the original transfer function be:

$$H(z) = \underbrace{\left(\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}\right)}_{B(z)} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{1}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}}_{1/A(z)}$$

**Step 1:** Introduce an intermediate variable $W(z)$ such that:

$$X(z) \xrightarrow{1/A(z)} W(z) \xrightarrow{B(z)} Y(z)$$

Explicitly:

$$W(z) \cdot A(z) = X(z) \implies W(z) = \frac{X(z)}{A(z)}$$

$$Y(z) = B(z) \cdot W(z)$$

**Step 2:** In the time domain:

$$w(n) = x(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, w(n-k)$$

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, w(n-k)$$

**Step 3:** Notice that $w(n)$ depends only on **past samples of $w$ itself** — so we need only **one delay line of length N** to compute $w(n-1), w(n-2), \dots, w(n-N)$.

**Step 4:** The output $y(n)$ is a linear combination of the same $w(n)$ samples already stored. So we can **tap off the same delay line** — eliminating the second delay line entirely.

**Result:** Direct Form-II uses only **max(N, M) delays**, proving it is **canonic**.

### 3.2 Derivation of Cascade Form

Given a 4th-order numerator and denominator:

$$H(z) = \frac{b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2} + b_3 z^{-3} + b_4 z^{-4}}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2} + a_3 z^{-3} + a_4 z^{-4}}$$

**Step 1:** Find the roots of numerator and denominator.

Let numerator roots be $z_{1}, z_{2}, z_{3}, z_{4}$ and denominator roots be $p_{1}, p_{2}, p_{3}, p_{4}$.

$$H(z) = b_0 \, \frac{(1 - z_1 z^{-1})(1 - z_2 z^{-1})(1 - z_3 z^{-1})(1 - z_4 z^{-1})}{(1 - p_1 z^{-1})(1 - p_2 z^{-1})(1 - p_3 z^{-1})(1 - p_4 z^{-1})}$$

**Step 2:** Pair real roots with real roots; for complex roots, form complex-conjugate pairs:

$$\text{Section 1: } (1 - z_1 z^{-1})(1 - z_2 z^{-1}) = 1 + \beta_{11} z^{-1} + \beta_{12} z^{-2}$$

$$\text{Section 2: } (1 - z_3 z^{-1})(1 - z_4 z^{-1}) = 1 + \beta_{21} z^{-1} + \beta_{22} z^{-2}$$

Similarly for the denominator to form two 2nd-order sections.

**Step 3:** Reconstruct the cascade product:

$$H(z) = b_0 \, \frac{1 + \beta_{11} z^{-1} + \beta_{12} z^{-2}}{1 + \alpha_{11} z^{-1} + \alpha_{12} z^{-2}} \cdot \frac{1 + \beta_{21} z^{-1} + \beta_{22} z^{-2}}{1 + \alpha_{21} z^{-1} + \alpha_{22} z^{-2}}$$

Each 2nd-order factor is realized using a **single Direct Form-II biquad**.

### 3.3 Derivation of Parallel Form

Starting from the same transfer function, perform partial-fraction expansion:

$$H(z) = \frac{B(z)}{A(z)}$$

Assuming distinct poles $p_1, p_2, \dots, p_N$ and $M < N$:

$$H(z) = \frac{R_1}{1 - p_1 z^{-1}} + \frac{R_2}{1 - p_2 z^{-1}} + \cdots + \frac{R_N}{1 - p_N z^{-1}}$$

Each residue $R_k$ is obtained by:

$$R_k = \left. (1 - p_k z^{-1}) \, H(z) \right|_{z = p_k}$$

For complex-conjugate pole pairs $(p, p^*)$, group them into 2nd-order sections with **real coefficients**:

$$\frac{R}{1 - p z^{-1}} + \frac{R^*}{1 - p^* z^{-1}} = \frac{B_{0} + B_{1} z^{-1}}{1 + a_{1} z^{-1} + a_{2} z^{-2}}$$

where $a_1 = -(p + p^*)$, $a_2 = p \cdot p^*$, and $B_0, B_1$ are obtained by algebraic combination of $R$ and $R^*$.

### 3.4 Derivation of the Lattice Stage Recursion

We derive the step-down recursion for an all-pole IIR filter of order N.

**Setup:** Let the transfer function at stage $m$ be $A_m(z)$ with reflection coefficient $k_m$. Define:

$$A_m(z) = 1 + a^{(m)}_1 z^{-1} + a^{(m)}_2 z^{-2} + \cdots + a^{(m)}_m z^{-m}$$

**The reverse Schur recursion** relates $A_m(z)$ to $A_{m-1}(z)$:

$$A_m(z) = A_{m-1}(z) + k_m \, z^{-m} \, A_{m-1}(z^{-1})$$

**Proof by polynomial reversal:** Define the reciprocal polynomial:

$$\tilde{A}_m(z) = z^{-m} A_m(z^{-1}) = a^{(m)}_m + a^{(m)}_{m-1} z^{-1} + \cdots + z^{-m}$$

Then:

$$A_m(z) = A_{m-1}(z) + k_m \, \tilde{A}_{m-1}(z)$$

Equating the coefficient of $z^{-m}$ on both sides yields $k_m = a^{(m)}_m$ (i.e., the **last coefficient of the m-th order polynomial** is the next reflection coefficient).

**Time-domain equivalent:** Multiply both sides by $X(z)$ and define $F_m(z) = A_m(z) X(z)$ and $G_m(z) = \tilde{A}_m(z) X(z)$. This gives the time-domain recursions:

$$f_m(n) = f_{m-1}(n) + k_m \, g_{m-1}(n-1)$$

$$g_m(n) = k_m \, f_{m-1}(n) + g_{m-1}(n-1)$$

These are precisely the lattice-stage update equations. The all-pole output is $y(n) = f_N(n)$, and stability is guaranteed iff $\vert k_m \vert < 1$ for all $m$.

### 3.5 Worked Example — Conversion to Cascade

**Problem:** Realize

$$H(z) = \frac{1 + 0.5 z^{-1} + 0.4 z^{-2} + 0.3 z^{-3} + 0.2 z^{-4}}{1 - 0.3 z^{-1} + 0.2 z^{-2} - 0.4 z^{-3} + 0.1 z^{-4}}$$

in **cascade form** with 2 biquads.

**Step 1: Find numerator roots** (using polynomial factorization or computational tool):

Numerator roots ≈ $\{z_1, z_2, z_3, z_4\}$. Form pairs.

**Step 2: Construct Section 1:**

$$H_1(z) = \frac{1 + \beta_{11} z^{-1} + \beta_{12} z^{-2}}{1 + \alpha_{11} z^{-1} + \alpha_{12} z^{-2}}$$

**Step 3: Construct Section 2** using remaining roots:

$$H_2(z) = \frac{1 + \beta_{21} z^{-1} + \beta_{22} z^{-2}}{1 + \alpha_{21} z^{-1} + \alpha_{22} z^{-2}}$$

**Step 4: Combine:**

$$H(z) = b_0 \cdot H_1(z) \cdot H_2(z), \quad b_0 \text{ is the leading coefficient scale factor.}$$

Each $H_k(z)$ is then implemented as a **Direct Form-II biquad** and cascaded.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** Always verify that $H_1(z) H_2(z)$ reproduces the original $H(z)$ by multiplying out and comparing coefficients.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Direct Form-I Block Diagram (5th-order example: N = M = 5)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp["x(n)"] --> b0["× b0"]
    inp --> dly1["z^-1"]
    dly1 --> dly1b["z^-1"]
    dly1b --> dly1c["z^-1"]
    dly1c --> dly1d["z^-1"]
    dly1d --> dly1e["z^-1"]
    dly1e --> b5["× b5"]
    b0 --> addA["Σ"]
    b5 --> addA
    addA --> outA["y(n) raw"]
    outA --> dly2["z^-1"]
    dly2 --> dly2b["z^-1"]
    dly2b --> dly2c["z^-1"]
    dly2c --> dly2d["z^-1"]
    dly2d --> a1["× -a1"]
    dly2d --> dly2e["z^-1"]
    dly2e --> a5["× -a5"]
    a1 --> addB["Σ"]
    a5 --> addB
    addB --> inp
    outA --> out["y(n)"]
```

### 4.2 Direct Form-II (Canonic) Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp["x(n)"] --> addA["Σ"]
    addA --> dly1["z^-1"]
    addA --> b0["× b0"]
    dly1 --> dly2["z^-1"]
    dly2 --> dly3["z^-1"]
    dly3 --> b3["× b3"]
    dly3 --> dly4["z^-1"]
    dly4 --> b4["× b4"]
    dly2 --> b2["× b2"]
    dly1 --> b1["× b1"]
    dly4 --> a1["× -a1"]
    dly4 --> dly5["z^-1"]
    dly5 --> a2["× -a2"]
    dly5 --> dly6["z^-1"]
    dly6 --> a3["× -a3"]
    dly3 --> aMid["× -a3"]
    a1 --> addA
    a2 --> addA
    a3 --> addA
    aMid --> addA
    b0 --> sumOut["Σ"]
    b1 --> sumOut
    b2 --> sumOut
    b3 --> sumOut
    b4 --> sumOut
    sumOut --> out["y(n)"]
```

### 4.3 Cascade Form Block Diagram (Two Biquad Sections)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp["x(n)"] --> bq1["Biquad 1: H1(z)"]
    bq1 --> bq2["Biquad 2: H2(z)"]
    bq2 --> bq3["Biquad K: HK(z)"]
    bq3 --> scale["× b0 gain"]
    scale --> out["y(n)"]
    bq1 -.Feed.-> bq1
    bq2 -.Feed.-> bq2
    bq3 -.Feed.-> bq3
```

### 4.4 Parallel Form Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp["x(n)"] --> spl["Branch Splitter"]
    spl --> sec1["Section 1: H1(z)"]
    spl --> sec2["Section 2: H2(z)"]
    spl --> sec3["Section K: HK(z)"]
    spl --> cterm["Constant C (FIR path)"]
    sec1 --> sum["Σ"]
    sec2 --> sum
    sec3 --> sum
    cterm --> sum
    sum --> out["y(n)"]
```

### 4.5 Lattice-Ladder IIR Structure (3rd-Order Example)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp["x(n)"] --> f0["f0(n)"]
    f0 --> f1add["+"]
    f0 --> g0["g0(n)"]
    g0 --> g1["× k1"]
    g1 --> g1dly["z^-1"]
    g1dly --> f1add
    f1add --> f1["f1(n)"]
    f1 --> f2add["+"]
    f1 --> g1n["g1(n)"]
    g1n --> g2calc["× k2"]
    g2calc --> g2dly["z^-1"]
    g2dly --> f2add
    f2add --> f2["f2(n)"]
    f2 --> f3add["+"]
    f2 --> g2n["g2(n)"]
    g2n --> g3calc["× k3"]
    g3calc --> g3dly["z^-1"]
    g3dly --> f3add
    f3add --> f3["f3(n)"]
    f3 --> outA["× v3"]
    g3n["g3(n)"] --> outB["× -mu3"]
    f2 --> outC["× v2"]
    g2n --> outD["× -mu2"]
    f1 --> outE["× v1"]
    g1n --> outF["× -mu1"]
    f0 --> outG["× v0"]
    outA --> sumOut["Σ"]
    outB --> sumOut
    outC --> sumOut
    outD --> sumOut
    outE --> sumOut
    outF --> sumOut
    outG --> sumOut
    sumOut --> out["y(n)"]
```

### 4.6 Functional Architecture — Structure Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root["H(z) Realization Choice"]
    root --> catA["Single-Section Forms"]
    root --> catB["Multi-Section Forms"]
    root --> catC["Modular Forms"]
    catA --> d1["Direct Form-I"]
    catA --> d2["Direct Form-II Canonic"]
    catA --> d3["Transposed Forms"]
    catB --> c1["Cascade of Biquads"]
    catB --> c2["Parallel Biquads"]
    catC --> l1["Lattice All-Pole"]
    catC --> l2["Lattice-Ladder General"]
    d1 --> note1["N+M delays; sensitive to quantization"]
    d2 --> note2["max(N,M) delays; minimum hardware"]
    c1 --> note3["Robust; standard for fixed-point DSP"]
    c2 --> note4["Fast; good for pipelining"]
    l1 --> note5["Stable iff k_m bounded by 1"]
    l2 --> note6["Used in linear prediction and speech"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

> Define a *canonic* realization of a digital filter. State the number of delay elements required for the Direct Form-I and Direct Form-II realizations of an Nth-order IIR filter.

**Model Answer:**

A canonic digital filter structure is one in which **the number of delay elements equals the order of the filter**, i.e., no redundant delays are used.

For an Nth-order IIR filter (with N ≥ M, the common case in KTU problems):

- **Direct Form-I:** Requires **N + M** delay elements (separate delay lines for the input and output sequences). It is **not canonic** because it has more delays than the filter order.
- **Direct Form-II (Canonic Form):** Requires only **max(N, M) = N** delay elements, achieved by combining the two delay lines into one. It is the standard **canonic realization**.

**(3 marks: 1 mark definition, 1 mark DF-I count, 1 mark DF-II count)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> List any **four advantages** of realizing an IIR filter in cascade form using 2nd-order sections rather than in Direct Form.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Reduced coefficient sensitivity:** In cascade form, each 2nd-order section has a low-order polynomial whose coefficients are less sensitive to finite-wordlength rounding than the high-order polynomials of the Direct Form.
2. **Better overflow control:** Each biquad can be scaled independently using $L_p$-norm or $L_\infty$-norm techniques to prevent intermediate overflow.
3. **Modularity:** Standard biquad DSP software/hardware blocks can be reused; identical building blocks simplify design.
4. **Pairing flexibility:** Poles and zeros can be paired and ordered to minimize round-off noise and dynamic-range issues, with the optimal pairing rule (closest pole-zero geometric distance).

**(3 marks: ¾ mark per point × 4)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

> **(a)** The transfer function of an IIR filter is
> $$H(z) = \frac{(1 + 0.5 z^{-1})(1 + 0.3 z^{-1})}{(1 - 0.6 z^{-1})(1 + 0.4 z^{-1})(1 - 0.7 z^{-1})}$$
> Realize the filter in **cascade form** using 2nd-order sections. **[7 marks]**
>
> **(b)** Derive the step-down recursion equations of the **lattice structure** for an all-pole IIR filter and state the stability condition on the reflection coefficients. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1:** Identify poles and zeros.

- Zeros: $z_1 = -0.5$, $z_2 = -0.3$
- Poles: $p_1 = +0.6$, $p_2 = -0.0.4$ (i.e., $-0.4$), $p_3 = +0.7$

Order is 3, so we need $\lceil 3/2 \rceil = 2$ biquad sections (one 1st-order + one 2nd-order, or pair one zero with two poles).

**Step 2:** Form Section 1 as a 1st-order section using the zero $z_1 = -0.5$ and pole $p_1 = 0.6$:

$$H_1(z) = \frac{1 + 0.5 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.6 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 3:** Form Section 2 as a 2nd-order section using the remaining zero $z_2 = -0.3$ and poles $p_2 = -0.4$, $p_3 = 0.7$:

Denominator polynomial: $(1 + 0.4 z^{-1})(1 - 0.7 z^{-1})$

Expanding:

$$1 + 0.4 z^{-1} - 0.7 z^{-1} - 0.28 z^{-2} = 1 - 0.3 z^{-1} - 0.28 z^{-2}$$

So $\alpha_{11} = -0.3$, $\alpha_{12} = -0.28$.

Numerator: $(1 + 0.3 z^{-1}) = 1 + 0.3 z^{-1}$ (constant term in $z^{-2}$ is 0).

So $\beta_{21} = 0.3$, $\beta_{22} = 0$.

$$H_2(z) = \frac{1 + 0.3 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.3 z^{-1} - 0.28 z^{-2}}$$

**Step 4:** Combine with $b_0 = 1$:

$$H(z) = H_1(z) \cdot H_2(z) = \frac{1 + 0.5 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.6 z^{-1}} \cdot \frac{1 + 0.3 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.3 z^{-1} - 0.28 z^{-2}}$$

Each section is implemented as a Direct Form-II biquad (1st-order section is a degenerate biquad).

**Verification:** Multiplying out gives the original $H(z)$. ✓

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

Let the Nth-order all-pole IIR filter have transfer function:

$$H(z) = \frac{1}{A_N(z)}, \quad A_N(z) = 1 + a_1 z^{-1} + \cdots + a_N z^{-N}$$

Define the **order-m polynomial** $A_m(z) = 1 + a_1^{(m)} z^{-1} + \cdots + a_m^{(m)} z^{-m}$.

The **reverse Schur recursion** proceeds from stage $m = N$ down to $m = 1$:

$$A_m(z) = A_{m-1}(z) + k_m \, z^{-m} \, A_{m-1}(z^{-1})$$

This is derived by extracting the last coefficient: $k_m = a_m^{(m)}$.

Define $f_m(n)$ and $g_m(n)$ via the $z$-domain relations:

$$F_m(z) = A_m(z) X(z), \quad G_m(z) = z^{-m} A_m(z^{-1}) X(z)$$

Applying the inverse Z-transform to $A_m(z) X(z) = A_{m-1}(z) X(z) + k_m \, z^{-1} [z^{-(m-1)} A_{m-1}(z^{-1}) X(z)]$:

$$f_m(n) = f_{m-1}(n) + k_m \, g_{m-1}(n-1)$$

The reciprocal term gives:

$$g_m(n) = k_m \, f_{m-1}(n) + g_{m-1}(n-1)$$

with initial conditions $f_0(n) = g_0(n) = x(n)$.

**Stability condition:** The all-pole filter is **stable if and only if all reflection coefficients satisfy** $\vert k_m \vert < 1$ for $m = 1, 2, \dots, N$.

**(7 marks: 2 marks recursion statement, 3 marks derivation, 1 mark initial conditions, 1 mark stability condition)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

> **(a)** For the transfer function
> $$H(z) = \frac{1 + 0.2 z^{-1} + 0.1 z^{-2}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1} + 0.3 z^{-2} - 0.1 z^{-3}}$$
> obtain the **Direct Form-II realization**. Show the block diagram and write the difference equations. **[7 marks]**
>
> **(b)** Perform the **partial-fraction expansion** and obtain the **parallel-form realization** of
> $$H(z) = \frac{z^2}{(z-0.5)(z^2 + 0.4z + 0.2)}$$
> Show all intermediate steps. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1:** Identify coefficients:
- Numerator: $b_0 = 1$, $b_1 = 0.2$, $b_2 = 0.1$, $b_3 = 0$ (M = 2)
- Denominator: $a_1 = -0.5$, $a_2 = 0.3$, $a_3 = -0.1$ (N = 3)
- $a_0 = 1$

**Step 2:** Define intermediate signal $w(n)$:

$$w(n) = x(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{3} a_k \, w(n-k)$$

Explicitly:

$$w(n) = x(n) + 0.5 \, w(n-1) - 0.3 \, w(n-2) + 0.1 \, w(n-3)$$

(Note: the minus sign flips each $a_k$.)

**Step 3:** Output equation:

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{2} b_k \, w(n-k)$$

Explicitly:

$$y(n) = w(n) + 0.2 \, w(n-1) + 0.1 \, w(n-2)$$

**Step 4:** Block Diagram (Direct Form-II, 3 delays):

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp["x(n)"] --> sumA["Σ"]
    sumA --> dly1["z^-1 = w(n-1)"]
    dly1 --> dly2["z^-1 = w(n-2)"]
    dly2 --> dly3["z^-1 = w(n-3)"]
    dly1 --> mulA1["× 0.5"]
    dly2 --> mulA2["× -0.3"]
    dly3 --> mulA3["× 0.1"]
    mulA1 --> sumA
    mulA2 --> sumA
    mulA3 --> sumA
    dly1 --> mulB1["× 0.2"]
    dly2 --> mulB2["× 0.1"]
    dly3 -.unused tap.-> mulB3["× 0 (no b3)"]
    sumA --> mulB0["× 1 (b0)"]
    mulB0 --> sumB["Σ"]
    mulB1 --> sumB
    mulB2 --> sumB
    sumB --> out["y(n)"]
```

**Delays used:** $\max(M, N) = 3$. Hence Direct Form-II is **canonic** for this filter. ✓

**(7 marks: 2 marks equations, 3 marks block diagram, 2 marks interpretation)**

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1:** Convert $H(z) = \frac{z^2}{(z-0.5)(z^2 + 0.4z + 0.2)}$ to negative powers of $z$ for partial-fraction expansion.

Multiply numerator and denominator by $z^{-3}$:

$$H(z) = \frac{z^{-1}}{(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})(1 + 0.4 z^{-1} + 0.2 z^{-2})}$$

**Step 2:** Find the roots of the denominator.

Pole $p_1 = 0.5$ (real).

For $z^2 + 0.4z + 0.2 = 0$, by quadratic formula:

$$p_{2,3} = \frac{-0.4 \pm \sqrt{0.16 - 0.8}}{2} = \frac{-0.4 \pm j \sqrt{0.64}}{2} = -0.2 \pm j \, 0.4$$

These are complex conjugates.

**Step 3:** Express $H(z)$ in terms of $z$ and perform PFE.

$$H(z) = \frac{z^2}{(z - 0.5)(z - p_2)(z - p_2^*)} = \frac{R_1}{z - 0.5} + \frac{R_2}{z - p_2} + \frac{R_3}{z - p_2^*}$$

Compute $R_1$:

$$R_1 = \left. \frac{z^2}{(z - p_2)(z - p_2^*)} \right|_{z = 0.5} = \frac{0.25}{(0.5 - p_2)(0.5 - p_2^*)}$$

Substituting $p_2 = -0.2 + j0.4$, $p_2^* = -0.2 - j0.4$:

$$0.5 - p_2 = 0.7 - j0.4, \quad 0.5 - p_2^* = 0.7 + j0.4$$

$$(0.7 - j0.4)(0.7 + j0.4) = 0.49 + 0.16 = 0.65$$

$$R_1 = \frac{0.25}{0.65} = \frac{5}{13} \approx 0.3846$$

**Step 4:** Group the complex-conjugate pair into a real 2nd-order section:

$$\frac{R_2}{z - p_2} + \frac{R_3}{z - p_2^*} = \frac{B_0 z + B_1}{z^2 + 0.4z + 0.2}$$

By partial fractions, $R_3 = R_2^*$. Using algebraic reduction (or residue formula), the real-coefficient form is:

$$H(z) = \frac{0.3846}{z - 0.5} + \frac{B_0 z + B_1}{z^2 + 0.4z + 0.2}$$

**Step 5:** Convert back to negative powers of $z$:

$$H(z) = \frac{0.3846 \, z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} + \frac{B_0 + B_1 z^{-1}}{1 + 0.4 z^{-1} + 0.2 z^{-2}}$$

**Step 6:** Parallel realization: two parallel branches feeding a summer.

**Branch 1:** 1st-order IIR section with $b_0 = 0.3846$, $b_1 = 0$, $a_1 = -0.5$.
**Branch 2:** 2nd-order IIR section with $b_0 = B_0$, $b_1 = B_1$, $a_1 = 0.4$, $a_2 = 0.2$.

The two branches operate **in parallel** and their outputs are summed to produce $y(n)$.

**(7 marks: 2 marks setup, 2 marks residues, 2 marks combination, 1 marks parallel structure)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Scheme Valuation):**
>
> 1. **Sign error in feedback coefficients:** Students forget to flip the sign of $a_k$ in the difference equation. The block diagram must use **$-a_k$** multipliers, not $a_k$. *(−1 to −2 marks)*
>
> 2. **Confusing Direct Form-I and Direct Form-II delays:** DF-I has **two separate delay lines**; DF-II has **one merged delay line**. Markers specifically check this. *(−1 mark)*
>
> 3. **Forgetting the "canonic" property:** Direct Form-II is canonic. Direct Form-I is **not** canonic. Examiners love to test this distinction. *(−1 mark)*
>
> 4. **In cascade form, omitting the gain $b_0$:** Many students forget that factoring the numerator/denominator requires a leading gain factor. Always check the leading coefficient. *(−1 mark)*
>
> 5. **In lattice structure, omitting the stability bound $\vert k_m \vert < 1$:** This is a guaranteed 1-mark question. Missing it costs a full mark.
>
> 6. **In parallel form, ignoring complex-conjugate pairing:** When poles are complex, students must combine them into a real-coefficient 2nd-order section — never implement complex-coefficient branches. *(−2 marks)*
>
> 7. **Transposed form confusion:** The Transposition Theorem requires **three** simultaneous operations (reverse arrows, swap adders/junctions, swap input/output). Applying only one will give a wrong transfer function. *(−2 marks)*

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for IIR Filter Structures (KTU Module 4):**
>
> ☐ **Direct Form-I** = two delay lines, N + M delays, **not canonic**. Direct mapping of LCCDE.
>
> ☐ **Direct Form-II** = one merged delay line, **max(N,M) delays, canonic**. Intermediate signal $w(n)$.
>
> ☐ **Transposed Forms** obtained via 3-step Transposition Theorem; same $H(z)$, different internal noise.
>
> ☐ **Cascade Form** = product of 2nd-order biquads. Use **closest pole-zero pairing**. Robust to quantization.
>
> ☐ **Parallel Form** = sum of partial-fraction branches. Compute residues via $R_k = (z - p_k) H(z)\vert_{z=p_k}$. Group complex-conjugate pairs into real 2nd-order sections.
>
> ☐ **Lattice (all-pole) recursion:**
> - Forward: $f_m(n) = f_{m-1}(n) + k_m g_{m-1}(n-1)$
> - Forward: $g_m(n) = k_m f_{m-1}(n) + g_{m-1}(n-1)$
> - Stability: $\vert k_m \vert < 1 \; \forall m$
>
> ☐ **Lattice-Ladder (general pole-zero)** adds ladder coefficients $v_m, \mu_m$ at the output:
> $y(n) = \sum v_m f_m(n) - \sum \mu_m g_m(n)$
>
> ☐ All structures produce **identical** output in **infinite-precision** arithmetic, but **different** output in fixed-point.
>
> ☐ **Direct Form** = minimum derivation effort; **Cascade** = best for hardware DSP; **Parallel** = best for pipelining; **Lattice** = best for speech / linear prediction.
>
> ☐ **Hardware count for a biquad:** 5 multipliers, 4 adders, 2 delays (Direct Form-II).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coefficient Quantization Effects in FIR and IIR Filters - Basic Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT758 - DIGITAL FILTER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Coefficient Quantization Effects in FIR and IIR Filters - Basic Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coefficient Quantization** is the process of representing the theoretically infinite-precision multiplier coefficients of a digital filter (FIR or IIR) using a finite number of bits. Since practical hardware (DSP processors, FPGAs) can only store coefficients with finite word length (B bits), each coefficient `b_k` (FIR) or `a_k`, `b_k` (IIR) must be mapped to the nearest representable value from a discrete set — this mapping introduces a permanent, deterministic **quantization error** that perturbs the filter's transfer function.

The mathematical representation of the quantization operation is:

$$Q[x] = x + e_q(x)$$

where `x` is the true infinite-precision value, `Q[x]` is the quantized (stored) value, and `e_q(x)` is the quantization error bounded by the **quantization step size**:

$$q = 2^{-B}$$

for a B-bit fractional representation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Board-Exam Vocabulary You Must Memorize:**
> - **Word Length (B):** Total number of bits available to store a coefficient.
> - **Quantization Step (q):** Smallest resolvable difference between two stored values.
> - **Significand / Mantissa:** Fractional bits carrying precision.
> - **Overflow:** Occurs when the magnitude of a value exceeds the representable range.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **currency exchange clerk** in the 1800s working only with **physical gold coins of fixed denominations** (1g, 5g, 10g). A customer wants to convert exactly **7.3 grams of gold** into a different currency. You can only give back coins in whole-gram increments. The best you can do is give a **7-gram coin**, and the **0.3 grams of "lost" gold** stays in the cash drawer as unavoidable error.

**Coefficient quantization works identically:**
- The customer's gold = ideal infinite-precision filter coefficient (e.g., `0.7321...`)
- Your coin denominations = discrete levels representable in B bits (e.g., `0.5000`, `0.6250`, `0.7500`, `0.8750`)
- The change in the drawer = **quantization error**
- The 7-gram coin you hand back = the stored finite-precision coefficient

**The Critical Danger in IIR Filters:** In FIR filters, "lost precision" only shifts the filter's frequency response slightly — annoying but safe. In **IIR filters**, the same imprecision **physically moves the poles** of the filter on the z-plane. If a pole gets pushed **outside the unit circle**, the filter **explodes into instability** (output grows unbounded) — like losing precision in the swinging rope's length until it slips off its hook.

---

## 1.3 Number Representation Formats Used in KTU Problems

| Format | Range of Values | Precision Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Signed Fixed-Point** | $-1 \le x < 1$ (normalized) | Uniform step of $q = 2^{-B}$ everywhere |
| **Unsigned Fixed-Point** | $0 \le x < 2$ | Uniform step of $q = 2^{-B}$ |
| **Floating-Point** | $\pm M \cdot 2^{E}$ | Relative error constant, absolute error grows with magnitude |
| **Block Floating-Point** | Shared exponent, per-block scaling | Hybrid: large dynamic range + group quantization |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 Scheme board questions, the **default assumption** (unless stated otherwise) is **signed 2's complement fixed-point representation with B fractional bits**, where the quantization step is $q = 2^{-B}$ and values lie in $[-1, 1)$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Why Quantization Matters

Digital filters are designed using **floating-point arithmetic on a computer**, producing theoretically infinite-precision coefficients. When these coefficients are implemented in real hardware:

1. **Storage Constraint:** Each coefficient occupies a fixed number of memory cells (B bits).
2. **Rounding/Truncation:** The infinite-precision value must be approximated to the nearest discrete level.
3. **Transfer Function Deviation:** $H(z)$ computed with quantized coefficients $\hat{H}(z)$ deviates from the ideal $H(z)$.
4. **Frequency Response Distortion:** Magnitude $\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert$ and phase $\angle H(e^{j\omega})$ change.
5. **Stability Threat (IIR only):** Poles may migrate outside the unit circle.

---

## 2.2 Quantization Methods and Their Error Bounds

### A) Rounding (Mid-Tread / Mid-Rise)
The quantized value is the **nearest representable level**:

$$-\frac{q}{2} \le e_q \le \frac{q}{2}$$

### B) Truncation
The fractional bits are simply discarded:

- **2's Complement Truncation:** $-q \le e_q \le 0$ (always rounds toward negative)
- **Sign-Magnitude Truncation:** $-\frac{q}{2} \le e_q \le \frac{q}{2}$ (same as rounding)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU board questions**, if the question does not specify the rounding method, **assume rounding** with error range $[-q/2, q/2]$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Quantization step size | $q = 2^{-B}$ | B = number of fractional bits |
| 2 | Rounding error bound | $\vert e_q \vert \le q/2$ | $e_q \in [-q/2,\, q/2]$ |
| 3 | Truncation error bound (2's comp.) | $-q \le e_q \le 0$ | Always non-positive |
| 4 | Mean of quantization error | $m_e = 0$ | (assuming uniform distribution) |
| 5 | Variance of quantization error | $\sigma_e^{2} = \dfrac{q^{2}}{12}$ | Mean-square error |
| 6 | Relative error (fixed-point) | $\varepsilon_r = \dfrac{\Delta a_k}{a_k}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 7 | Pole displacement (IIR) | $\Delta p_i = \sum\limits_{k=1}^{N} \dfrac{\partial p_i}{\partial a_k}\,\Delta a_k$ | Vector shift on z-plane |
| 8 | Pole sensitivity coefficient | $S_{a_k}^{p_i} = \dfrac{\partial p_i}{\partial a_k}$ | Derivative w.r.t. coefficient |
| 9 | Sensitivity magnitude bound | $\vert \Delta p_i \vert \le \sum\limits_{k=1}^{N} \vert S_{a_k}^{p_i} \vert \cdot \dfrac{q}{2}$ | Worst-case pole shift |
| 10 | Frequency response deviation | $\Delta H(e^{j\omega}) = \hat{H}(e^{j\omega}) - H(e^{j\omega})$ | Complex-valued deviation |
| 11 | Maximum pole shift (high Q) | $\vert \Delta p \vert_{\max} \approx \dfrac{q}{2 \sin\theta}$ | $\theta$ = pole angle |
| 12 | Floating-point relative error | $\varepsilon_r \le 2^{-(t-1)}$ | $t$ = mantissa bits |
| 13 | Output SNR (coefficient quant.) | $\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\sigma_y^{2}}{\sigma_{eq}^{2}}\right)$ dB | |
| 14 | Equivalent quantization noise at output | $\sigma_{eq}^{2} = \sigma_e^{2}\sum\limits_{k} \vert h[k] \vert^{2}$ | For FIR filter of length N |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Coefficient Quantization Analysis |
|---|---|
| **Audio DSP (smartphones, headphones)** | Active Noise Cancellation filters — must fit in 16-bit/24-bit fixed-point hardware. |
| **Hearing Aids** | Ultra-low-power DSPs use 16-bit coefficients; quantization limits filter selectivity. |
| **5G/6G Baseband Processing** | High-order FIR filters in channel equalizers — coefficient word length affects spectral mask compliance. |
| **Biomedical Implants** (pacemakers, neural stimulators) | IIR filters with severely limited bit depth to save power — stability must be guaranteed. |
| **Automotive Radar (ADAS)** | Cascaded IIR biquads for chirp processing — pole movement tolerance is critical. |
| **IoT Sensor Nodes** | 8-bit/16-bit microcontrollers — coefficient quantization governs achievable filter Q. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this analysis critical for KTU?**
> Because a perfect floating-point filter designed on MATLAB can **become unstable** when implemented in C on a 16-bit DSP. The examiner will test whether you understand **why** and **how much** degradation occurs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Variance of Quantization Error (Rounding)

**Goal:** Derive the mean-square quantization noise power $\sigma_e^{2}$ for a B-bit rounding quantizer assuming the input signal traverses many quantization levels (uniform distribution assumption).

**Step 1 — State the quantization error definition:**

$$e_q = Q[x] - x$$

For rounding, every value of `x` within a quantization interval is mapped to the **midpoint** of that interval, so $e_q$ lies in the half-open range $[-q/2,\, q/2)$.

**Step 2 — Compute the mean (statistical average):**

Assuming $e_q$ is uniformly distributed across the interval of width $q$:

$$m_e = E[e_q] = \int_{-q/2}^{q/2} e_q \cdot \dfrac{1}{q}\, de_q$$

$$m_e = \dfrac{1}{q}\left[\dfrac{e_q^{2}}{2}\right]_{-q/2}^{q/2}$$

$$m_e = \dfrac{1}{q}\left(\dfrac{q^{2}}{8} - \dfrac{q^{2}}{8}\right)$$

$$m_e = 0$$

**Step 3 — Compute the variance:**

$$\sigma_e^{2} = E[e_q^{2}] - (E[e_q])^{2} = E[e_q^{2}]$$

$$\sigma_e^{2} = \int_{-q/2}^{q/2} e_q^{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{q}\, de_q$$

$$\sigma_e^{2} = \dfrac{1}{q}\left[\dfrac{e_q^{3}}{3}\right]_{-q/2}^{q/2}$$

$$\sigma_e^{2} = \dfrac{1}{q}\left(\dfrac{q^{3}}{24} - \left(-\dfrac{q^{3}}{24}\right)\right)$$

$$\sigma_e^{2} = \dfrac{1}{q} \cdot \dfrac{2 q^{3}}{24}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_e^{2} = \dfrac{q^{2}}{12}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $q = 2^{-B}$:**

$$\sigma_e^{2} = \dfrac{2^{-2B}}{12}$$

This is the **fundamental noise power formula** for any rounding quantizer with B fractional bits. Each additional bit of precision **reduces noise power by a factor of 4 (6 dB)**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pole Sensitivity in a Second-Order IIR Section

**Setup:** Consider a second-order IIR section with transfer function:

$$H(z) = \dfrac{b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2}}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2}}$$

The poles are the roots of the denominator polynomial:

$$D(z) = 1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2} = 0$$

Multiplying by $z^{2}$:

$$z^{2} + a_1 z + a_2 = 0$$

**Goal:** Find how the pole $p_i$ moves when coefficient $a_k$ is perturbed by $\Delta a_k$.

**Step 1 — Apply the Implicit Function Theorem:**

Since $D(p_i) = 0$ identically, the total differential must vanish:

$$\dfrac{\partial D}{\partial a_1}\Delta a_1 + \dfrac{\partial D}{\partial a_2}\Delta a_2 + \dfrac{\partial D}{\partial p_i}\Delta p_i = 0$$

**Step 2 — Compute partial derivatives:**

$$\dfrac{\partial D}{\partial a_1} = z \bigg|_{z = p_i} = p_i$$

$$\dfrac{\partial D}{\partial a_2} = 1 \bigg|_{z = p_i} = 1$$

$$\dfrac{\partial D}{\partial p_i} = 2p_i + a_1 = \dfrac{d D(z)}{d z}\bigg|_{z = p_i}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\Delta p_i$:**

$$\Delta p_i = -\dfrac{p_i \, \Delta a_1 + \Delta a_2}{2 p_i + a_1}$$

**Step 4 — Bound the pole displacement magnitude:**

Using the triangle inequality and $\vert \Delta a_k \vert \le q/2$:

$$\vert \Delta p_i \vert \le \dfrac{\vert p_i \vert (\dfrac{q}{2}) + \dfrac{q}{2}}{\vert 2 p_i + a_1 \vert}$$

$$\vert \Delta p_i \vert \le \dfrac{q}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\vert p_i \vert + 1}{\vert 2 p_i + a_1 \vert}$$

**Step 5 — Critical observation for **KTU**:**

If the two poles are **close together** (high-Q narrowband filter), then $2 p_i + a_1 \approx 0$, and the denominator approaches zero. The pole displacement **blows up**:

$$\boxed{\vert \Delta p_i \vert_{\max} \approx \dfrac{q}{2 \sin\theta}}$$

where $\theta$ is the angle of the pole from the positive real axis. **Smaller $\theta$ (poles closer to $z = +1$, i.e., DC narrowband filters) ⇒ larger pole shift ⇒ greater instability risk.**

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Comprehensive Numerical Problem

**Problem:** An FIR filter has coefficients `b = [0.2031, 0.5938, 0.3125, 0.0938]` designed in floating-point. The hardware uses B = 4 bits (signed 2's complement). The desired ideal frequency response at $\omega = 0.5\pi$ is $H_d(e^{j0.5\pi}) = -0.15 + j\,0.20$.

**Tasks:**
1. Identify the quantization step $q$.
2. Compute the quantization error for each coefficient.
3. Compute the actual frequency response deviation at $\omega = 0.5\pi$.

**Step 1 — Quantization step:**

$$q = 2^{-4} = \dfrac{1}{16} = 0.0625$$

The representable levels in $[-1, 1)$ are $\{-0.9375, -0.8750, \ldots, 0, \ldots, +0.9375\}$.

**Step 2 — Round each coefficient to the nearest level:**

| Coefficient | True Value | Quantized Value | Error $\Delta b_k$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $b_0$ | 0.2031 | 0.1875 (3/16) | $-0.0156$ |
| $b_1$ | 0.5938 | 0.5625 (9/16) | $-0.0313$ |
| $b_2$ | 0.3125 | 0.3125 (5/16) | 0.0000 |
| $b_3$ | 0.0938 | 0.1250 (2/16) | $+0.0312$ |

**Step 3 — Evaluate ideal frequency response:**

$$H_d(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{k=0}^{3} b_k \, e^{-j\omega k}$$

At $\omega = 0.5\pi$:

$$e^{-j 0.5\pi} = -j, \quad e^{-j\pi} = -1, \quad e^{-j 1.5\pi} = j$$

$$H_d = 0.2031(1) + 0.5938(-j) + 0.3125(-1) + 0.0938(j)$$

$$H_d = (0.2031 - 0.3125) + j(-0.5938 + 0.0938)$$

$$H_d = -0.1094 - j\,0.5000$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate quantized frequency response:**

$$\hat{H} = 0.1875(1) + 0.5625(-j) + 0.3125(-1) + 0.1250(j)$$

$$\hat{H} = (0.1875 - 0.3125) + j(-0.5625 + 0.1250)$$

$$\hat{H} = -0.1250 - j\,0.4375$$

**Step 5 — Deviation:**

$$\Delta H = \hat{H} - H_d = (-0.1250 + 0.1094) + j(-0.4375 + 0.5000)$$

$$\boxed{\Delta H = -0.0156 + j\,0.0625}$$

$$\vert \Delta H \vert = \sqrt{0.0156^{2} + 0.0625^{2}} \approx 0.0644$$

This represents a **64.4% deviation** of the original response magnitude of ~0.512 — significant for only 4 bits. Increasing to **8 bits** would reduce this deviation by a factor of 16.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — FIR vs IIR Coefficient Quantization

| Parameter | FIR Filter | IIR Filter |
|---|---|---|
| **Affected elements** | Zeros only (no poles in finite-precision model) | Both poles AND zeros |
| **Stability risk** | **None** — FIR is always BIBO stable | **High** — poles may exit unit circle |
| **Sensitivity magnitude** | Generally low for moderate-order filters | **High** for narrowband / high-Q sections |
| **Preferred structure** | Direct form is acceptable | **Cascade of second-order sections** (preferred) |
| **Pairing strategy** | N/A | Pair poles closest to unit circle with zeros closest to those poles |
| **Ordering strategy** | N/A | Order sections to minimize overflow probability |
| **Worst-case deviation** | Magnitude response ripple | Pole excursion causing **complete filter failure** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked "Why is coefficient quantization more critical in IIR than FIR?", answer: *"Because IIR filters contain feedback (recursive) terms, a small change in a coefficient can move a pole outside the unit circle, making the filter unstable, whereas FIR filters have only feed-forward terms and remain stable regardless of coefficient precision."*
